summary: The etcd-operator incorrectly reports healthy etcd members as unhealthy due to API server throttling, causing false positive cluster degradation alerts.
description:
**Description of problem:**

```
ClusterOperator etcd: Reports "Degraded=True, Available=False"
Actual etcd status: All 3 members healthy, leader election working, data synchronized
jtaleric at draco in ~/code/tinker on main* $ oc exec -n openshift-etcd etcd-ip-10-0-64-226.us-west-2.compute.internal -c etcd -- sh -c 'time etcdctl endpoint health --write-out=table'; oc get co/etcd
+--------------------------+--------+------------+-------+
|         ENDPOINT         | HEALTH |    TOOK    | ERROR |
+--------------------------+--------+------------+-------+
| https://10.0.64.226:2379 |   true | 8.247882ms |       |
|   https://10.0.22.9:2379 |   true |  10.4248ms |       |
|   https://10.0.56.9:2379 |   true |  10.6166ms |       |
+--------------------------+--------+------------+-------+

real	0m0.023s
user	0m0.014s
sys	0m0.014s
NAME   VERSION                              AVAILABLE   PROGRESSING   DEGRADED   SINCE   MESSAGE
etcd   4.22.0-0.nightly-2026-03-23-022245   False       False         True       2d16h   EtcdMembersAvailable: 1 of 3 members are available, ip-10-0-22-9.us-west-2.compute.internal is unhealthy, ip-10-0-56-9.us-west-2.compute.internal is unhealthy
```

**Version-Release number of selected component (if applicable):**

```
4.22.0-0.nightly-2026-03-23-022245
```

**How reproducible:**

```
unknown.
```

**Steps to Reproduce:**

```
1. Running our udn-density job on a 24-node OpenShift Cluster, etcd-operator reports this at 475 UDNs
```

**Actual results:**

```
etcd   4.22.0-0.nightly-2026-03-23-022245   False       False         True       2d16h   EtcdMembersAvailable: 1 of 3 members are available, ip-10-0-22-9.us-west-2.compute.internal is unhealthy, ip-10-0-56-9.us-west-2.compute.internal is unhealthy
```

**Expected results:**

```
etcd to properly report status.
```

**Additional info:**

```
Operator health check timeout (30s) is insufficient under API server load, we are seeing : took=29.99591128s, err=health check failed: context deadline exceeded 

There is no distinction between network/API issues vs actual etcd problems?
```

summary: 4.20 - Extreme CPU Usage observed from Kubernetes components due to golang-fips TLS1.3 handling
description:
**Description of problem:**

```
Customer is seeing huge CPU usage on the kube-apiserver after upgrading to OCP 4.20.

Customer managed to get a pprof from an API server as this occurred 
and found that 60%+ of the time was being spent handling TLS handshakes with the following call tree:

runtime.cgocall                                    164.63s  61.05%
  crypto/tls.(*Conn).HandshakeContext              161.88s  60.03%
    crypto/tls.(*serverHandshakeStateTLS13).handshake  148.86s  55.20%
      crypto/tls.expandLabel                       154.47s  57.28%
        io.ReadAll                                 153.35s  56.86%
          golang-fips/openssl/v2.(*hkdf).Read
            _Cfunc_go_openssl_EVP_PKEY_derive_wrapper 

Customer noticed that compiling the API server using https://github.com/golang-fips/go and 
believes the root cause of the issues is due the expandLabel handling in there, specifically 
this looks to be very expensive: https://github.com/golang-fips/go/blob/f9990dae1ad17df49fe7ec70a707e764fc3c0004/patches/000-fips.patch#L4694-L4699 
when tracing the code due to the number of times expandLabel is called in a handshake and the 
implementation of that between the IO handling and the reader.

This started to happen in 4.20 and customer's assumption is because images were upgraded 
to be compiled with Go 1.24 which has a much different handling of expandLabel in 1.24 vs 1.23

This is causing huge impact on the stability of the cluster.

Customer also sees that this issue is reproducible when shutting down one kube-apiserver,
the same behavior starts on the other kube-apiservers.
```

**Version-Release number of selected component (if applicable):**

```
OpenShift Container Platfrom 4.20.5
With enabled FIPS
```

**How reproducible:**

```
n/a - at customer's site
```

**Steps to Reproduce:**

```
1. 
2. 
3. 
```

**Actual results:**

```
‌
```

**Expected results:**

```
‌
```

**Additional info:**

```
- the pprof data will be provided through the next comment.
```

summary: Clusters born in OpenShift 4.9 and earlier may have duplicate members upon upgrading to 4.21
description:
Clusters which ran 4.9 and earlier at any point may run into <custom data-type="smartlink" data-id="id-0">https://github.com/etcd-io/etcd/issues/20967</custom> 

Therefore we should

* Increase the minimum version required to upgrade to 4.21 to 4.20.15 where we brought in etcd-3.5.26
* Declare a risk on 4.21.z <= the version in which we implemented <custom data-type="emoji" data-id="id-1">:point_up:</custom> which removes update recommendations for clusters that have 4.9 or lower in their history

This bug can be marked verified once the first step is done, but we should not forget to follow up on the second step. Marking this as UpgradeBlocker

If there’s a workaround to remove the duplicate members after the upgrade to 4.21 it would be good to detail that.

summary: Kube-apiserver operator: KubeAPIServer status.nodeStatuses not cleared on Node delete (same-name replacement)
description:
## Description of problem

When a **Node** is **deleted** from the API and later **recreated with the same \`nodeName\`** (e.g. `master-0`), **\`kubeapiserver.operator.openshift.io/cluster\` \`status.nodeStatuses\` should lose the row for that name** (or be reset) so the replacement host is **not** treated as already at **\`currentRevision\`**.

Observed on live TNF cluster (2026-03-20): **\`installer-5-master-0\` completed \~18:45Z** on the **old** host, while the **current** Node `master-0` object was **created \~19:14Z** (new UID). **\`nodeStatuses\` still showed \`master-0\` at \`currentRevision: 5\`** with **no installer pod for \`master-0\` after the new Node existed**, while **\`MissingStaticPodControllerDegraded\`** / **\`GuardControllerDegraded\`** reflected **no kube-apiserver static pod** on the new host.

## Version-Release number

4.22

## How reproducible

Always (when same-name control-plane node replacement is performed)

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Deploy TNF (Two-Node with Fencing) cluster
2. Delete a control-plane Node from the API (e.g., `oc delete node master-0`)
3. Reprovision the node with the same name (`master-0`)
4. Observe `kubeapiserver.operator.openshift.io/cluster` `status.nodeStatuses`

## Actual results

* `status.nodeStatuses` retains entry for `master-0` with stale `currentRevision: 5`
* No installer pod created for replacement `master-0`
* `MissingStaticPodControllerDegraded` condition present
* `GuardControllerDegraded` condition present
* No kube-apiserver static pod on replacement host

## Expected results

* Deleting the old Node should prune or invalidate operator node-installer state for that name
* Revision materialization should run again on the replacement
* kube-apiserver static pod should start on replacement node

## Additional info

**Root cause:**

* `StaticPodOperatorStatus.nodeStatuses` uses `+listMapKey=nodeName` with no Node UID tracking
* Same-name replacement after full Node delete is not modeled as a transition that clears the list entry
* `currentRevision` can describe the previous host while the new kubelet never receives a fresh install pass

**Proposed fix:**

* Align with CEO / library-go fixes: on Node delete, remove or reset the `nodeStatuses` row for that nodeName
* OR key invalidation off Node `metadata.uid` changes for a stable name
* OR long-term key by UID
* Validate DualReplica / TNF same-name CP replace

**Workarounds:**

* TNF NR test (`origin` `tnf_node_replacement.go`): `removeKubeAPIServerClusterOperatorNodeStatusForDeletedNode` drops the `status.nodeStatuses` row
* Manual: Patch `kubeapiserver/cluster` status to drop/reset the `nodeStatuses` entry, then force rollout

**Related:** OCPBUGS-80957 (same `listMapKey=nodeName` pattern in CEO)

summary: CEO: Etcd status.nodeStatuses not cleared on Node delete (same-name replacement)
description:
## Description of problem

When a **Node** is **deleted** from the API and later **recreated with the same \`nodeName\`** (e.g. `master-0`), **\`etcd.operator.openshift.io/cluster\` \`status.nodeStatuses\` keeps the old row** keyed only by **\`nodeName\`**. CEO then treats the replacement host as **already at \`currentRevision\`**, so the **node installer / static etcd manifest path may not re-run**.

On-disk symptoms include:

* **missing \`/var/lib/etcd/certs.hash\`**
* **Pacemaker \`podman-etcd\` start failures**
* **\`MissingStaticPodControllerDegraded\`** for etcd on that node

Even though **\`nodeStatuses\` still shows both nodes at the latest revision**.

## Version-Release number

4.22

## How reproducible

Always (when same-name control-plane node replacement is performed)

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Deploy TNF (Two-Node with Fencing) cluster
2. Delete a control-plane Node from the API (e.g., `oc delete node master-0`)
3. Reprovision the node with the same name (`master-0`)
4. Observe `etcd.operator.openshift.io/cluster` `status.nodeStatuses`

## Actual results

* `status.nodeStatuses` retains the old entry for `master-0` with stale `currentRevision`
* Node installer does not run on the replacement node
* `/var/lib/etcd/certs.hash` missing on replacement node
* Pacemaker `podman-etcd` fails to start
* `MissingStaticPodControllerDegraded` condition present

## Expected results

* On Node delete, the matching `nodeStatuses` entry should be removed or invalidated
* Node installer should run on the replacement node
* etcd static pod should start successfully

## Additional info

**Root cause:**

* `StaticPodOperatorStatus.nodeStatuses` uses **\`+listMapKey=nodeName\`** with no Node UID tracking
* Same-name replacement is not modeled as a transition that clears stale state

**Proposed fix (one or more of):**

1. On Node delete, remove the matching `nodeStatuses` entry
2. On Node create/update with different UID for same name, invalidate installer state
3. Long-term: key installer progress by Node UID instead of just nodeName

**Workarounds:**

* Test/automation: `Etcds().UpdateStatus` with filtered `status.nodeStatuses` (see `origin` TNF NR test `tnf_node_replacement.go`)
* Manual recovery: Patch etcd status + CEO/Pacemaker recovery (`pcs resource cleanup etcd`)

**References:**

* TNF NR run `20260320-150540` live cluster debugging
* OpenShift API: `operator/v1` `NodeStatus` / `listMapKey=nodeName` (`api/operator/v1/types.go`)

summary: CVE-2026-33413 openshift4/ose-etcd-rhel9: etcd: Authorization bypass allows information disclosure and denial of service [openshift-4.17]
description:
Security Tracking Issue

Do not make this issue public.

Flaw:

---

etcd: Authorization bypass allows information disclosure and denial of service

etcd is a distributed key-value store for the data of a distributed system. Prior to versions 3.4.42, 3.5.28, and 3.6.9, unauthorized users may bypass authentication or authorization checks and call certain etcd functions in clusters that expose the gRPC API to untrusted or partially trusted clients. In unpatched etcd clusters with etcd auth enabled, unauthorized users are able to call MemberList and learn cluster topology, including member IDs and advertised endpoints; call Alarm, which can be abused for operational disruption or denial of service; use Lease APIs, interfering with TTL-based keys and lease ownership; and/or trigger compaction, permanently removing historical revisions and disrupting watch, audit, and recovery workflows. Kubernetes does not rely on etcd’s built-in authentication and authorization. Instead, the API server handles authentication and authorization itself, so typical Kubernetes deployments are not affected. Versions 3.4.42, 3.5.28, and 3.6.9 contain a patch. If upgrading is not immediately possible, reduce exposure by treating the affected RPCs as unauthenticated in practice. Restrict network access to etcd server ports so only trusted components can connect and/or require strong client identity at the transport layer, such as mTLS with tightly scoped client certificate distribution.

\~\~\~

The following link provides references to all essential vulnerability management information. If something is wrong or missing, please contact a member of PSIRT.  
[https://prodsec.pages.redhat.com/prodsec-docs/incident-response/Vulnerability_Management_Essentials/](https://prodsec.pages.redhat.com/prodsec-docs/incident-response/Vulnerability_Management_Essentials/)

Tracker accuracy feedback form: [https://docs.google.com/forms/d/e/1FAIpQLSfa6zTaEGohRdiIqGVAvWTSAL0kpO_DkkEICuIHzQHFwmKswg/viewform](https://docs.google.com/forms/d/e/1FAIpQLSfa6zTaEGohRdiIqGVAvWTSAL0kpO_DkkEICuIHzQHFwmKswg/viewform)

summary: CVE-2026-33186 openshift4/ose-hyperkube-rhel9: gRPC-Go: Authorization bypass due to improper HTTP/2 path validation [openshift-4.21]
description:
Security Tracking Issue

Do not make this issue public.

Flaw:

---

gRPC-Go: Authorization bypass due to improper HTTP/2 path validation

gRPC-Go is the Go language implementation of gRPC. Versions prior to 1.79.3 have an authorization bypass resulting from improper input validation of the HTTP/2 \`:path\` pseudo-header. The gRPC-Go server was too lenient in its routing logic, accepting requests where the \`:path\` omitted the mandatory leading slash (e.g., \`Service/Method\` instead of \`/Service/Method\`). While the server successfully routed these requests to the correct handler, authorization interceptors (including the official \`grpc/authz\` package) evaluated the raw, non-canonical path string. Consequently, "deny" rules defined using canonical paths (starting with \`/\`) failed to match the incoming request, allowing it to bypass the policy if a fallback "allow" rule was present. This affects gRPC-Go servers that use path-based authorization interceptors, such as the official RBAC implementation in \`google.golang.org/grpc/authz\` or custom interceptors relying on \`info.FullMethod\` or \`grpc.Method(ctx)\`; AND that have a security policy contains specific "deny" rules for canonical paths but allows other requests by default (a fallback "allow" rule). The vulnerability is exploitable by an attacker who can send raw HTTP/2 frames with malformed \`:path\` headers directly to the gRPC server. The fix in version 1.79.3 ensures that any request with a \`:path\` that does not start with a leading slash is immediately rejected with a \`codes.Unimplemented\` error, preventing it from reaching authorization interceptors or handlers with a non-canonical path string. While upgrading is the most secure and recommended path, users can mitigate the vulnerability using one of the following methods: Use a validating interceptor (recommended mitigation); infrastructure-level normalization; and/or policy hardening.

\~\~\~

The following link provides references to all essential vulnerability management information. If something is wrong or missing, please contact a member of PSIRT.  
[https://prodsec.pages.redhat.com/prodsec-docs/incident-response/Vulnerability_Management_Essentials/](https://prodsec.pages.redhat.com/prodsec-docs/incident-response/Vulnerability_Management_Essentials/)

Tracker accuracy feedback form: [https://docs.google.com/forms/d/e/1FAIpQLSfa6zTaEGohRdiIqGVAvWTSAL0kpO_DkkEICuIHzQHFwmKswg/viewform](https://docs.google.com/forms/d/e/1FAIpQLSfa6zTaEGohRdiIqGVAvWTSAL0kpO_DkkEICuIHzQHFwmKswg/viewform)

summary: Azure installation fail when v-net subnets are pre-configured.
description:
IPI OCP 4.18.x Installation in Azure fail with error:

```java
ERROR failed to fetch Cluster: failed to generate asset "Cluster": failed to create cluster: failed to create infrastructure manifest: admission webhook "validation.azurecluster.infrastructure.cluster.x-k8s.io" denied the request: AzureCluster.infrastructure.cluster.x-k8s.io "csaggin-repro-0419050-jzg58" is invalid: spec.networkSpec.apiServerLB.frontendIPConfigs[0].privateIP: Invalid value: "172.16.0.52": Internal LB IP address needs to be in control plane subnet range ([172.16.0.0/27])
```

The validation fails due to the wrong subnet association when the v-net are already existing.

It is very easy to reproduce:

1) Create the v-net subnet like:

```java
priv-endpoint: 172.16.0.32/29
controlplan:   172.16.0.48/28
workers:       172.16.0.0/27
```

2) Associate the subnet in the install-config.yaml:

```java
networking:
  clusterNetwork:
  - cidr: 10.128.0.0/14
    hostPrefix: 23
  machineNetwork:
  - cidr: 172.16.0.0/26
  networkType: OVNKubernetes
  serviceNetwork:
  - 172.30.0.0/16
platform:
  azure:
    baseDomainResourceGroupName: default_domain
    cloudName: AzurePublicCloud
    outboundType: Loadbalancer
    region: eastus
    computeSubnet: workers
    networkResourceGroupName: csaggin_ocp_sp_rg
    controlPlaneSubnet: controlplan
    virtualNetwork: csaggin-vnet
```

3) Create the manifest from the install-config.yaml and check the 02_azure-cluster.yaml created and see the wrong subnet association:

```java
frontendIPs:
      - name: csaggin-repro-0419050-hs6pc-internal-frontEnd
        privateIP: 172.16.0.52
.....
    subnets:
    - cidrBlocks:
      - 172.16.0.0/27
      name: controlplan
    - cidrBlocks:
      - 172.16.0.32/27
      name: workers
```

**Working 4.16:** [https://github.com/openshift/installer/blob/release-4.16/pkg/asset/manifests/azure/cluster.go](https://github.com/openshift/installer/blob/release-4.16/pkg/asset/manifests/azure/cluster.go)  
**Not Working 4.18:** [https://github.com/openshift/installer/blob/release-4.18/pkg/asset/manifests/azure/cluster.go](https://github.com/openshift/installer/blob/release-4.18/pkg/asset/manifests/azure/cluster.go)

As far I can see in this commit: [https://github.com/openshift/installer/commit/9cc3209613f7b04a43b9d0686a17a517e1a42af8](https://github.com/openshift/installer/commit/9cc3209613f7b04a43b9d0686a17a517e1a42af8) there has been an enhancement of how the automatic v-net subnet creation are handled forcing them to be split, however when subnets get previously configured it introduce also this bug/issue.

For the moment the workaround founds are:  
\-) Installing an older version and excute the upgrade.  
\-) Manually modify the 02_azure-cluster.yaml file to match the right configure subnets.

summary: [CTRLPLANE-1544-4.21] runAsUser/runAsGroup not behaving to the expected range values in user namespaces
description:
Description of problem:

```java
runAsUser/runAsGroup not behaving to the expected range values in user namespaces
```

Version-Release number of selected component (if applicable):

```java
4.21.0-0.nightly-2025-11-03-191704
```

How reproducible:

```java
Always
```

Steps to Reproduce:

```java
1. Install AWS/GCP/Azure cluster
2. Create newproject and do deployment as mentioned below.
3. Check the status of deployment

// Deployment Behavior with Different UID/GID Values
Parameter       Value   Deployment Status       Error Message / Notes

runAsUser       1000    ✅ Works        Minimum allowed value for restricted-v3 SCC
runAsUser       65534   ✅ Works        Maximum allowed value for restricted-v3 SCC
runAsUser       65535   ❌ Fails        Invalid value: 65535: must be in the ranges: [1000, 65534]
runAsUser       999     ❌ Fails        Invalid value: 999: must be in the ranges: [1000, 65534]

runAsGroup      1000    ✅ Works        Minimum allowed value for restricted-v3 SCC
runAsGroup      65535   ✅ Works        Maximum allowed value for restricted-v3 SCC
runAsGroup      65536   ❌ Fails        deployment does not have minimum availability.
runAsGroup      999     ✅ Works        No error message, as it is working successfully

// deployment.yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: deployment-invalid-user-test-65535
  namespace: testropatil
  annotations:
    openshift.io/required-scc: restricted-v3
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: testing
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: testing
      annotations:
        openshift.io/required-scc: restricted-v3
    spec:
      hostUsers: false
      securityContext:
        runAsUser: 65535  # This allows only till 65534 for user namespaces
        runAsGroup: 1000
        fsGroup: 1000
        runAsNonRoot: true
        seccompProfile:
          type: RuntimeDefault
      containers:
      - name: testing
        image: ubuntu
        command: ["/bin/sleep"]
        args: ["3600"]
        securityContext:
          allowPrivilegeEscalation: false
          capabilities:
            drop: ["ALL"]
---
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: deployment-valid-user-test-65534
  namespace: testropatil
  annotations:
    openshift.io/required-scc: restricted-v3
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: testing
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: testing
      annotations:
        openshift.io/required-scc: restricted-v3
    spec:
      hostUsers: false
      securityContext:
        runAsUser: 65534  # This allows only till 65534 for user namespaces
        runAsGroup: 1000
        fsGroup: 1000
        runAsNonRoot: true
        seccompProfile:

        command: ["/bin/sleep"]
        args: ["3600"]

summary: [openshift-kube-scheduler] Honor Centralized TLS Configuration for PQC-Safety
description:
Description of problem:

```java
The [openshift-kube-scheduler-ip] pod in the [openshift-kube-scheduler] component is using a local, hardcoded TLS configuration. This component's TLS Security Profile is expected to be managed by the API Server config custom resource. If you feel you should be using the Kubelet or Ingress configuration as the source-of-truth for TLS configuration, please leave a comment justifying your decision and rationale.

Hardcoding TLS configuration creates a security vulnerability because it does not adhere to our evolving, centrally-managed security policy for Post-Quantum Cryptography (PQC) readiness.

This ticket requires refactoring the component to dynamically inherit its TLS settings from the designated global configuration source, rather than managing them locally.

Business & Security Impact
This is a critical, long-term security initiative. Hardcoded configurations prevent agile responses to new threats and lead to policy drift. By not using the central policy, we are exposed to "store-now-decrypt-later" attacks, where adversaries capture today's data to decrypt with future quantum computers.
Centralizing this configuration is the only scalable way to ensure every service in our ecosystem remains secure against emerging cryptographic threats.An audit has confirmed this component is not using the central TLS policy.
```

Version-Release number of selected component (if applicable):

```java
4.20
```

How reproducible:

```java
100%
A scan of its endpoint shows a configuration that is out-of-sync with the current global standard.
Evidence Link:
```

[https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1nb7o0g3Ahn9GMTV-XvqraZY4Q7-1ZSYYlXH2EUpU4Dk/edit?gid=410280687#gid=410280687](https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1nb7o0g3Ahn9GMTV-XvqraZY4Q7-1ZSYYlXH2EUpU4Dk/edit?gid=410280687#gid=410280687) 

 

Steps to Reproduce:

```java
1. Deploy OCP
    2. Configure to "Modern Profile" the Control Plane, kubelet and Ingress
    3. Scan for non-TLS 1.3 reply from component (there should be none)
```

Actual results:

```java
TLS 1.2 responded
```

Expected results:

```java
TLS 1.3 only. If using a custom TLS 1.3 profile, the custom profile should be honored.
```

Additional info:

```java
The service/component must be refactored to dynamically pull and apply TLS policy from the official, centrally-managed sources, like the APIServer configuration, Kubelet configuration, or Ingress controller configuration (e.g., hardcoded protocols or cipher lists).

Hardcoding values like "TLS 1.3" is not an acceptable fix. The component must remain compliant as the central policy is updated over time without requiring a code change.

Central Configuration Source:
Documentation: https://docs.redhat.com/en/documentation/openshift_container_platform/4.19/html/security_and_compliance/tls-security-profiles#tls-profiles-kubernetes-configuring_tls-security-profiles 

Acceptance Criteria
[ ] All local or hardcoded TLS configurations (protoc

summary: TLS registry: refactor testcase annotations
description:
This is a clone of issue <custom data-type="smartlink" data-id="id-0">https://redhat.atlassian.net/browse/OCPBUGS-57049#icft=OCPBUGS-57049</custom>. The following is the description of the original issue:   
—  
Description of problem:

```java
Instead of using `https//github.com/link/to/pr/adding/annotation, \"quote escaped formatted name of e2e test that ensures the PKI artifact functions properly\"` for annotations we should break it down into:

certificates.openshift.io/test-name: \"quote escaped formatted name of e2e test that ensures the PKI artifact functions properly\"

and 

certificates.openshift.io/auto-regenerate-after-offline-expiry: https//github.com/link/to/pr/adding/annotation

as the test case will verify that the cert is refreshed and used for any type of test.

More metadata should be added here too - namely `certificates.openshift.io/refresh-period` which sets the expected period when certificate is being updated
```

summary: [4.20] oauth and openshift-apiserver related disruptions around operator upgrade
description:
```java
We have seen an oauth/openshift api disruption issue that is likely related to Kubernetes 1.33 bump for those repos. The pattern can be seen in this dashboard: https://grafana-loki.ci.openshift.org/d/ISnBj4LVk/disruption?orgId=1&var-percentile=P50&var-platform=azure&var-backend=oauth-api-new-connections&var-upgrade_type=minor&var-master_nodes_updated=Y&var-architectures=amd64&var-topologies=ha&var-networks=ovn&var-releases=4.21&var-releases=4.20&var-lookback=1&var-min_disruption_regression=-10&var-min_disruption_job_list=0&var-min_relevance=0&var-featureset=All&from=now-38d&to=now.

This is affecting both 4.20 and 4.21 minor upgrade jobs.

Here is a summary of my analysis so far:

* Here is an example job for 4.21: https://prow.ci.openshift.org/view/gs/origin-ci-test/logs/periodic-ci-openshift-release-master-ci-4.21-upgrade-from-stable-4.20-e2e-azure-ovn-upgrade/1961248707785527296
* The disruption seems to be happening while openshift-apiserver and authentication operators are being upgraded.
* The new signature of the connection error is “etcdserver: leader changed” with code 500.
* The new pattern seem to be showing up around those CI payloads: 4.21.0-0.ci-2025-08-29-020134 and 4.20.0-0.ci-2025-08-28-230356
* The change list for 4.20.0-0.ci-2025-08-28-230356 is very small. Kube 1.33 update for oauth-apiserver is right there. You can also see the same change among the long list of changes in 4.21.0-0.ci-2025-08-29-020134.
* There is a comment in that PR about payload test done in https://github.com/openshift/oauth-server/pull/197. So I checked an azure upgrade job and saw the same disruption pattern here.
```

summary: Missing endpoint slices for open ports the operator uses
description:
Description of problem:

```java
The communication matrix project aims to automatically generate an accurate and up-to-date communication flows matrix that can be delivered to customers as part of product documentation for all ingress flows of OpenShift (see documented communication matrix example[https://docs.openshift.com/container-platform/4.16/installing/install_config/configuring-firewall.html#network-flow-matrix_configuring-firewall]).
The communication matrix consists of the cluster's endpoint slices which are created automatically for every service on the cluster. Your operator includes some open ports with missing services, and by that there are missing endpoint slices. To solve this issue, please add service to the following ports: 17697 (master node, TCP protocol), 6080 (master node, TCP protocol).
```

Version-Release number of selected component (if applicable):

```java
‌
```

How reproducible:

```java
Compare between endpoint slices to open ports.
```

Steps to Reproduce:

```java
1. Get endpoint slices:
run the following command: `oc get endpointslices -n <operator's-namespace>`

2. Get open ports:
Make sure the `ss` command is available within your pod - if not use the following command to install iprune2 on your pod: `dnf install -y iproute`
In order to get ports using TCP protocol run from your node:
`ss -anpltH`
In order to get ports using UDP protocol run from your node:
`ss -anpluH`

3. Compare the ports between the outputs.
```

Actual results:

```java
The following ports are missing endpoint slices: 17697 (master node, TCP protocol), 6080 (master node, TCP protocol).
```

Expected results:

```java
Every open port will have an endpoint slice.
```

Additional info:

```java
In order to resolve this issue, service should be created for the following ports: 17697 (master node, TCP protocol), 6080 (master node, TCP protocol). The endpoint slices should be created automatically once the service is up.
```

summary: Do not allow the starting port number of serviceNodePortRange to be changed to a smaller one
description:
Description of problem:

```java
Do not allow the starting port number of serviceNodePortRange to be changed to a smaller one.

The current document [1] describes how to extend “serviceNodePortRange” as follows:

1. To expand the node port range, enter the following command. Replace <port> with the largest port number in the new range.
~~~
$ oc patch network.config.openshift.io cluster --type=merge -p \
  '{
    "spec":
      { "serviceNodePortRange": "30000-<port>" }
  }'
~~~
[1] https://docs.openshift.com/container-platform/4.15/networking/configuring-node-port-service-range.html

However, as of now, changing the starting port number of “serviceNodePortRange” to a smaller one is not an error.
At this point, however, the process of changing the starting port number(30000) in "serviceNodePortRange" to a smaller one does not fail.
```

Version-Release number of selected component (if applicable):

```java
4.15
```

How reproducible:

```java
eg) 
Change the "serviceNodePortRange" from "30000-32768" to "29000-32768".

oc patch network.config.openshift.io cluster --type=merge -p '{ "spec": { "serviceNodePortRange": "29000-32768" } }'
```

Actual results:

```java
no error

$ oc patch network.config.openshift.io cluster --type=merge -p '{ "spec": { "serviceNodePortRange": "29000-32768" } }'
network.config.openshift.io/cluster patched

$ oc get network -o yaml cluster
apiVersion: config.openshift.io/v1
kind: Network
...
spec:
  ...
  serviceNodePortRange: 29000-32768
```

Expected results:

```java
should terminate with an error
```

Additional info:

```java
I believe that the code should be changed to strengthen error checking to avoid unnecessary problems.
Alternatively, I believe that the documentation should be modified to clearly state that the starting port number cannot be changed, although this will not result in an error.
```

Version-Release number of selected component (if applicable):

```

How reproducible:

```java
100%
```

Steps to Reproduce:

```java
1. install OCP 4.20.14 cluster
    2. remove the auto-created `50-worker-auto-sizing-disabled` & `50-master-auto-sizing-disabled` MachineConfigs
    3. wait 5 minutes and they will be automatically recreated
```

Actual results:

```java
auto-sizing-disabled MachineConfigs keep getting recreated
```

Expected results:

```java
auto-sizing-disabled MachineConfigs should stay removed when deleted
```

Additional info:

```java
Automatically calculate and apply CPU and memory resources for system components

	OpenShift Container Platform now automatically calculates and 
reserves a portion of the CPU and memory resources for use by the 
underlying node and system components. Previously, you needed to enable 
the feature by creating a KubeletConfig custom resource (CR) with the autoSizingReserved: true parameter. For clusters updated to OpenShift Container Platform 4.21, you can enable the feature by deleting the 50-worker-auto-sizing-disabled
 machine config. After you delete the machine config, the nodes reboot 
with the new resource settings. If you manually configured system 
reserved CPU or memory resources, these settings remain upon update and 
do not change.
```

Reference: [https://docs.redhat.com/en/documentation/openshift_container_platform/4.21/html/release_notes/ocp-4-21-release-notes#ocp-release-notes-nodes_release-notes](https://docs.redhat.com/en/documentation/openshift_container_platform/4.21/html/release_notes/ocp-4-21-release-notes#ocp-release-notes-nodes_release-notes)

NOTE: this feature is supposedly only available in 4.21 yet the MachineConfigs are there in 4.20 also which does not seem correct as it's not documented in 4.20 release notes.

PROBLEM: "you can enable the feature by deleting the 50-worker-auto-sizing-disabled machine config"

**Please move to correct component if CVO is not relevant.** Thanks!

summary: ingress-canary DaemonSet no longer honors IngressController tolerations in compact clusters after upgrade to OCP 4.20
description:
Description of problem:

After upgrading from OpenShift 4.19.22 to 4.20.12, the ingress cluster operator reports Degraded=True due to CanaryChecksSucceeding=False. This occurs because the ingress-canary DaemonSet has desired=0 and does not schedule any pods.

The affected environment is a supported compact cluster topology where all nodes are control-plane and unschedulable, with infra labels and taints as documented in KCS 7109585.

In previous versions (4.17–4.19), the DaemonSet correctly inherited tolerations defined in the IngressController.spec.nodePlacement.tolerations section (e.g., { operator: Exists }), allowing pods to run on tainted infra/master nodes.

In 4.20.12, the ingress-canary DaemonSet appears to use only a fixed toleration (key: node-role.kubernetes.io/infra) and no longer respects custom tolerations defined by the user, resulting in no schedulable nodes and ingress operator degradation.

This breaks compatibility with previously working and supported cluster architectures.

Version-Release number of selected component (if applicable):  
4.20.12

How reproducible:

The issue consistently occurs after upgrading from OpenShift 4.19.x to 4.20.12 in compact clusters where all nodes are control-plane (master), unschedulable, and tainted, with the IngressController configured to use custom tolerations (e.g., operator: Exists).

Steps to Reproduce:

1\. Deploy an OpenShift 4.19.x cluster using a compact topology:

* All nodes are control-plane (master), tainted as unschedulable.
* Nodes are labeled as infra: node-role.kubernetes.io/infra=""
* IngressController is configured with tolerations:  
       spec:  
         nodePlacement:  
           tolerations:
* operator: Exists

2\. Confirm that the ingress-canary DaemonSet schedules pods and ingress operator is healthy.

3\. Upgrade the cluster to OpenShift 4.20.12.

4\. Observe that:

* The ingress cluster operator becomes Degraded with \`CanaryChecksSucceeding=False\`.
* The ingress-canary DaemonSet shows desired/current pods = 0.
* The toleration from IngressController is no longer respected.
* No pods are scheduled in \`openshift-ingress-canary\` namespace.

Actual results:

After upgrading to OpenShift 4.20.12, the ingress-canary DaemonSet no longer schedules any pods, and its desired/current count is 0. The ingress cluster operator reports Degraded=True with CanaryChecksSucceeding=False. The custom tolerations defined in the IngressController are ignored, and only a fixed toleration (key=node-role.kubernetes.io/infra) is present in the DaemonSet spec. This causes the canary route health checks to fail, despite the cluster functioning correctly otherwise.

Expected results:

The ingress-canary DaemonSet should inherit and apply the tolerations defined in the IngressController's spec.nodePlacement.tolerations. In a compact cluster with properly tainted infra/control-plane nodes, the DaemonSet should schedule pods accordingly. As in previous OpenShift versions (4.17–4.19), this wou

summary: [release-4.18] Route spec.path that have space or # causes HaProxy error and ingress degraded
description:
This is a clone of issue OCPBUGS-47773. The following is the description of the original issue:   
—  
Description of problem:

```java
A route with the following spec.path with a space (/route-admission-test1 / test1) or # (/route-admission-test!#) and  (annotations:     haproxy.router.openshift.io/rewrite-target: /)
can broke haproxy and cause ingress degraded.

~~~
kind: Route
apiVersion: route.openshift.io/v1
metadata:
  name: hello-openshift
  namespace: route-admission-test1
  annotations:
    haproxy.router.openshift.io/rewrite-target: /
spec:
  host: example-route-admission-test1.apps.xxxxx.japaneast.aroapp.io
  path: /route-admission-test1 / test1
  to:
    kind: Service
    name: hello-openshift
    weight: 100
  tls:
    termination: edge
    insecureEdgeTerminationPolicy: Allow
  wildcardPolicy: None
~~~
```

Version-Release number of selected component (if applicable):

```java
4.14 (other version could also have same issue)
```

How reproducible:

```java
Create a route with the haproxy.router.openshift.io/rewrite-target annotation and which spec.path have space like
 /route-admission-test1 / test1 
or with #
 /route-admission#
```

Steps to Reproduce:

```java
1.oc new-project route-admission-test1     

2. oc create -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/openshift/origin/master/examples/hello-openshift/hello-pod.json     

3.oc expose pod/hello-openshift

4. Create route like below 

kind: Route
apiVersion: route.openshift.io/v1
metadata:
  name: hello-openshift
  namespace: route-admission-test1
  annotations:
    haproxy.router.openshift.io/rewrite-target: /
spec:
  host: example-route-admission-test1.apps.xxxxx.japaneast.aroapp.io
  path: /route-admission-test1 / test1
  to:
    kind: Service
    name: hello-openshift
    weight: 100
  tls:
    termination: edge
    insecureEdgeTerminationPolicy: Allow
  wildcardPolicy: None



5. delete ingress pods and then check ingress status



$ oc get pods -n openshift-ingress

6. check logs

summary: Can not customize resource requests on ovnkube-control-plane pods on hosted control plane
description:
This is a clone of issue OCPBUGS-66161. The following is the description of the original issue:   
—  
Description of problem:

```java
The ovnkube-control-plane pods that run on a hosted control plane overwrite the cpu and memory resource requests if they are ever changed, so changing them to improve control plane performance does not work.  Any customizations to these deployment's resource requests are overwritten by the cluster-network-operator.This commit changes that so customizations/changes are left in place, to match the behavior of the multus-admission-controller.  For reference, the PR that implemented this for multus-admission-controller is https://github.com/openshift/cluster-network-operator/pull/2335/
```

Version-Release number of selected component (if applicable):

```java
All
```

How reproducible:

```java
Easy to reproduce
```

Steps to Reproduce:

```java
1. Deploy hosted control plane cluster
    2. Modify the cpu or memory request on the ovnkube-control-plane deployment
    3. Watch the operator eventually change it back
```

Actual results:

```java
Operator changes the resource requests back to the defaults
```

Expected results:

```java
modified cpu or memory requests stay as is and are not reverted to the defaults
```

summary: [kube-rbac-proxy / network operator] Honor Centralized TLS Configuration for PQC-Safety
description:
Description of problem:

```java
The [kube-rbac-proxy] in the [network-operator] pod in the [openshift-network-operator] component is using a local, hardcoded TLS configuration. This component's TLS Security Profile is expected to be managed by the API Server config custom resource. If you feel you should be using the Kubelet or Ingress configuration as the source-of-truth for TLS configuration, please leave a comment justifying your decision and rationale.

Hardcoding TLS configuration creates a security vulnerability because it does not adhere to our evolving, centrally-managed security policy for Post-Quantum Cryptography (PQC) readiness.

This ticket requires refactoring the component to dynamically inherit its TLS settings from the designated global configuration source, rather than managing them locally.

Business & Security Impact
This is a critical, long-term security initiative. Hardcoded configurations prevent agile responses to new threats and lead to policy drift. By not using the central policy, we are exposed to "store-now-decrypt-later" attacks, where adversaries capture today's data to decrypt with future quantum computers.
Centralizing this configuration is the only scalable way to ensure every service in our ecosystem remains secure against emerging cryptographic threats.An audit has confirmed this component is not using the central TLS policy.
```

Version-Release number of selected component (if applicable):

```java
4.20
```

How reproducible:

```java
100%
A scan of its endpoint shows a configuration that is out-of-sync with the current global standard.
Evidence Link:
```

[https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1nb7o0g3Ahn9GMTV-XvqraZY4Q7-1ZSYYlXH2EUpU4Dk/edit?gid=410280687#gid=410280687](https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1nb7o0g3Ahn9GMTV-XvqraZY4Q7-1ZSYYlXH2EUpU4Dk/edit?gid=410280687#gid=410280687) 

 

Steps to Reproduce:

```java
1. Deploy OCP
    2. Configure to "Modern Profile" the Control Plane, kubelet and Ingress
    3. Scan for non-TLS 1.3 reply from component (there should be none)
```

Actual results:

```java
TLS 1.2 responded
```

Expected results:

```java
TLS 1.3 only. If using a custom TLS 1.3 profile, the custom profile should be honored.
```

Additional info:

```java
The service/component must be refactored to dynamically pull and apply TLS policy from the official, centrally-managed sources, like the APIServer configuration, Kubelet configuration, or Ingress controller configuration (e.g., hardcoded protocols or cipher lists).

Hardcoding values like "TLS 1.3" is not an acceptable fix. The component must remain compliant as the central policy is updated over time without requiring a code change.

Central Configuration Source:
Documentation: https://docs.redhat.com/en/documentation/openshift_container_platform/4.19/html/security_and_compliance/tls-security-profiles#tls-profiles-kubernetes-configuring_tls-security-profiles 

Acceptance Criteria
[ ] All local or hardcoded TLS config

summary: [openshift-router] Honor Centralized TLS Configuration for PQC-Safety
description:
Description of problem:

```java
The [openshift-route] in the [router-default] pod in the [openshift-ingress] component is using a local, hardcoded TLS configuration. This component's TLS Security Profile is expected to be managed by the API Server config custom resource. If you feel you should be using the Kubelet or Ingress configuration as the source-of-truth for TLS configuration, please leave a comment justifying your decision and rationale.

Hardcoding TLS configuration creates a security vulnerability because it does not adhere to our evolving, centrally-managed security policy for Post-Quantum Cryptography (PQC) readiness.

This ticket requires refactoring the component to dynamically inherit its TLS settings from the designated global configuration source, rather than managing them locally.

Business & Security Impact
This is a critical, long-term security initiative. Hardcoded configurations prevent agile responses to new threats and lead to policy drift. By not using the central policy, we are exposed to "store-now-decrypt-later" attacks, where adversaries capture today's data to decrypt with future quantum computers.
Centralizing this configuration is the only scalable way to ensure every service in our ecosystem remains secure against emerging cryptographic threats.An audit has confirmed this component is not using the central TLS policy.
```

Version-Release number of selected component (if applicable):

```java
4.20
```

How reproducible:

```java
100%
A scan of its endpoint shows a configuration that is out-of-sync with the current global standard.
Evidence Link:
```

[https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1nb7o0g3Ahn9GMTV-XvqraZY4Q7-1ZSYYlXH2EUpU4Dk/edit?gid=410280687#gid=410280687](https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1nb7o0g3Ahn9GMTV-XvqraZY4Q7-1ZSYYlXH2EUpU4Dk/edit?gid=410280687#gid=410280687) 

 

Steps to Reproduce:

```java
1. Deploy OCP
    2. Configure to "Modern Profile" the Control Plane, kubelet and Ingress
    3. Scan for non-TLS 1.3 reply from component (there should be none)
```

Actual results:

```java
TLS 1.2 responded
```

Expected results:

```java
TLS 1.3 only. If using a custom TLS 1.3 profile, the custom profile should be honored.
```

Additional info:

```java
The service/component must be refactored to dynamically pull and apply TLS policy from the official, centrally-managed sources, like the APIServer configuration, Kubelet configuration, or Ingress controller configuration (e.g., hardcoded protocols or cipher lists).

Hardcoding values like "TLS 1.3" is not an acceptable fix. The component must remain compliant as the central policy is updated over time without requiring a code change.

Central Configuration Source:
Documentation: https://docs.redhat.com/en/documentation/openshift_container_platform/4.19/html/security_and_compliance/tls-security-profiles#tls-profiles-kubernetes-configuring_tls-security-profiles 

Acceptance Criteria
[ ] All local or hardcoded TLS configurations (p

summary: CI fails on testGatewayAPIIstioInstallation because Istiod has too many pods
description:
## Description of problem

CI can fail because of test failures such as the following:

```
gateway_api_test.go:158: failed to find expected Istiod control plane: too many pods for deployment openshift-ingress/istiod-openshift-gateway: 2
```

This failure comes from [https://prow.ci.openshift.org/view/gs/test-platform-results/pr-logs/pull/openshift_cluster-ingress-operator/1245/pull-ci-openshift-cluster-ingress-operator-master-e2e-aws-operator/1949881963301048320](https://prow.ci.openshift.org/view/gs/test-platform-results/pr-logs/pull/openshift_cluster-ingress-operator/1245/pull-ci-openshift-cluster-ingress-operator-master-e2e-aws-operator/1949881963301048320).

## Version-Release number of selected component (if applicable)

I have seen this in 4.20.

## How reproducible

I have only seen it happen once.

## Steps to Reproduce

1\. Post a PR and have bad luck.

## Actual results

CI fails.

## Expected results

CI passes, or fails on some other test failure.

## Additional info

The failure occurred because HPA scaled istiod out temporarily to 2 replicas.  I found the following event in the must-gather archive for the referenced CI run:

```
apiVersion: v1
count: 1
eventTime: null
firstTimestamp: "2025-07-28T19:10:42Z"
involvedObject:
  apiVersion: autoscaling/v2
  kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
  name: istiod-openshift-gateway
  namespace: openshift-ingress
  resourceVersion: "86760"
  uid: 112b10f4-bad3-433f-9bb0-f0c1ca333e06
kind: Event
lastTimestamp: "2025-07-28T19:10:42Z"
message: 'New size: 2; reason: cpu resource utilization (percentage of request)
  above target'
metadata:
  creationTimestamp: "2025-07-28T19:10:42Z"
  managedFields:
  # ...
  name: istiod-openshift-gateway.18567fffeaf8b275
  namespace: openshift-ingress
  resourceVersion: "86914"
  uid: e3f5d32f-9370-46be-ae56-934293cf68f7
reason: SuccessfulRescale
reportingComponent: horizontal-pod-autoscaler
reportingInstance: ""
source:
  component: horizontal-pod-autoscaler
type: Normal
```

We can consider turning off HPA, but it isn't clear why the test expects the number of pod replicas to be exactly 1.

summary: OVN-K8S expected NAD when Multus disabled in HyperShift
description:
**Description of problem:**  
When `disableMultus` is enabled in a HyperShift cluster, Multus is correctly not deployed by the CNO, but `ovnkube-node` still expects NAD. no CNI config is written, and the network remains in a `NetworkNotReady` state.

**Version-Release number of selected component (if applicable):**  
Tested on OpenShift 4.20 nightly

**How reproducible:**  
Always, when `disableMultus` is enabled in a HyperShift deployment using OVN-Kubernetes. 

**Steps to Reproduce:**

1. Create a guest cluster with `--disable-multus` set using the implementation here - [https://github.com/openshift/hypershift/pull/6358](https://github.com/openshift/hypershift/pull/6358)

1. Wait for hc to start and create a worker. check the state of `ovnk in HC`.

1. Observe the logs

**Actual results:**

* `ovnk pod` crashes repeatedly.

* `/etc/kubernetes/cni/net.d/` is empty — no CNI config is written.

* Error in logs: failed to list \*v1.NetworkAttachmentDefinition: the server could not find the requested resource (get network-attachment-definitions.k8s.cni.cncf.io)

**Expected results:**

`ovnk` should not expect the NAD CRD when Multus is disabled (I believe).

summary: Route spec.path that have space or # causes HaProxy error and ingress degraded
description:
Description of problem:

```java
A route with the following spec.path with a space (/route-admission-test1 / test1) or # (/route-admission-test!#) and  (annotations:     haproxy.router.openshift.io/rewrite-target: /)
can broke haproxy and cause ingress degraded.

~~~
kind: Route
apiVersion: route.openshift.io/v1
metadata:
  name: hello-openshift
  namespace: route-admission-test1
  annotations:
    haproxy.router.openshift.io/rewrite-target: /
spec:
  host: example-route-admission-test1.apps.xxxxx.japaneast.aroapp.io
  path: /route-admission-test1 / test1
  to:
    kind: Service
    name: hello-openshift
    weight: 100
  tls:
    termination: edge
    insecureEdgeTerminationPolicy: Allow
  wildcardPolicy: None
~~~
```

Version-Release number of selected component (if applicable):

```java
4.14 (other version could also have same issue)
```

How reproducible:

```java
Create a route with the haproxy.router.openshift.io/rewrite-target annotation and which spec.path have space like
 /route-admission-test1 / test1 
or with #

4. Create route like below 

kind: Route
apiVersion: route.openshift.io/v1
metadata:

summary: No topology.cinder.csi.openstack.org/zone labels added to nodes on OpenShift 4.19
description:
**Description of problem:**

When creating new cluster on OpenStack running OpenShift 4.19 and trying to create a PVC this is not able to find a PV:

```yaml
    message: '0/6 nodes are available: 3 node(s) didn''t find available persistent
      volumes to bind, 3 node(s) had untolerated taint {node-role.kubernetes.io/master:
      }. preemption: 0/6 nodes are available: 6 Preemption is not helpful for scheduling.'
    reason: Unschedulable
    status: "False"
    type: PodScheduled 
```

 This happens only on new clusters. Upgraded clusters from 4.18 to 4.19 are fine. 

 The following storage class is available in the cluster:

```shell
oc get storageclass block-silver -o yaml
```

```yaml
allowVolumeExpansion: true
allowedTopologies:
- matchLabelExpressions:
  - key: topology.cinder.csi.openstack.org/zone
    values:
    - nova
    - nova-az2
    - nova-az3
apiVersion: storage.k8s.io/v1
kind: StorageClass
metadata:
  annotations:
    storageclass.kubernetes.io/is-default-class: "true"
  creationTimestamp: "2026-03-03T09:27:10Z"
  name: block-silver
  resourceVersion: "527"
  uid: 5043a22d-f6df-4db9-ab7a-8dfd4f6c8016
parameters:
  availability: nova
  type: ceph-silver
provisioner: cinder.csi.openstack.org
reclaimPolicy: Delete
volumeBindingMode: WaitForFirstConsumer
```

Due to the labels missing on the node this causes the problems.

From initial investigation it's the CSI drivers responsibility to add the labels to the nodes as given here:

<custom data-type="smartlink" data-id="id-0">https://that.guru/blog/availability-zones-in-openstack-and-openshift-part-2/</custom> 

But this seems to have changed in 4.19

**Version-Release number of selected component (if applicable):**

```
OpenShift 4.19
```

**How reproducible:**

```
Always
```

**Steps to Reproduce:**

```
apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolumeClaim
metadata:
  name: ubuntu-pvc
  namespace: default
spec:
  accessModes:
    - ReadWriteOnce
  resources:
    requests:
      storage: 5G
  storageClassName: block-silver
---
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: ubuntu
  namespace: default
spec:
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: ubuntu
  replicas: 1
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: ubuntu
    spec:
      securityContext:
        fsGroup: 1000
      containers:
        - name: ubuntu
          image: docker.io/ubuntu:latest
          tty: true
          stdin: true
          volumeMounts:
          - name: ubuntu-home
            mountPath: /mnt/storage
      volumes:
        - name: ubuntu-home
          persistentVolumeClaim:
            claimName: ubuntu-pvc
```

I created it in cluster in version 4.18 and everything was ok. Now I'm going to run it in cluster in version 4.19 but the created resources stays in 'pending' status and pv is not created for pod.

```
oc describe pvc ubuntu-pvc
```

```
Name:          ubuntu-pvc
Namespace:     default
StorageClass:  block-silver
Status:        Pending
Volume:
Labels:        <none>
Annotations:   <none

summary: kueue.x-k8s.io/queue-name referring to non-existent queue can cause pod to become permanently stuck
description:
### Description of problem

A notebook was labeled with kueue.x-k8s.io/queue-name: invalid, which points to a LocalQueue that does not exist. The sequence was:

1\. Notebook created and pod started running normally (images pulled, containers started successfully)  
2\. Kueue labels applied to notebook/statefulset with queue invalid  
3\. The running pod was killed (events show Killing for both containers)  
4\. A new pod was created with two scheduling gates: kueue.x-k8s.io/admission and kueue.x-k8s.io/topology  
5\. Kueue created a Workload object but rejected it as Inadmissible: "LocalQueue invalid doesn't exist"  
6\. The scheduling gates will never be removed — the pod is permanently stuck

### Prerequisites (if any, like setup, operators/versions)

RHOAI 3.3  
RHBoK v1.2

### Steps to Reproduce

1. Create DSP
2. Add Workbenches resources to DSP
3. Label namespace containing workbenches with kueue.openshift.io/managed=true
4. Label a workbench with kueue.x-k8s.io/queue-name that points to a non-existent LocalQueue

### Actual results

* The running pod was killed (events show Killing for both containers)
* A new pod was created with two scheduling gates: kueue.x-k8s.io/admission and kueue.x-k8s.io/topology
* Kueue created a Workload object but rejected it as Inadmissible: "LocalQueue invalid doesn't exist"
* The scheduling gates will never be removed — the pod is permanently stuck

Key detail: The workload condition explicitly states:

message: LocalQueue invalid doesn't exist  
reason: Inadmissible  
status: "False"  
type: QuotaReserved

### Expected results

Invalid annotations should be rejected outright and not allow the system to get into a state where a previously running workload is now unable to run.

### Reproducibility (Always/Intermittent/Only Once)

always

### Found in what build

RHOAI 3.3  
RHBoK v1.2

### Describe any workarounds

possibly delete annotation and/or apply valid one - need to confirm

### Additional information

* See [https://docs.google.com/document/d/1ToVijR1jWk9gC007Vsh5g9anMvrlG37t7ybF94JxcNE/edit?tab=t.btks76hijizf](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1ToVijR1jWk9gC007Vsh5g9anMvrlG37t7ybF94JxcNE/edit?tab=t.btks76hijizf) for more information on how to reproduce the bug as well as information about debugging the issue.

The temporary folders are not removed leading the node running the `operator-controller-controller-manager` to be under disk pressure once number of temporary folder starts to be too much.

## Expected results:

The temporary folders from previous attempts should be removed.

OR  
Some quota should be use in order to prevent the space usage to fill up the disk.

summary: Make bootMACAddress optional for virtual media BMC drivers when inspection is enabled
description:
**Description of problem:**

```
  Virtual media BMC drivers (redfish-virtualmedia, idrac-virtualmedia, ilo5-virtualmedia) can boot from virtual media and discover the host's MAC address during hardware
  inspection. Currently, bootMACAddress is required on BareMetalHost resources regardless of the BMC driver type, which forces users to manually determine and provide the boot MAC
  address before creating the BMH.

  This change makes bootMACAddress optional for virtual media drivers when hardware inspection is enabled, since the MAC address can be discovered automatically during the
  inspection process. bootMACAddress remains required when:
  - The BMC driver uses PXE boot (e.g., ipmi, libvirt), which needs the MAC for DHCP/TFTP
  - Inspection is disabled (via InspectionMode field or inspect.metal3.io annotation), since there is no other mechanism to discover the MAC

  Changes include:
  - Update virtual media driver NeedsMAC() methods to return false
  - Add webhook validation to require bootMACAddress for virtual media only when inspection is disabled
  - Skip MAC-based port queries in the Ironic provisioner when bootMACAddress is empty to prevent false MAC conflict errors

  This is used by the O-Cloud Manager (oran-o2ims) to support deferred boot MAC population, where the MAC address is resolved from interface labels on the BMH after inspection
  completes, removing the requirement for users to pre-populate bootMACAddress at BMH creation time.
```

summary: Control Plane Operator fails to reconcile on vanilla Kubernetes on IBMCloud due to missing Route API
description:
**Description of problem:**

```
On IBM Cloud the `servicePublishingStrategy: Route` can't assume that 
the management cluster is an OCP cluster, because it still has
vanilla k8s management clusters.
When running on vanilla Kubernetes on IBM Cloud 
(which doesn't have Route CRDs), the operator crashes during reconciliation 
when attempting to list Route resources.

HyperShift on IBM Cloud should work on both OpenShift and vanilla Kubernetes (IBM Cloud) 
management clusters, gracefully handling the absence of optional APIs like 
Route.
```

**Version-Release number of selected component (if applicable):**

```
4.19, 4.20, 4.21, 4.22
```

**How reproducible:**

```
Always on IBM Cloud
```

**Steps to Reproduce:**

```
1. Create a management cluster without Routes capability
2. Deploy a hosted cluster
```

**Actual results:**

```
Cluster can't get up, CPO can't reconcile.
```

**Expected results:**

```
Cluster is healthy and CPO is working
```

**Additional info:**

```
This was the commit that caused this problem to appear: 
https://github.com/openshift/hypershift/commit/191a2e24eeaad2d21eb8a130468c1db8d7be4fd3#diff-3d4ffda0834e09a59a4d510523932e20b9234bcb9816e40258a0fb32a87a7006R1104
```

summary: Upgrading with NADs using same network name causes VMs using a ovn-k8s-cni-overlay localnet NAD to lose connectivity due to a missing logical port on some of the VMs
description:
Description of problem:

While upgrading the cluster to 4.18 from 4.17, after the cluster network operator finishes upgrading to the 4.18 image, Some VMs begin to lose network connectivity through their ovn-k8s-cni-overlay localnet NADs, all the NADs are sharing the network name, some VMs do not lose connectivity.

Restarting the ovnkube-node pod seems to resolve the issue, as does performing a VM live migration for the impacted VM. No OVN DB rebuild was tested as restarting the ovnkube-node pod works.

 Running   
ovn-nbctl list logical-switch-port  
shows that the impacted VM does not have the logical switch port for connectivity.

Version-Release number of selected component (if applicable):

4.18.z

Currently has been seen on 4.18.34.

 

How reproducible:

Currently unsure, has proved difficult so far but I am currently working on a potential reproduction.

 

Steps to Reproduce:

1\. Create a 4.17.z cluster

2\. Install and configure OpenShift Virt

3\. Configure a ovn-k8s-cni-overlay localnet NAD

4\. Create multiple VMs using diffenert localnet ipamless NADs with same network name configured in step 3

6\. Upgrade to 4.18.z

 

Actual results:

After the cluster network operator upgrades, connectivity is lost to some VMs over their localnet NADs.

 

Expected results:

During and after upgrade connectivity remains for VMs over their localnet NADs.

Additional info:

Details on specific testing will be commented.

 

Affected Platforms:

OpenShift Container Platform 4.18

summary: [release-4.18] Etcd Operator should attempt to scale down replicas in the same AZ first
description:
This is a clone of issue OCPBUGS-78047. The following is the description of the original issue:   
—  
This is a clone of issue OCPBUGS-77921. The following is the description of the original issue:   
—  
Description of problem:

```java
Etcd operator currently appears to pick at random which member to remove (I haven't found the code) when machines are going away, and this can lead to a scenario where you end up with 2 control plane nodes, and not three.

To reproduce:
* Create a cluster (I used AWS)
* Ensure there is a ControlPlaneMachineSet, that it's spec.state is Active and that the strategy is RollingUpdate (this is the default)
* Use oc to delete ALL control plane Machines simultaneously
* CPMS will create a new machine for index 0 only
* Etcd operator will see the new instance, add etcd, join it as a learner, eventually swap it with one of the existing members
  * This swap appears to be random, it would be preferable to swap onto the same AZ/index where this is duplication
* Etcd removes the lifecycle hook, MAPI removes the instance
* In the case that the index was not 0 for the etcd chosen, CPMS continues to not bring up a new node, we now have [0, 0, 2]
* Etcd operator then decided to remove the lifecycle hook from the origin index 0 machine
* The cluster went down to 2 control plane machines
* The cluster eventually recovered

I would not expect it to allow the cluster to go down to 2 control plane machines, but it also should be picking index 0 to replace, since it's duplicated
```

```java
‌
```

```java
‌

1.
    2.
    3.

summary: False positive for scheduler degradation in Console Control Plane Status caused by user workloads named "scheduler"
description:
**Description of problem:**

False positives can be triggered for degradation of the scheduler component, in the Overview > Control Plane Status section of the OpenShift console, when user workloads containing the label job=”scheduler”

The response rate for the scheduler is calculsted with the following PromQL query:  

```
(sum(up{job="scheduler"} == 1) / count(up{job="scheduler"})) * 100  
```

Please see <custom data-type="smartlink" data-id="id-0">https://access.redhat.com/solutions/5232641</custom>   
  
The query lacks any further constraints which leaves it open to false positives, from other workloads with this label.

**Version-Release number of selected component (if applicable):**

```
Reported and reproduced on 4.18.z
```

**How reproducible:**

```
Always
```

**Steps to Reproduce:**

1. Ensure user workload monitoring is enabled.
2. Create a namespace and a test workload in this namespace. the test workload is not instrumented for Prometheus metrics

```shell
 oc create namespace fake-scheduler-test
 oc run fake-scheduler-pod --image=nginx:latest --port=80 -n fake-scheduler-test
```

2. Create a yaml file (for example test.yaml) containing the following content:

```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: scheduler
  namespace: fake-scheduler-test
  labels:
    app: fake-scheduler
spec:
  ports:
  - name: web
    port: 8080
    targetPort: 80
  selector:
    run: fake-scheduler-pod
---
apiVersion: monitoring.coreos.com/v1
kind: ServiceMonitor
metadata:
  name: fake-scheduler-monitor
  namespace: fake-scheduler-test
spec:
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: fake-scheduler
  endpoints:
  - port: web
    interval: 30s
    path: /metrics
```

3. Apply the yaml file

```shell
oc apply -f test.yaml
```

**Actual results:**

1. Overview > Status > Control Plane > Schedulers  no longer reports a 100% response rate and is reports the scheduler as degraded

2. Delete the test namespace and the response rate returns to 100%

```
‌
```

**Expected results:**

1. The control plane should not show as degraded, based on user-workloads.

```
‌
```

**Additional info:**

This could be easily be overcome by adding a constraint such as namespace selector the query:  
  
namespace=”openshift-kube-scheduler”

```
‌
```

```
‌
```

summary: Set up a PQC-ready TLS profile
description:
‌

Set up a PQC-ready TLS profile in one pass by properly adhering to all aspects of the configured profile.

Hardcoding TLS configuration creates a security vulnerability because it does not align with our evolving, centrally managed security policy for Post-Quantum Cryptography (PQC) readiness. It is necessary that TLS configurations throughout OpenShift & layered products are configured centrally.

summary: LDAP group sync fails with "Filter Compile Error" when using RFC 2307 schema despite following official documentation
description:
**Description of problem:**

```
An error occurs during LDAP group synchronization in OpenShift Container Platform (OCP) 4.20 when using a configuration file based on the official documentation's RFC 2307 sample. The validation fails even if the user follows the instructions to omit the filter when groupUIDAttribute is set to dn.
```

**Version-Release number of selected component (if applicable):**

```
4.20.8
```

**How reproducible:**

```
‌
```

**Steps to Reproduce:**

```
1. Write the LDAPSyncConfig
- rfc2307_config.yaml 
kind: LDAPSyncConfig
apiVersion: v1
url: ldap://LDAP_SERVICE_IP:389 
insecure: false 
bindDN: cn=admin,dc=example,dc=com
#bindPassword:
#  file: "/etc/secrets/bindPassword"
bindPassword: test
rfc2307:
    groupsQuery:
        baseDN: "ou=groups,dc=example,dc=com"
        scope: sub
        derefAliases: never
        pageSize: 0
    groupUIDAttribute: dn 
    groupNameAttributes: [ cn ] 
    groupMembershipAttributes: [ member ] 
    usersQuery:
        baseDN: "ou=users,dc=example,dc=com"
        scope: sub
        derefAliases: never
        pageSize: 0
    userUIDAttribute: dn 
    userNameAttributes: [ mail ] 
    tolerateMemberNotFoundErrors: false
    tolerateMemberOutOfScopeErrors: false

2. 
$  oc adm groups sync --sync-config=/var/tmp/rfc2307_config.yaml 
error: validation of LDAP sync config failed: groupsQuery.filter: Invalid value: "": invalid query filter: LDAP Result Code 201 "Filter Compile Error": ldap: filter does not start with an '('
```

**Actual results:**

```
$  oc adm groups sync --sync-config=/var/tmp/rfc2307_config.yaml 
error: validation of LDAP sync config failed: groupsQuery.filter: Invalid value: "": invalid query filter: LDAP Result Code 201 "Filter Compile Error": ldap: filter does not start with an '('
```

**Expected results:**

```
The sync should proceed without requiring a filter when the entry is uniquely identified by DN, as stated in the documentation.
```

‌

* **Root Cause:** There appears to be a discrepancy between the OCP group sync validation logic and the [documentation](https://docs.redhat.com/en/documentation/openshift_container_platform/4.20/html/authentication_and_authorization/ldap-syncing#ldap-syncing-config-rfc2307_ldap-syncing-groups). 

> The attribute that uniquely identifies a group on the LDAP server. You cannot specify `groupsQuery` filters when using DN for `groupUIDAttribute`. For fine-grained filtering, use the whitelist / blacklist method.

‌

* **Workaround:** Adding a dummy or broad filter (e.g., `(objectclass=ipausergroup)` or `(uid=*)`) bypasses the validation error.

summary: Egressfirewall is completely invalidated after an incorrect configuration is applied
description:
**Description of problem: Egressfirewall object is completely invalidated if an incorrect rule is applied to it** **Version-Release number of selected component (if applicable): 4.19, 4.20** **How reproducible: 100%** **Steps to Reproduce:** 1\. Create and apply an egressfirewall object which denies traffic to the external world ``` apiVersion: k8s.ovn.org/v1 kind: EgressFirewall metadata: name: default namespace: test spec: egress: - to: dnsName: www.redhat.com type: Allow - to: cidrSelector: 0.0.0.0/0 type: Deny ``` 2\. Invalidate the rule: I performed `oc edit egressfirewall default` and replaced `0.0.0.0/0` with `0.0.0.0.0/0`. The customer replaced `0.0.0.0/0` with `random-text` After this, the status of the egressfirewall will be invalidated like this: ``` oc get egressfirewall -o custom-columns="NAME:.metadata.name,NAMESPACE:.metadata.namespace,Status:.status.status" NAME NAMESPACE Status default test EgressFirewall Rules not correctly applied ``` 3\. Now from any pod within the namespace, I am actually able to access the external world (In this example, although I get Access Denied, the curl request did reach this domain ``` sh-5.1# curl https://www.espncricinfo.com/ Access Denied

Access Denied

You don't have permission to access "http://www.espncricinfo.com/" on this server.

Reference #18.cdab3717.1773227540.9dfc3914

https://errors.edgesuite.net/18.cdab3717.1773227540.9dfc3914

``` ‌ **Actual results: I am able to reach the external world and the egressfirewall object is invalidated posing a security risk** **Expected results: The egressfirewall object should not get invalidated. Ideally the** `oc patch` or `oc edit` should not have succeeded. But it does succeed and the “EgressFirewall Rules not correctly applied” is shown. Even if “EgressFirewall Rules not correctly applied” is to be shown, the previous working configurations should not be invalidated. Now I am actually able to access external websites after the Egressfirewall object has been invalidated. ‌ **Additional info:** --- Please fill in the following template while reporting a bug and provide as much relevant information as possible. Doing so will give us the best chance to find a prompt resolution. --- ### Affected Platforms Is it an: * \[ \] internal CI failure * \[-\] customer issue / SD * \[ \] internal Red Hat testing failure --- ### If it is an internal Red Hat testing failure * Please share a kubeconfig or creds to a live cluster for the assignee to debug/troubleshoot along with reproducer steps (especially if it's a telco use case like ICNI, secondary bridges or BM + kubevirt). --- ### If it is a CI failure * Did it happen in different CI lanes? If so, please provide links to multiple failures with the same error instance. * Did it happen in both SDN and OVN jobs

summary: global-pull-secret daemonset node labeling race condition causes pods not to be scheduled on Replace upgrade strategy nodes in time
description:
## Description of problem

The global-pull-secret daemonset relies on a node label to schedule pods onto nodes. For NodePools using the Replace upgrade strategy, the HCCO globalps controller reactively labels nodes after they appear. This creates a race condition: the node may be ready before the controller labels it, causing the daemonset pod to not be scheduled on that node.

The fix moves the labeling from the HCCO globalps controller to the NodePool controller, propagating the label via CAPI Machine labels so it is applied during node onboarding rather than reactively.

## Version-Release number of selected component (if applicable)

4.22

## How reproducible

Sometimes (race condition — depends on timing between node creation and controller reconciliation)

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Create a HostedCluster with a NodePool using the Replace upgrade strategy
2. Trigger a node replacement (e.g., upgrade the NodePool)
3. Observe the new nodes and global-pull-secret daemonset pod scheduling

## Actual results

New Replace-strategy nodes may not receive the global-pull-secret managed label in time, causing the daemonset pod to not be scheduled on those nodes.

## Expected results

All nodes, including those created via the Replace upgrade strategy, should receive the global-pull-secret managed label during onboarding so the daemonset pods are scheduled immediately.

## Additional info

Fix moves label propagation from the HCCO globalps controller (labelNodesForGlobalPullSecret) to the NodePool controller via CAPI Machine label propagation in reconcileMachineDeployment. Only AWS and Azure Replace nodes are affected.

summary: OpenShift Container Platform 4.12  notice end of Extended update support
description:
OpenShift Container Platform 4.12  notice end of Extended update support

This is an automated  notification for the end of Extended update support for OpenShift Container Platform 4.12 on January 17, 2025.  
This notification applies only to those customers operating OpenShift Container Platform 4.12.  
As part of the Extended update support, qualified security patches of critical or important impact, as well as select mission-critical bug-fix patches, were released for OpenShift Container Platform 4.12. 

On January 17, 2025, OpenShift Container Platform 4.12 transitions into its Extended Update Support (EUS) phase, updates may be available for Critical and Important security advisories as well as Urgent priority bugs.  Customers should check for any additional subscription requirements in order to consume these updates, during the EUS phase. Please consult with your sales team to understand more about Extended Update offerings. 

At the end of Extended update support, software and documentation will continue to be available to customers throughout the time OpenShift Container Platform 4 remains available. 

Please see the OpenShift Container Platform 4 life cycle for further information about OpenShift Container Platform 4 releases and life cycle phases: [https://access.redhat.com/support/policy/updates/openshift/](https://access.redhat.com/support/policy/updates/openshift/)

For important instructions on how to install or upgrade to the latest version of OpenShift Container Platform, see the following documentation: [https://docs.openshift.com/container-platform/4.12/updating/updating-cluster-within-minor.html](https://docs.openshift.com/container-platform/4.12/updating/updating-cluster-within-minor.html)

However, no technical support will be provided except assistance to upgrade to a supported version as per the Red Hat OpenShift Container Platform Life Cycle Policy: [https://access.redhat.com/support/policy/updates/openshift/](https://access.redhat.com/support/policy/updates/openshift/)

For important instructions on how to install or upgrade to the latest version of OpenShift Container Platform, see the following documentation: [https://docs.openshift.com/container-platform/4.21/updating/updating-cluster-within-minor.html](https://docs.openshift.com/container-platform/4.21/updating/updating-cluster-within-minor.html)

summary: [release-4.20] - Cluster Baremetal Operator webhook is preventing IBI workflow to work with o-cloud manager
description:
Description of problem:

```java
The baremetal-operator-validating-webhook-configuration is preventing updates to the BaremetalHost.spec.externallyProvisioned property in existing BMHs.

When using o-cloud manager to provisioning clusters, it requires BaremetaHosts to be inspected before these can 
be provisioned. This prevents users from setting externallyProvisioned: true when adding BMHs to the o-cloud manager inventory.

When using IBI to deploy clusters, it's expected that the BMH has the externallyProvisioned property set to true so the configuration ISO can be attached to the node and at thesame time the node boots from the hard drive.

The SiteConfig operator will try to set the externallyProvisioned property to true if the BMH selected for the cluster provisioning doesn't have it set.

The admission webhook will block changes to the BMH and the cluster won't be deployed.
```

Version-Release number of selected component (if applicable):

```java
4.20
```

How reproducible:

```java
Always
```

Steps to Reproduce:

```java
1. Add a BMH and get it inspected
2. Try to deploy a SNO using IBI and o-cloud manager that uses that BMH
```

Actual results:

```java
Cluster deployment will fail, the siteconfig operator will try to set externallyProvisioned to true and the admission webhook will deny the request:

    message: 'failed to update resource during patch operation: admission webhook
      "baremetalhost.metal3.io" denied the request: externallyProvisioned can not
      be changed'
```

Expected results:

```java
The cluster gets deployed.
```

Additional info:

```java
Slack thread: https://redhat-internal.slack.com/archives/C02EG99MR9C/p1762358086401289?thread_ts=1762180815.811049&cid=C02EG99MR9C
```

summary: GlobalPullSecret e2e test flaky due to race condition with CountAvailableNodes during kubelet restarts
description:
This is a clone of issue OCPBUGS-67262. The following is the description of the original issue:   
—  
Description of problem:

```java
The EnsureGlobalPullSecret e2e test is flaky because CountAvailableNodes() is called while nodes are temporarily NotReady due to kubelet restarts triggered by global-pull-secret-syncer.

When additional-pull-secret is updated or deleted, the global-pull-secret-syncer DaemonSet updates /var/lib/kubelet/config.json and restarts kubelet on each node. If CountAvailableNodes() is called during this window, it returns a stale count (e.g., 1 instead of 2).

This causes waitForDaemonSetsReady() to either:
- False pass: actualReady(1) == nodeCount(1) passes prematurely
- False fail: actualReady(2) != nodeCount(1) fails forever with "2/1 pods ready"
```

Version-Release number of selected component (if applicable):

```java
HyperShift main branch (affects all versions with GlobalPullSecret e2e test)
```

How reproducible:

```java
Intermittent - depends on timing of kubelet restart vs. node count query
```

Steps to Reproduce:

```java
N/A , it's a flake that shows intermittently in pre-submits
```

Actual results:

```java
Test either passes prematurely (missing pods) or fails with message like:
"DaemonSet global-pull-secret-syncer not ready: 2/1 pods ready"
```

Expected results:

```java
Test should wait for nodes to stabilize after kubelet-restarting operations and use NodePool replicas as authoritative source for expected node count.
```
