summary: openshift-console does not start with an old logging-view-plugin configuration on 4.19
description:
We had a customer cluster hitting an issue during an upgrade from 4.18 to 4.19.22 that resulted in the console not being able to start up with the following error message:

```java
E0302 12:22:40.578717       1 configmap.go:189] unknown backend type for "logging-view-plugin" plugin: "". Currently only "Service" backend type is supported.
E0302 12:22:40.578754       1 configmap.go:210] unknown proxy service type for "logging-view-plugin" plugin: "". Currently only "Service" proxy endpoint type is supported.
E0302 12:22:40.578759       1 configmap.go:210] unknown proxy service type for "logging-view-plugin" plugin: "". Currently only "Service" proxy endpoint type is supported.
```

Further investigation showed that they were using the 'logging-view-plugin' with the following configuration:

```java
apiVersion: console.openshift.io/v1
kind: ConsolePlugin
metadata:
  creationTimestamp: "2025-02-19T05:37:30Z"
  generation: 25810
  labels:
    app.kubernetes.io/managed-by: observability-operator
    app.kubernetes.io/name: logging-view-plugin
    app.kubernetes.io/part-of: logging
  name: logging-view-plugin
  ownerReferences:
  - apiVersion: observability.openshift.io/v1alpha1
    blockOwnerDeletion: true
    controller: true
    kind: UIPlugin
    name: logging
    uid: 352dff88-4109-463c-b2d2-cf82429319ce
  resourceVersion: "1595400396"
  uid: 8278211e-4c3e-47ec-9c39-1bea5ef791a5
spec:
  displayName: Logging View
  proxy:
  - alias: backend
    authorization: None
  - alias: korrel8r
    authorization: None
```

The 'proxy' fields seem to be missing a required field according to the 'oc explain consoleplugin.spec.proxy' output: 'endpoint'

```java
endpoint      <Object> -required-
    endpoint provides information about endpoint to which the request is proxied
    to.
```

The plugin itself should have been removed from the customer's end before upgrading to 4.18 already (see: [https://access.redhat.com/solutions/7124891](https://access.redhat.com/solutions/7124891) ) - however as this is not enforced it should not break the console.

summary: ABI OVE: Node fails to reboot due to sysroot.mount and ignition-ostree-transposefs-autosave-xfs.service failures
description:
This is a clone of issue OCPBUGS-76382. The following is the description of the original issue:   
—  
Description of problem:

```java
After a system reboot, the node fails during early boot. The failure occurs while mounting /sysroot, which causes ignition-ostree-transposefs-autosave-xfs.service to fail and leads to subsequent failures in initrd. As a result, the system does not complete the boot process.
```

Version-Release number of selected component (if applicable):

```java
4.22
```

```java
Always

2. Wait for the system to reboot.
3. Observe the boot failure during the initrd stage.

summary: HyperShift nodepool controller deletion path does not clear paused state set by OADP backup
description:
Description of problem:

````java
When OADP backup pauses a HC that is currently being uninstalled, the backup may fail and oadp-recovery unpauses the HC. However, the HyeprShift Operator does not propagate the unpause to the inner layers (i.e. HCP object, Machine/machineDeployment objects).

This looks to be a race in the nodepool controller; Reconcile() enters the deletion branch early when DeletionTimestamp (~L201) is set, thus returning before it can reach the pause/unpause logic (~L379).

This blocks cluster uninstallation, causing the cluster to end up in an error state:
```
State:                          error
Details:                        Manifest work deletion is stuck
```

Impact: stuck uninstallations on ROSA HCP production. This is a race condition which only happens when a cluster is being backed up with paused state set during deletion.
````

Version-Release number of selected component (if applicable):

```java
n/a
```

How reproducible:

```java
100% when reproducing the race condition
```

Steps to Reproduce:

```java
1. OADP backup is scheduled/running and pauses the HC
    2. Initiate deletion of a ROSA HCP cluster while backup is in progress
    3. Backup fails (e.g. possibly due to conflict with deletionTimestamp)
    4. oadp-recovery unpauses the HC but the unpause is not reconciled properly.
```

Actual results:

```java
Cluster uninstallation is blocked, and ultimately enters error state. Objects are seen to retain "pausedUntil: "true" annotations.
```

Expected results:

```java
Deletion path should propagate the unpause to inner resources, i.e. `paused: false`, even if a backup fails.
```

**Additional info:**

See also; slack thread: [https://redhat-internal.slack.com/archives/C0A8S4L99C2/p1772448122386659?thread_ts=1772217732.253899&cid=C0A8S4L99C2](https://redhat-internal.slack.com/archives/C0A8S4L99C2/p1772448122386659?thread_ts=1772217732.253899&cid=C0A8S4L99C2)

**Suspected Code path/flow:**

In Reconcile() ([hypershift-operator/controllers/nodepool/nodepool_controller.go](https://github.com/openshift/hypershift/blob/main/hypershift-operator/controllers/nodepool/nodepool_controller.go)), the flow is:  
1.. [Line \~201: If DeletionTimestamp is set → call r.delete() → return early](https://github.com/openshift/hypershift/blob/cce02430ccc1c322e0698bb36421ed003873edef/hypershift-operator/controllers/nodepool/nodepool_controller.go#L202)

2\. [Line \~237: Call r.reconcile() (never reached during deletion) ](https://github.com/openshift/hypershift/blob/cce02430ccc1c322e0698bb36421ed003873edef/hypershift-operator/controllers/nodepool/nodepool_controller.go#L237)

3\. [Line \~379: Pause/unpause logic lives here (capi.Pause())](https://github.com/openshift/hypershift/blob/cce02430ccc1c322e0698bb36421ed003873edef/hypershift-operator/controllers/nodepool/nodepool_controller.go#L379)

4\. [Line \~412](https://github.com/openshift/hypershift/blob/cce02430ccc1c322e0698bb36421ed003873edef/hypershift-operator/contr

summary: Add a note to the Communication Matrix documentation about EndpointSlice limitations and host-open-ports usage
description:
We would like to add the following note to the documentation for [commatrix plugin](https://docs.redhat.com/en/documentation/openshift_container_platform/4.21/html/installation_configuration/configuring-firewall#network-commatrix-plugin-install_configuring-firewall):  
**Note:** The communication matrix relies on EndpointSlices to discover exposed ports. EndpointSlices are automatically created by Kubernetes for each Service. All core OpenShift ports are fully covered by the matrix **from OpenShift 4.21 release**. However, non-core operators that do not expose a Service will not appear in the matrix.  
For a full picture of the cluster’s listening ports (as seen via `ss`), please use the `--host-open-ports` flag to capture the actual open ports on the nodes. You can also compare this output with the declared ports using the generated diff file, which shows differences between intended and actual state

summary: Remove oVirt CSI driver operator + driver images
description:
OCP still ships ovirt-csi-driver-operator and ovirt-csi-driver in 4.15 - 4.20 payloads.

* OCP on RHV Support was announced as [**deprecated & no long supported**](https://docs.redhat.com/en/documentation/openshift_container_platform/4.14/html/release_notes/ocp-4-14-release-notes#ocp-4-14-rhv-deprecations) in OCP 4.14,   RHV [ELP ends on Aug 31, 2026](https://access.redhat.com/support/policy/updates/rhev) .
* The latest approved [support exceptions for OCP on RHV](https://redhat.atlassian.net/secure/Dashboard.jspa?selectPageId=12394701) ended on Oct 31, 2025.

We should remove the image from the payload.

There are instructions how to remove an image from y-stream: [https://art-docs.engineering.redhat.com/sop/remove-payload-tag/](https://art-docs.engineering.redhat.com/sop/remove-payload-tag/)

On our side, we need to ensure that there is no CI job using/building the images and that CSO does not refer to the image in code, yaml assets and image-references. Sync with ART how to actually stop building & shipping the images on their side.

summary: Certificate expiry dates are not uniform
description:
Description of problem:

```java
Different certificates in MicroShift have different expiry dates. Some of them have 1y or 10y validity, and having drift in the year is ok. 
What may be disruptive is having different months and/or days in the expiry dates, which would trigger different forced restarts for renewal. It would be ideal to align the expiry date of all certificates to the same day and time.
```

description:
Description of problem:

```java
after reboot of nodes, GNSS port number attached to primary interface (ens1f0) leading to node time getting out of sync.
```

PTP operator ptp-operator.v4.18.0-202512150311

```

Steps to Reproduce:

```java

2. check the active primary interface in the nodes and the active GNSS port index 
            [root@bastion 7.ptp]# oc debug node/master1.viairtel.samsungbsc.local
        Temporary namespace openshift-debug-w8mrn is created for debugging node...
        Starting pod/master1viairtelsamsungbsclocal-debug-ldxhg ...

        sh-5.1# chroot /host
        sh-5.1# for iface in /sys/class/net/*; do
            if ls "$iface"/device/gnss* >/dev/null 2>&1; then
                echo "Interface: $(basename "$iface")"
                ls -l "$iface"/device/gnss*
                echo
            fi
        done
        Interface: ens1f0
        total 0
        drwxr-xr-x. 3 root root 0 Feb 18 13:38 gnss0

        Interface: ens2f0
        total 0
        drwxr-xr-x. 3 root root 0 Feb 18 13:38 gnss1

        Interface: ens4f0
        total 0
        drwxr-xr-x. 3 root root 0 Feb 18 13:38 gnss2

        sh-5.1#
        sh-5.1#
        sh-5.1# sudo cat /dev/gnss0
        $GNRMC,120737.00,A,3715.43806,N,12703.17228,E,0.000,,020326,,,A,V*11
        $GNGGA,120737.00,3715.43806,N,12703.17228,E,1,11,99.99,191.8,M,18.8,M,,*72
        ▒bx▒▒d▒h▒▒▒b"x▒▒^n▒▒▒▒xterm-256color$GNRMC,120738.00,A,3715.43806,N,12703.17228,E,0.000,,020326,,,A,V*1E
        $GNGGA,120738.00,3715.43806,N,12703.17228,E,1,11,99.99,191.8,M,18.8,M,,*7D
        xterm-256color^Cq▒▒'
        sh-5.1#3. apply grandmaster YAML with the correct values (below is the grandmaster file we apply in one of the nodes. Similar is applied for all nodes)[root@bastion 7.ptp]# cat 04.grandmaster-clock-master1.yaml
apiVersion: ptp.openshift.io/v1
kind: PtpConfig
metadata:
  name: grandmastermaster1
  namespace: openshift-ptp
spec:
  profile:
  - name: "grandmastermaster1"
    ptp4lOpts: "-2 --summary_interval -4"
    phc2sysOpts: -r -u 0 -m -O -37 -N 8 -R 16 -s ens1f0 -n 24
    ptpSchedulingPolicy: SCHED_FIFO
    ptpSchedulingPriority: 10
    ptpSettings:
      logReduce: "true"
    plugins:
      e810:
        enableDefaultConfig: false

summary: [release-4.18] UDP connections appear to continue sending traffic against stale pods instead the right endpoints
description:
Description of problem:

After an endpoint rollout from an application deployment, the new pod endpoints have been updated to the OVN DB correctly where they are part of a LoadBalancer service advertised via Metallb. 

```java
$ sed -n '4p' ovnkube-node-xxx_nbdb_compact  | jq -r '.Load_Balancer[]| select(.name == "Service_<app-namespace>/snmpmgr_UDP_cluster")'
{
  "name": "Service_<app-namespace>/snmpmgr_UDP_cluster",
  "protocol": "udp",

  "vips": [
    "map",
    [
      [
        "1xx.76.xxx.11:162", <-----
        "10.128.4.50:1620,10.130.0.46:1620" <----
      ],
      [
        "172.30.105.157:162",
        "10.128.4.50:1620,10.130.0.46:1620"
      ]
    ]
  ]
```

On the other hand, the existing traffic against the LB SVC IP is going against the the stale pod IPs (10.131.2.31 and 10.129.4.20). New connections toward the same service at UDP port are going to the right IPs from updated endpoints, however these 'older' connections continue being forwarded to the wrong pod IPs. The TCP traffic is not affected.

While checking the OVS datapaths and conntrack table, it is able to notice that connection still exist in the node. As UDP connections in conntrack from node are marked as seen reply and assured. Even after an OVN DB rebuilding + conntrack delete from undesired sessions, the external connections are still being sent to the wrong pod IPs:

summary: [4.21] metal3-baremetal-operator pod not honoring NO_PROXY and routing metal3-state service traffic via proxy (works in 4.19.z)
description:
**Summary:**  
In OpenShift Container Platform (OCP) **4.20.z** versions, the `metal3-baremetal-operator` pod is not honoring the configured `NO_PROXY` environment variable and is attempting to connect to the internal `metal3-state` **service** via the configured HTTP/HTTPS proxy. **This behavior is not observed in OCP 4.19.z**, where the operator correctly bypasses the proxy for internal service communication.

* Platform: Baremetal (IPI)

summary: CAPI v1.11: Nodepool rolling update e2e test fails
description:
Description of problem:

The _TestRollingUpdate_ e2e test fails when checking for the updated instance types on all AWS machines when updating to CAPI v1.11:

```java
nodepool_rolling_upgrade_test.go:120: Successfully waited for NodePool e2e-clusters-n24l8/node-pool-brllr-test-rolling-upgrade to finish the rolling upgrade in 6m33.125s
    nodepool_rolling_upgrade_test.go:143: 
        Expected
            <string>: m5.large
        to equal
            <string>: m5.xlarge
```

The test checks the _InstanceType_ on old AWS machines that are still being deleted despite waiting for the nodepool to finish the upgrade.

Version-Release number of selected component (if applicable):

```java
CAPA v2.10.0 / CAPI v1.11
```

How reproducible:

```java
Always
```

Steps to Reproduce:

1\. Build Hypershift and the Hypershift operator with CAPI 1.11 from the [bump PR.](https://github.com/openshift/hypershift/pull/7590)  
2\. Install hypershift using the new operator image in a management cluster.  
3\. Run the _TestNodePool/HostedCluster0/Main/TestRollingUpgrade_ e2e test on the management cluster with the CAPI 1.11 operator.

Actual results:

```java
The MachineDeploymentComplete function returns true despite old machines still not deleted.
```

Expected results:

```java
The MachineDeploymentComplete function should return true only when old machines are completely removed as it behaves in CAPI v1.10
```

 

RCA:

After debugging, the sequence of events that leads to the bug is the following:

1. Old replicas = 1; This is the one replica in deletion throughout the whole test (this never changes). One old machine is deleted already and the second one is in deletion.
2. `UpdatedReplicas` goes from 1 to 2 (the second updated machine is ready)
3. `Replicas` has not caught up yet and is 2 instead of 3 (what it should be: 2 updated + 1 deleting)
4. `Replicas` catches up and is now 3, but it is too late, the test has already moved to the machine verification => test fails.

Logs of the blip in `{*}MachineDeploymentComplete(){*}:`

```java
{"level":"info","ts":"2026-03-03T13:08:09Z","msg":"Machine deployment completion:","name":"node-pool-lh767-test-rolling-upgrade","complete":false,"status.Replicas":2,"spec.Replicas":2,"status.AvailableReplicas":2,"status.UpdatedReplicass":1} {"level":"info","ts":"2026-03-03T13:08:09Z","msg":"Machine deployment completion:","name":"node-pool-lh767-test-rolling-upgrade","complete":true,"status.Replicas":2,"spec.Replicas":2,"status.AvailableReplicas":2,"status.UpdatedReplicass":2} {"level":"info","ts":"2026-03-03T13:08:09Z","msg":"Machine deployment completion:","name":"node-pool-lh767-test-rolling-upgrade","complete":true,"status.Replicas":2,"spec.Replicas":2,"status.AvailableReplicas":2,"status.UpdatedReplicass":2} {"level":"info","ts":"2026-03-03T13:08:09Z","msg":"Machine deployment completion:","name":"node-pool-lh767-test-rolling-upgrade","complete":false,"status.Replicas":3,"spec.Replicas":2,"status.AvailableReplicas":2,"

summary: [IBMCloud] Machine-Controller Premature Replacement
description:
Description of problem:

```java
the machine Replaced, Node object still use the deleted one
```

Version-Release number of selected component (if applicable):

```java
4.22.0-0.nightly-2026-02-26-092444
```

How reproducible:

```java
just found once
```

Steps to Reproduce:

```java
1. install cluster on the IBM Cloud VPC 
    2. one worker VM replaced.
    3.
```

Actual results:

```java
Node Object not update
```

Expected results:

```java
Node Object updated
```

Additional info:

```java
Prow CI job https://qe-private-deck-ci.apps.ci.l2s4.p1.openshiftapps.com/view/gs/qe-private-deck/logs/periodic-ci-openshift-openshift-tests-private-release-4.22-amd64-nightly-ibmcloud-ipi-private-byo-kms-f28/2027994464655511552 

The machine-api operator logs reveal the EXACT sequence of events that caused
the Node object to become permanently desynchronized from the Machine object
during machine replacement.
ROOT CAUSE: During machine replacement, the machine-controller clears the
Machine's providerID and addresses (as designed), which breaks the link that
nodelink-controller uses to match Nodes to Machines. When the replacement
completes and the Machine gets a new IP address, nodelink-controller cannot
re-establish the link because it searches by IP/providerID matching, and the
Node still has the old IP.
RESULT: Node object permanently stuck with deleted instance's IP (10.244.1.4),
while Machine object correctly updated with new instance's IP (10.244.1.5).
```

summary: HCP request-serving reconciliation behavior during single-AZ outage (mec1-az2) - validation of multi-AZ self-healing
description:
During an AWS power outage impacting a single Availability Zone (me-central-1, mec1-az2), we observed degraded behavior in clusters with multi-AZ configuration.

* External trigger: AWS AZ outage (mec1-az2)

* API degradation observed (AssociateAddress / networking errors)

* ROSA Classic and HCP clusters impacted

**Expected Behaviors: For multi-AZ clusters:**

* ROSA Classic: workers are AZ-bound via MachineSets; recreation expected within same AZ.

* ROSA HCP: request-serving nodes are expected to recreate in healthy AZs to preserve serving capacity.

**Observed Behavior:**

* HCP request-serving nodes did not immediately rebalance to healthy AZs.

* Machine reconciliation observed (Provisioning / Deleting states), but replacement progress appeared stalled during outage.

* Need to validate:

    * Whether MachineHealthCheck triggered deletion as expected.
    * Whether replacement machine creation progressed in healthy AZs.
    * Whether AWS API errors were the primary bottleneck.
    

This ticket is created to track engineering investigation into reconciliation behavior under single-AZ outage conditions and confirm whether system behavior aligns with design expectations.

P.S  [INT-2026-00058](https://redhat-internal.slack.com/archives/C0AHSCGT3QW/p1772409985579889?thread_ts=1772407975.161689&cid=C0AHSCGT3QW)

The gathered logs as required: [https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1ipIfg3xDkhLjvgLSEcra-qr6CIQjiwkq?usp=sharing](https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1ipIfg3xDkhLjvgLSEcra-qr6CIQjiwkq?usp=sharing)

summary: Node stop announcing its address if the node is cordoned when using MetalLB Operator in v4.18
description:
Description of problem:

IHAC who reported an issue with Metallb in OpenShift v4.18. In the past, the same issue has been reported in the upstream version `0.14.9`, which eventually was resolved by upgrading to verison `0.15.2`. But now in the metallb-operator version `metallb-operator.v4.18.0-202601201947`, we seem to be seeing the same issue.

* The change in PR 2470 \[1\] causes a node to stop announcing its address if the node is cordoned. For us this is a landmine waiting to catch us by surprise and cause an outage.
* That change was reverted in PR 2715 \[2\] in version 0.15.0

\[1\] [https://github.com/metallb/metallb/pull/2470](https://github.com/metallb/metallb/pull/2470)  
\[2\] [https://github.com/metallb/metallb/pull/2715](https://github.com/metallb/metallb/pull/2715)

The customer also confirmed that they do have an OpenShift v4.19 cluster in which we have `metallb-operator.v4.19.0-202601120612`, and we don't see the misbehavior there. We can cordon a node, and it continues to announce addresses.

Version-Release number of selected component (if applicable):

```java
metallb-operator version metallb-operator.v4.18.0-202601201947
```

How reproducible:

```java
Always
```

Steps to Reproduce:

```java
1. Configure Metallb to announce an IP address in L2 mode
    2. Cordon the node which is announcing the IP address currently
    3. Observe if the node still continues to announce the address
```

Actual results:

```java
Node stop announcing its address if the node is cordoned.
```

Expected results:

```java
After we cordon a node, it should continue to announce addresses
```

Additional info:

I also opened a Slack thread\[3\] with engineering, and as discussed, I am reporting this bug, so that engineering can work on the backport.

\[3\] [https://redhat-internal.slack.com/archives/C01EH16NFPZ/p1771928354721749](https://redhat-internal.slack.com/archives/C01EH16NFPZ/p1771928354721749)

summary: Windows ImageType e2e test missing EC2 AMI verification for WinLI billing compliance
description:
## Description of problem

The Windows ImageType e2e test in HyperShift did not verify that EC2 instances actually use the Windows License Included (WinLI) AMI. Without this critical verification layer, nodes can boot with an incorrect AMI while the test still passes, leading to AWS billing compliance violations where customers are billed incorrectly for Windows licensing.

The test only validated the NodePool condition (`ValidPlatformImageType`) and node OS metadata, but never queried the AWS EC2 API to confirm the actual AMI in use on running instances.

## Version-Release number of selected component (if applicable)

4.21

## How reproducible

Always (structural gap present in all test runs prior to fix)

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Run the Windows ImageType e2e test against a ROSA HCP cluster on a release prior to the fix.
2. Observe that the test validates the `ValidPlatformImageType` NodePool condition is populated.
3. Observe that the test validates node OS is reported as RHCOS/Linux.
4. Note that the test does **not** call AWS EC2 `DescribeInstances` to confirm the actual AMI ID in use.
5. If the controller selects an incorrect AMI, the test passes without detecting the billing compliance violation.

## Actual results

The Windows ImageType test passes even when the EC2 instance AMI is not confirmed against the expected Windows LI AMI ID from the NodePool condition. There is no `DescribeInstances` call to validate actual AMI usage at the infrastructure layer, leaving billing compliance violations undetected.

## Expected results

The Windows ImageType test should perform a 3-layer verification:

1. **Layer 1 – NodePool Discovery:** `ValidPlatformImageType` condition is populated and contains the expected AMI ID.
2. **Layer 2 – Node OS Validation:** Node boots with RHCOS and OS image contains "Red Hat Enterprise Linux CoreOS".
3. **Layer 3 – EC2 AMI Verification (critical):** AWS EC2 `DescribeInstances` is called to confirm instances are actually running the expected Windows LI AMI, comparing actual vs. expected AMI IDs. A mismatch must fail the test.

## Additional info

* Fix PR (main): [openshift/hypershift#7680](https://github.com/openshift/hypershift/pull/7680)
* Related JIRA story: [CNTRLPLANE-753](https://redhat.atlassian.net/browse/CNTRLPLANE-753)
* Related JIRA feature: [OCPSTRAT-1949](https://redhat.atlassian.net/browse/OCPSTRAT-1949)
* The fix adds `extractAMIFromMessage()` helper, EC2 AMI comparison via AWS SDK, and node OS validation to the test.

summary: [release-4.21]Windows ImageType test lacks EC2 AMI verification, risking AWS billing compliance violations
description:
## Description of problem

The Windows ImageType e2e test in HyperShift did not verify that EC2 instances actually use the Windows License Included (WinLI) AMI. Without this verification layer, it is possible for nodes to boot with an incorrect AMI while the test still passes, leading to AWS billing compliance violations — specifically, customers being billed incorrectly for Windows licensing.

The test previously validated only the NodePool condition (`ValidPlatformImageType`) and node OS metadata, but never queried the AWS EC2 API to confirm the actual AMI in use on running instances.

This gap was addressed by cherry-pick PR [#7830](https://github.com/openshift/hypershift/pull/7830) (backport of [#7680](https://github.com/openshift/hypershift/pull/7680)) to the `release-4.21` branch.

## Version-Release number of selected component (if applicable)

4.21

## How reproducible

Always (test gap present in all test runs prior to fix)

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Run the Windows ImageType e2e test against a ROSA HCP cluster on a release prior to the fix.
2. Observe that the test validates:

    * `ValidPlatformImageType` NodePool condition is populated
    * Node OS is reported as RHCOS/Linux
    
3. Observe that the test does **not** query the AWS EC2 API (`DescribeInstances`) to confirm the actual AMI ID in use.
4. If the controller selects an incorrect AMI, the test passes without detecting the billing compliance violation.

## Actual results

The Windows ImageType test passes even when the EC2 instance AMI is not confirmed against the expected Windows LI AMI ID extracted from the NodePool condition. There is no `DescribeInstances` call to validate actual AMI usage at the infrastructure layer.

## Expected results

The Windows ImageType test should perform a 3-layer verification:

1. **Layer 1 – NodePool Discovery:** `ValidPlatformImageType` condition is populated and contains the expected AMI ID.
2. **Layer 2 – Node OS Validation:** Node boots with RHCOS and OS image contains "Red Hat Enterprise Linux CoreOS".
3. **Layer 3 – EC2 AMI Verification (critical):** AWS EC2 `DescribeInstances` is called to confirm instances are actually running the expected Windows LI AMI, comparing actual vs. expected AMI IDs. A mismatch must fail the test.

## Additional info

* Fix PR (main): [openshift/hypershift#7680](https://github.com/openshift/hypershift/pull/7680)
* Cherry-pick PR (release-4.21): [openshift/hypershift#7830](https://github.com/openshift/hypershift/pull/7830)
* Related JIRA story: [CNTRLPLANE-753](https://redhat.atlassian.net/browse/CNTRLPLANE-753)
* Related JIRA feature: [OCPSTRAT-1949](https://redhat.atlassian.net/browse/OCPSTRAT-1949)
* The fix adds `extractAMIFromMessage()` helper, EC2 AMI comparison via AWS SDK, and comprehensive validation comments to the test.

summary: CVE-2026-25639 openshift4/ose-monitoring-plugin-rhel9: Axios affected by Denial of Service via __proto__ Key in mergeConfig [openshift-4.22]
description:
Security Tracking Issue

Do not make this issue public.

Flaw:

---

Axios affected by Denial of Service via \__proto_\_ Key in mergeConfig

Axios is a promise based HTTP client for the browser and Node.js. Prior to 1.13.5, the mergeConfig function in axios crashes with a TypeError when processing configuration objects containing \__proto_\_ as an own property. An attacker can trigger this by providing a malicious configuration object created via JSON.parse(), causing complete denial of service. This vulnerability is fixed in 1.13.5.

\~\~\~

summary: e2e-aws-ovn-edge-zones CI job fails: TEST_SKIPS regex "internal should be reachable with hairpinning traffic" matches no tests
description:
## Description of problem

The CI job `pull-ci-openshift-origin-release-4.19-e2e-aws-ovn-edge-zones` fails immediately in the `openshift-e2e-test` step because the `TEST_SKIPS` workaround configured in the `openshift-e2e-aws-edge-zones` workflow no longer matches any test in the filtered test suite.

The workflow file in openshift/release sets:

```java
TEST_SKIPS: internal should be reachable with hairpinning traffic
# FIXME: remove this once the issue is fixed: https://redhat.atlassian.net/browse/OCPBUGS-77166
```

The `openshift-e2e-test-commands.sh` script validates that the `TEST_SKIPS` regex matches at least one test before proceeding. When no tests match, the step exits with code 1 before any real tests run.

This failure is a CI infrastructure issue in the openshift/release step registry - it is unrelated to the PR under test.

## Version-Release number of selected component (if applicable)

4.19.0-0.ci-2026-02-28-041706

## How reproducible

Always (every run of this job with the current workflow configuration)

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Open a PR against openshift/origin release-4.19 branch
2. CI triggers `pull-ci-openshift-origin-release-4.19-e2e-aws-ovn-edge-zones`
3. The `openshift-e2e-test` step runs and executes `openshift-tests` with `TEST_SKIPS="internal should be reachable with hairpinning traffic"`
4. `openshift-tests` lists 3,534 filtered tests for the aws/OVNKubernetes/edge-zones configuration
5. The TEST_SKIPS grep finds no matching test - immediate exit with code 1

## Actual results

The `e2e-aws-ovn-edge-zones-openshift-e2e-test` step fails immediately with:

```java
time="2026-02-28T05:21:22Z" level=info msg="Discovered 7925 internal tests, 6399 external tests - 7402 total unique tests"
time="2026-02-28T05:21:22Z" level=info msg="Found 3534 filtered tests"
Skipping tests:
Error: no tests were found matching the TEST_SKIPS regex:
internal should be reachable with hairpinning traffic
```

## Expected results

Either:

* The TEST_SKIPS regex matches the hairpinning test and excludes it from the run, then the remaining tests execute normally
* OR the TEST_SKIPS workaround is removed/updated (if OCPBUGS-77166 is resolved or the test was renamed/removed)

## Additional info

**Job Details:**

* Job URL: [https://prow.ci.openshift.org/view/gs/test-platform-results/pr-logs/pull/30399/pull-ci-openshift-origin-release-4.19-e2e-aws-ovn-edge-zones/2027595494296915968](https://prow.ci.openshift.org/view/gs/test-platform-results/pr-logs/pull/30399/pull-ci-openshift-origin-release-4.19-e2e-aws-ovn-edge-zones/2027595494296915968)
* Build ID: 2027595494296915968
* Example PR affected: openshift/origin#30399 (OCPBUGS-57206: Add imagestream update dryrun test - unrelated to this CI failure)

**Root Cause:**  
The FIXME workaround in `ci-operator/step-registry/openshift/e2e/aws/edge-zones/openshift-e2e-aws-edge-zones-workflow.yaml` (referencing OCPBUGS-77166) skips a hairpinning traffic test. However, the test "internal should be reachable

summary: OC is not a stripped binary
description:
This is a clone of issue OCPBUGS-77486. The following is the description of the original issue:   
—  
This is a clone of issue OCPBUGS-76319. The following is the description of the original issue:   
—  
The OC Binaries released on mirror.openshift.com and inside of RHCOS/OCP images are unstripped binaries.  
This means OC is being shipped with debug symbols which bloats images with unnecessary debug symbols.

Stripping manually \`/usr/bin/oc\` produces a 130MB clean binary instead of 201MB one.

‌

```

Actual results:

```java
The VolumeSnapshotContent object gets indefinitely stuck in a Deleting/Terminating state until the azure-disk-csi-driver-controller pods are restarted.

Expected results:

The Terminating VolumeSnapshotContent object would eventually reconcile with the Azure backend resource and get removed without a restart of the disk-csi-driver-controller pods being required.

summary: OCP 4.20.z: metal3-baremetal-operator pod not honoring NO_PROXY and routing metal3-state service traffic via proxy (works in 4.19.z)
description:
**Summary:**  
In OpenShift Container Platform (OCP) **4.20.z** versions, the `metal3-baremetal-operator` pod is not honoring the configured `NO_PROXY` environment variable and is attempting to connect to the internal `metal3-state` **service** via the configured HTTP/HTTPS proxy. **This behavior is not observed in OCP 4.19.z**, where the operator correctly bypasses the proxy for internal service communication.

**Environment:**

* OCP Version: 4.20.z (multiple z-streams observed)

* Working Version: 4.19.z

* Platform: Baremetal (IPI)

* Proxy configuration enabled (cluster-wide proxy configured)

* Affected Pod: `metal3-baremetal-operator`

* Target Service: `metal3-state` (ClusterIP service)

 

**Please find the reproducer details below. All cluster information is from my test environment and does not contain any sensitive data. I will also share the cluster must-gather shortly.**

summary: podman-ectcd should check if peer is capable of adding members
description:
The \`podman-etcd\` RA's start function, when joining as a learner, enters a  
10-minute polling loop waiting for the peer's monitor to set the  
\`learner_node\` CRM attribute. The loop does not check whether the peer is  
actually capable of adding members — it blindly polls the attribute every  
5 seconds for up to 600 seconds regardless of the peer's state

Secondary issue: In the restore script: improve error messaging

Regardless of the RA fix, the restore script's error message should tell  
the customer what happened and what to do.

Analysis: [https://drive.google.com/file/d/1N7xACCN7xHeQdVExx42lOyV57nFEbUs5/view?usp=drive_link](https://drive.google.com/file/d/1N7xACCN7xHeQdVExx42lOyV57nFEbUs5/view?usp=drive_link)

summary: AWS S3 to disable the use of SSE-C encryption by default
description:
AWS is changing default behavior for S3 buckets (with customer managed keys). We need to check whether these changes will affect us and if action is needed. 

Specifically, we need to check whether we allow customer managed keys to encrypt bootstrap ignition and whether this change affects those.

Here's the relevant email, which includes links with more info:

 

Starting on April 6, 2026, we will be changing how [server-side encryption with customer-provided keys (SSE-C)](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/ServerSideEncryptionCustomerKeys.html) is enabled for [Amazon S3](https://aws.amazon.com/s3/) buckets. With this change, SSE-C will be disabled by default on all new S3 general purpose buckets. Furthermore, SSE-C will also be disabled for all existing buckets in Amazon Web Services (AWS) Accounts that do not have any SSE-C encrypted data. This change will start on April 6, 2026 and will be rolled out to all [AWS Regions](https://aws.amazon.com/about-aws/global-infrastructure/regions_az/) within weeks.

Following blog has more details about this change:

[https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/storage/advanced-notice-amazon-s3-to-disable-the-use-of-sse-c-encryption-by-default-for-all-new-buckets-and-select-existing-buckets-in-april-2026/](https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/storage/advanced-notice-amazon-s3-to-disable-the-use-of-sse-c-encryption-by-default-for-all-new-buckets-and-select-existing-buckets-in-april-2026/)

AWS S3 Service team asked account team to communicate this upcoming change to Red Hat as we had a major issue last time with OpenShift installer due to this S3 [change](https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/aws/heads-up-amazon-s3-security-changes-are-coming-in-april-of-2023/) in 2023.

summary: IPI on IBM Cloud - `install-config.yaml` referenced after being consumed by `create manifests` in Manual CCO procedure
description:
Description of problem:

[https://docs.redhat.com/en/documentation/openshift_container_platform/4.21/html-single/installing_on_ibm_cloud/index#manually-create-iam-ibm-cloud_installing-ibm-cloud-vpc](https://docs.redhat.com/en/documentation/openshift_container_platform/4.21/html-single/installing_on_ibm_cloud/index#manually-create-iam-ibm-cloud_installing-ibm-cloud-vpc)

 

**Brief Description:**  
In section 6.8 (Manually creating IAM), the documented workflow first instructs running \`openshift-install create manifests\`, which consumes and removes the \`install-config.yaml\` file from the installation directory. However, a later step requires passing \`--install-config=<path>/install-config.yaml\` to the \`oc adm release extract --credentials-requests\` command. At this stage, the \`install-config.yaml\` file no longer exists, causing confusion and command failure. The documentation does not mention preserving a copy of the file or recreating it before this step.

**Suggested Fix:**

One of the following clarifications should be added before running \`create manifests\`:

**Option 1 (Recommended - simplest):**  
Add an explicit note before Step 2:

\> The \`create manifests\` command consumes the \`install-config.yaml\` file. Before running this command, create a backup copy of the file (for example, \`cp install-config.yaml install-config.yaml.bak\`) and use that backup when running the \`oc adm release extract --credentials-requests\` command.

Then update the example command to reference the backup file:

\`\`\`  
\--install-config=<path>/install-config.yaml.bak  
\`\`\`

**Option 2 (Alternative approach):**  
Reorder the steps so that the \`oc adm release extract --credentials-requests\` command is executed **before** running \`openshift-install create manifests\`, ensuring the \`install-config.yaml\` file is still present.

Either of these corrections would prevent command failure and remove ambiguity for users performing manual CCO setup on IBM Cloud VPC.

summary: PTP Operator 4.20: ubxtool EnableNMEA/DisableBinary logs success but GNSS remains in binary-only mode unless additional flags (-v 1 -w 2) are explicitly provided
description:
Customer is testing PTP Operator on OpenShift Container Platform 4.20.4 (fresh installation) using Intel E810 NIC with ZED-F9T GNSS module in T-GM configuration.

The linuxptp-daemon internally executes ubxtool commands to enable NMEA and disable BINARY mode. Operator logs show successful execution:

Enable NMEA  
disable binary

No "error executing ubxtool command" messages are present.

However, despite success logs, the GNSS module sometimes remains in binary-only mode. When this occurs, ts2phc fails with:

nmea: no valid rmc fix  
source ts not valid

Customer confirmed that when explicitly defining the same ubxtool commands in PtpConfig under ublxCmds, including additional flags (-v 1 -w 2), the GNSS mode switches correctly and time synchronization works reliably.

This suggests the internal ubxtool invocation may be missing required flags or there may be a timing/race condition during initialization.

Version-Release number of selected component (if applicable):  
PTP Operator on OpenShift Container Platform 4.20.4

How reproducible:  
Intermittent on specific server hardware. Reproducible when GNSS module is manually set to binary-only mode and PtpConfig is applied without explicit ublxCmds including -v 1 -w 2 flags.

Steps to Reproduce:  
1\. Deploy OpenShift 4.20.4 on bare metal with Intel E810 + ZED-F9T GNSS.  
2\. Configure PTP Operator in T-GM mode without explicit ublxCmds.  
3\. Ensure GNSS module is in binary-only mode.  
4\. Apply PtpConfig and observe linuxptp-daemon logs.  
5\. Observe ts2phc errors: "nmea: no valid rmc fix" and "source ts not valid".

Actual results:

Operator logs indicate successful execution of Enable NMEA and disable binary commands, but GNSS remains in binary-only mode and ts2phc fails to receive valid NMEA data.

Expected results:

When linuxptp-daemon logs show successful execution of Enable NMEA and disable binary, the GNSS module should reliably switch to NMEA mode and ts2phc should receive valid RMC messages.

Additional info:

Workaround: explicitly define ubxtool commands in PtpConfig with flags:

\-v 1 -w 2 -P 29.20 -e NMEA  
\-v 1 -w 2 -P 29.20 -d BINARY

When these are included, GNSS mode switches correctly and synchronization works.

This is a fresh 4.20.4 installation (not an upgrade). Not reproducible on all tested servers with similar hardware.

summary: RHACS is keeping stale/outdated nodes and resources.
description:
Description of problem:

```java
- After deleting a node, customer found out the node still shows up in the Nodes CVEs page (/main/vulnerabilities/node-cves/nodes/) even if the node was recycled 10 days ago.
- It seems that RHACS is keeping stale/outdated nodes (and resources), and this node should have been removed from RHACS since it does not exist anymore.
- It seems ACS doesn't purge older information.
- Outdated deployments are still showing up in Platfrom Vulnerabilties.
- This leads to false positives and creates unnecessary noise in vulnerability reports/dashboards.
```

How reproducible:

```java
In RHACS 4.8, delete the node with few deployments. After that the node still shows up in the Nodes CVEs page.
```

Actual results:

```java
Node and Deployments still shows up
```

Expected results:

```java
Node and Outdated Deployments should be deleted
```

summary: In rhel 10 podman inside nodes cannot reach outside the cluster
description:
Description of problem:

```java
When in a rhel10 clutser we connect to a node, and we try to use podman inside the node, podman cannot reach the network outside the cluster.

    If we do it in a rhel9 node there is no problem doing that.
```

Version-Release number of selected component (if applicable):

```java
4.22
```

How reproducible:

```java
Always
```

Steps to Reproduce:

```java
1. Enable the rhel10 stream in a 4.22 techpreview cluster

 oc patch mcp worker --type=merge -p "{\"spec\":{\"osImageStream\":{\"name\":\"rhel-10\"}}}"



    2. Wait until the worker pool is using rhel10

    3. Debug into a worker node with "oc debug...."

    4. Create a Containerfile with this content

sh-5.2# cat Containerfile 
FROM quay.io/openshift-release-dev/ocp-v4.0-art-dev@sha256:4a1798a3b92a794a69d56eaf78c1521a1c4d2e52fd05057072780ec19ccabd45
RUN curl -LO https://pkgs.tailscale.com/stable/fedora/tailscale.repo


    5. Build an image using the container file 


     sh-5.2# podman build  --authfile /var/lib/kubelet/config.json  . -t with9 
The result is this error
STEP 2/2: RUN curl -LO https://pkgs.tailscale.com/stable/fedora/tailscale.repo
  % Total    % Received % Xferd  Average Speed   Time    Time     Time  Current
                                 Dload  Upload   Total   Spent    Left  Speed
  0     0    0     0    0     0      0      0 --:--:--  0:00:19 --:--:--     0curl: (6) Could not resolve host: pkgs.tailscale.com
Error: building at STEP "RUN curl -LO https://pkgs.tailscale.com/stable/fedora/tailscale.repo": while running runtime: exit status 6
```

Actual results:

```java
The podman build command cannot reach the network outside the cluster
```

Expected results:

```java
The podman build command should be able to get the tailscale information and build the image
```

Additional info:

```java
This is the AI analysis:

Root Cause

  The problem is a conflict between two FORWARD chains combined with a firewall backend change between netavark versions on RHEL 9 vs RHEL 10.

  RHEL 9 (works) — netavark 1.17.2 with iptables-nft backend

  There is one FORWARD chain in table ip filter with policy DROP, and netavark injects its rules directly into it:

  chain FORWARD {
      policy drop;
      jump NETAVARK_FORWARD   <-- netavark adds this to allow container traffic
      <OVN rules>
  }

  Container traffic from 10.88.0.0/16 hits NETAVARK_FORWARD first and gets accepted. Packets are forwarded, masqueraded, and reach the internet.

  RHEL 10 (fails) — netavark 1.16.0 with native nftables backend

  There are two FORWARD chains at the same priority:

  1. table ip filter → chain FORWARD (from OVN/kube — policy DROP):
  chain FORWARD {
      policy drop;
      <only OVN/kube subnets accepted>
      # NO netavark rules here!
  }
  2. table inet netavark → chain FORWARD (from netavark — policy ACCEPT):
  chain FORWARD {
      policy accept;
      ct state invalid drop
      jump NETAVA

summary: When creating a hosted cluster, if the nodeport is defined as 10000, the creation fails
description:
This is a clone of issue OCPBUGS-65824. The following is the description of the original issue:   
—  
Description of problem:

```java
When creating a hosted cluster, if the port is defined as 10000, the creation fails. Stuck with 3 pods only.
```

Version-Release number of selected component (if applicable):

```java
Experienced with:
Hub: 4.16.37
Hosted: 4.17.44
```

How reproducible:

```java
Always
```

Steps to Reproduce:

```java
1. Create a new cluster
    2. Specify the cluster port as 10000
    3.
```

Actual results:

```java
Installation get stuck with 3 pods only in the hosted cluster namespace.
```

Expected results:

```java
Successful installation
```

Additional info:

```java
The logs from control-plane-operator report that the port 10000 is not within the acceptable range: 30000-xxxxx.

This is what I recommend:
- Add this range in the field tool tip message.
- Validate the port value in the form, and reject invalid values.
```

summary: [release-4.17] Incorrect AlertManagerConfig causing Monitoring operator degraded
description:
This is a clone of issue OCPBUGS-77398. The following is the description of the original issue:   
—  
This is a clone of issue OCPBUGS-77269. The following is the description of the original issue:   
—  
This is a clone of issue OCPBUGS-77190. The following is the description of the original issue:   
—  
This is a clone of issue OCPBUGS-74477. The following is the description of the original issue:   
—  
This is a clone of issue <custom data-type="smartlink" data-id="id-0">https://redhat.atlassian.net/browse/OCPBUGS-67303#icft=OCPBUGS-67303</custom>. The following is the description of the original issue:   
—  
Description of problem:

```java
- Incorrect AlertManagerConfig causing Monitoring Operator Degradation, Facing the below issue:
UpdatingUserWorkloadAlertmanager: waiting for Alertmanager User Workload object changes failed: waiting for Alertmanager openshift-user-workload-monitoring/user-workload: context deadline exceeded: expected 1 replicas, got 0 updated replicas

- When running OCP 4.16.50, experiencing a persistent, high-impact issue where a single user-defined AlertmanagerConfig that references a non-existent Kubernetes Secret causes the Prometheus Operator to fail configuration generation, blocking the reconciliation of all other valid AlertmanagerConfigs in the user-workload monitoring stack (openshift-user-workload-monitoring).
```

 

Version-Release number of selected component (if applicable):

```java
Openshift cluster version - 4.16.50 
   

This specific failure mode (**missing Secret**) persists even with the backported fix and in newer upstream Prometheus Operator versions (tested up to **0.85.0** upstream [here](https://gss--c.vf.force.com/apex/support#/cases/04323140/caseupdates/a0aHn00000a2B3ZIAU), linked to GitHub Issue **#7264** [here](https://gss--c.vf.force.com/apex/support#/cases/04323140/caseupdates/a0aHn00000a2AI4IAM)). The operator's Secret retrieval failure halts the entire configuration generation process, regardless of the URL validation fix.
```

How reproducible:

```java
Always
```

```java
YAML to use:

apiVersion: monitoring.coreos.com/v1beta1
kind: AlertmanagerConfig
metadata:
  name: example
  namespace: noodles
spec:
  route:
    groupBy:
    - namespace
    receiver: msteams
  receivers:
  - name: msteams
    msteamsConfigs:
    - webhookUrl: 
        key: url # 
        name: my-workflow-webhook # k8s secret name in same namespace as AlertManagerConfig
      sendResolved: true
      title: "mytitle"
      text: "mytext"
```

Steps to Reproduce:

```java
- Enable User Workload Monitoring
- Enable custom AlertManager configs
- Create a custom AlertManager config
- Check Prometheus Operator pod logs in User Workload Monitoring namespace to verify we see similar error
- Attempt to upgrade
```

Actual results:

```java
$ oc logs prometheus-operator-85fcd7664-xfstm | grep webhook | tail -n1
ts=2025-12-10T07:26:57.027512738Z level=error caller=/go/src/github.com/coreos

```java
IBM Cloud Infrastructure Service added the ability to create VPC Security Group Rules using additional protocols, one included is 'any'. Older IBM Cloud vpc-go-sdk versions are unable to process SG's and SG Rules and cause an error. The IBM Cloud MAPI component is using an old SDK, and is unable to create new Machines as a result.
```

Version-Release number of selected component (if applicable):

```java
4.19.21
```

How reproducible:

```java
100%
```

Steps to Reproduce:

```java
1. Create a new IPI deployed OCP cluster on IBM Cloud
    2. Using the VPC API's, via curl, create a new SecurityGroup Rule using 'any' protocol in the cluster's VPC
    3. Attempt to create a new MachineSet
```

Actual results:

```java
Machine gets stuck Provisioning
machine.machine.openshift.io/ocp-jenkins-30-48nrh-worker-us-east-failset-ps8gt   Provisioning                                    61s


MAPI logs:

E0107 16:59:33.554905       1 actuator.go:66] ocp-jenkins-30-48nrh-worker-us-east-failset-ps8gt error: ocp-jenkins-30-48nrh-worker-us-east-failset-ps8gt: reconciler failed to Create machine: failed to create instance via ibm vpc client: could not retrieve security group ids of names: map[ocp-jenkins-30-48nrh-sg-cluster-wide: ocp-jenkins-30-48nrh-sg-openshift-net:]
```

Expected results:

```java
New Machine is Provisioned and joins the cluster
```

Additional info:

```java
Added an 'any' SG Rule to the existing cluster's default VPC SG
% ibmcloud is sg-rules r014-4da3252a-b066-45f1-a8d1-e424971fea1b ocp-jenkins-30-48nrh-vpc -q                  ID                                          Direction   IP version   Remote                        Protocol       Local       Name    r014-ec486e2b-ad12-483e-8ba4-0ec2e151992f   outbound    ipv4         0.0.0.0/0                     icmp_tcp_udp   0.0.0.0/0   outbound-icmp-tcp-udp    r014-6823adb1-a49e-4d7a-aaa4-2e1205a77e94   inbound     ipv4         polio-stoke-lurex-surcharge   icmp_tcp_udp   0.0.0.0/0   inbound-icmp-tcp-udp-from-this-security-group    r014-25f3f7cb-f117-4d3d-a006-85ebf1395493   outbound    ipv4         0.0.0.0/0                     any            0.0.0.0/0   shamrock-reformer-crawfish-trimester



The latest vpc-go-sdk available, should contain a fix to prevent this issue
https://github.com/IBM/vpc-go-sdk/releases/tag/v0.78.1

It is possible a long term fix is provided by IBM Cloud Infrastructure team soon, but updating to the latest vpc-go-sdk is still desirable.
```

summary: [stub] CVE-2026-33186 openshift4/ose-insights-rhel9-operator: gRPC-Go: Authorization bypass due to improper HTTP/2 path validation [openshift-4.20]
description:
Security Tracking Issue

Do not make this issue public.

Flaw:

---

gRPC-Go: Authorization bypass due to improper HTTP/2 path validation

gRPC-Go is the Go language implementation of gRPC. Versions prior to 1.79.3 have an authorization bypass resulting from improper input validation of the HTTP/2 \`:path\` pseudo-header. The gRPC-Go server was too lenient in its routing logic, accepting requests where the \`:path\` omitted the mandatory leading slash (e.g., \`Service/Method\` instead of \`/Service/Method\`). While the server successfully routed these requests to the correct handler, authorization interceptors (including the official \`grpc/authz\` package) evaluated the raw, non-canonical path string. Consequently, "deny" rules defined using canonical paths (starting with \`/\`) failed to match the incoming request, allowing it to bypass the policy if a fallback "allow" rule was present. This affects gRPC-Go servers that use path-based authorization interceptors, such as the official RBAC implementation in \`google.golang.org/grpc/authz\` or custom interceptors relying on \`info.FullMethod\` or \`grpc.Method(ctx)\`; AND that have a security policy contains specific "deny" rules for canonical paths but allows other requests by default (a fallback "allow" rule). The vulnerability is exploitable by an attacker who can send raw HTTP/2 frames with malformed \`:path\` headers directly to the gRPC server. The fix in version 1.79.3 ensures that any request with a \`:path\` that does not start with a leading slash is immediately rejected with a \`codes.Unimplemented\` error, preventing it from reaching authorization interceptors or handlers with a non-canonical path string. While upgrading is the most secure and recommended path, users can mitigate the vulnerability using one of the following methods: Use a validating interceptor (recommended mitigation); infrastructure-level normalization; and/or policy hardening.

\~\~\~

The following link provides references to all essential vulnerability management information. If something is wrong or missing, please contact a member of PSIRT.  
[https://prodsec.pages.redhat.com/prodsec-docs/incident-response/Vulnerability_Management_Essentials/](https://prodsec.pages.redhat.com/prodsec-docs/incident-response/Vulnerability_Management_Essentials/)

Tracker accuracy feedback form: [https://docs.google.com/forms/d/e/1FAIpQLSfa6zTaEGohRdiIqGVAvWTSAL0kpO_DkkEICuIHzQHFwmKswg/viewform](https://docs.google.com/forms/d/e/1FAIpQLSfa6zTaEGohRdiIqGVAvWTSAL0kpO_DkkEICuIHzQHFwmKswg/viewform)

```

oc adm must-gather \
  --image=registry.redhat.io/multicluster-engine/must-gather-rhel9:v<mce_version> \
  /usr/bin/gather hosted-cluster-namespace=HOSTEDCLUSTERNAMESPACE \
  hosted-cluster-name=HOSTEDCLUSTERNAME \
  --dest-dir=NAME ; tar -cvzf NAME.tgz NAME

For OCP 4.22, this need to be updated to 

oc adm must-gather \
  --image=registry.redhat.io/rhacm2/acm-must-gather-rhel9:v2.17 \
  /usr/bin/gather hosted-cluster-namespace=HOSTEDCLUSTERNAMESPACE \
  hosted-cluster-name=HOSTEDCLUSTERNAME \

oc adm must-gather \

  /usr/bin/gather hosted-cluster-namespace=HOSTEDCLUSTERNAMESPACE \
  hosted-cluster-name=HOSTEDCLUSTERNAME \
  --dest-dir=NAME ; tar -cvzf NAME.tgz NAME

oc adm must-gather \

  /usr/bin/gather hosted-cluster-namespace=HOSTEDCLUSTERNAMESPACE \

oc adm must-gather \

  /usr/bin/gather hosted-cluster-namespace=HOSTEDCLUSTERNAMESPACE \

  --dest-dir=NAME ; tar -cvzf NAME.tgz NAME

Also, right under this section, remove:

You replace <mce_version> with the version of multicluster engine Operator that you are using; for example, 2.6.

summary: [release-4.19] New console cookies are generated without removing old ones. With a one-month duration, this can make the cookie header oversized due to multiple session tokens.
description:
This is a clone of issue <custom data-type="smartlink" data-id="id-0">https://redhat.atlassian.net/browse/OCPBUGS-78366#icft=OCPBUGS-78366</custom>. The following is the description of the original issue:   
—  
This is a clone of issue <custom data-type="smartlink" data-id="id-1">https://redhat.atlassian.net/browse/OCPBUGS-75000#icft=OCPBUGS-75000</custom>. The following is the description of the original issue:   
—  
This is a clone of issue <custom data-type="smartlink" data-id="id-2">https://redhat.atlassian.net/browse/OCPBUGS-65967#icft=OCPBUGS-65967</custom>. The following is the description of the original issue:   
—  
IHAC whose Cookie Header Length Exceeds 4096 Bytes in OpenShift Console

```java
Even though the session cookies (openshift-session-token-console-xxxxx-yyyyy) are valid for 1 month, new ones are generated after logging out and back in (often after overnight logins). This happens because Azure AD issues a new authentication token each time a new SSO session is established, and OpenShift maps that to a new session cookie instead of reusing an old one.
Logout behavior: When logging out, cookies are correctly expired and removed, but after re-login, some previously issued cookies may reappear. This can occur due to browser-level caching or Azure AD session persistence — the console may reuse stored authentication state or refresh tokens, causing OpenShift to restore old session metadata temporarily.
In short:
New cookies are expected when Azure AD starts a new SSO session. Residual cookies seen after logout usually result from cached tokens or session restoration by Azure AD or the OpenShift OAuth proxy.
```

summary: Start-up race between OVS and Corosync can cause unnecessary fencing
description:
# Description of Problem

**CONFIRMED in Run 2 with precise timestamps. Mechanism corrected via adversarial review (2026-04-01).**

On reboot after fencing, `configure-ovs.sh` creates `br-ex` and moves `enp2s0` from standalone NIC to OVS port. This strips the corosync address (`fd2e:..::14`) from `enp2s0`. The address must be re-acquired via DHCPv6 on the new `br-ex` interface. During the \~1.6s gap, knet detects the link as down (within 84ms of the interface state change), the 3000ms TOTEM token timeout fires, and the surviving node fences master-0 again — creating a reboot loop.

**Previous description was wrong**: the CLAUDE.md previously said "`del-br br-ex` kills the corosync NIC." In fact, `br-ex` does NOT exist during the reboot cycle — `del-br` is a no-op (`--if-exists`). The disruptive operation is bridge **creation**, not deletion.

## Boot-order race

1. Boot: corosync starts, knet link comes up on `enp2s0` (standalone NIC with `::14`), node joins cluster
2. `configure-ovs.sh` creates `br-ex`, moves `enp2s0` to OVS port → address `::14` stripped from `enp2s0`
3. **84ms later**: knet detects link down (no address on underlying interface)
4. **\~1.6s later**: DHCPv6 assigns `::14` to `br-ex` — but TOTEM token warning already fired at 2250ms
5. **\~3s later**: TOTEM token timeout (3000ms) → surviving node fences master-0 → reboot → loop

## Run 2 precise timeline (boot 2)

| Time | Event |
| --- | --- |
| 06:11:18 | Corosync joins cluster on `enp2s0` (standalone, addr `::14`) |
| 06:11:34 | `configure-ovs.sh` starts |
| 06:11:37.338 | `br-ex` bridge activated, `enp2s0` becomes OVS port |
| 06:11:38.421 | NM starts IP config on `br-ex` interface (addr not yet assigned) |
| **06:11:38.505** | **KNET: host 2 link 0 is DOWN** (84ms after interface state change) |
| 06:11:39.147 | TOTEM: "Token has not been received in 2250ms" |
| 06:11:39.897 | TOTEM: "token timed out (3000ms)" |
| 06:11:40.008 | DHCPv6 on `br-ex` gets address `::14` — **too late** |
| 06:11:43.499 | Membership: node left — master-0 fenced again |

## All 8 boots in Run 2 — complete causal chain

| Boot | Kernel boot | Corosync join (m1) | configure_ovs | br-ex created | knet DOWN | br-ex→knet gap | Outcome |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 1 | 06:09:48 | — | — | — | — | — | Expected fence |
| 2 | 06:10:08 | 06:11:18 | 06:11:34 | 06:11:37 | **06:11:38** | **1.2s** | Fenced again |
| 3 | 06:12:03 | 06:13:10 | — | — | — | — | Fenced before configure-ovs |
| 4 | 06:13:34 | 06:14:42 | 06:14:59 | 06:15:01 | **06:15:02** | **1.1s** | Fenced again |
| 5 | 06:15:28 | 06:15:40 | 06:15:57 | 06:16:00 | **06:16:00** | **0.6s** | Fenced again |
| 6 | 06:16:24 | 06:16:36 | — | — | — | — | Fenced before configure-ovs |
| 7 | 06:17:33 | 06:17:44 | 06:18:02 | 06:18:05 | **06:18:05** | **0.7s** | Fenced again |
| 8 | 06:18:29 | 06:18:41 | 06:19:00 | 06:19:02 | **06:19:03** | **0.8s** | **Loop breaks** (rejoins at 06:19:08) |

**Pattern**: Every boot where `configure-ovs.sh`

summary: vmware-vsphere-csi-driver daemonset emits pathological SuccessfulCreate events (regression after OCPBUGS-65674)
description:
## Description of problem

The vmware-vsphere-csi-driver-node daemonset is creating pods excessively, generating pathological SuccessfulCreate events (27-29 repetitions per pod). This is a regression that returned after <custom data-type="smartlink" data-id="id-0">https://redhat.atlassian.net/browse/OCPBUGS-65674#icft=OCPBUGS-65674</custom> was marked as Verified.

This is the exact same failure pattern that was supposedly fixed in <custom data-type="smartlink" data-id="id-1">https://redhat.atlassian.net/browse/OCPBUGS-65674#icft=OCPBUGS-65674</custom>. The bug was verified on March 25, 2026, but the regression re-appeared on March 29, 2026 (only 4 days later), indicating the fix did not permanently resolve the issue.

## Test Name

```java
[Monitor:legacy-test-framework-invariants-pathological][sig-arch] events should not repeat pathologically for ns/openshift-cluster-csi-drivers
```

## Test ID

openshift-tests:30ac23dcd7037e581ed41a85fad97ecf

## Regression ID

37244

## Version-Release number

4.22

## Regression Timeline

* Regression Opened: March 30, 2026
* First Failure: March 29, 2026 at 05:59:10 UTC
* Last Failure: April 1, 2026
* Previous Bug (<custom data-type="smartlink" data-id="id-2">https://redhat.atlassian.net/browse/OCPBUGS-65674#icft=OCPBUGS-65674</custom>) Verified: March 25, 2026

## How reproducible

Always (on periodic-ci-openshift-release-main-nightly-4.22-e2e-vsphere-ovn-serial since March 29)

## Affected Variants

* Platform: vsphere
* Network: ovn
* Installer: ipi
* Suite: serial
* Topology: ha
* Architecture: amd64

## Focused Analysis: periodic-ci-openshift-release-main-nightly-4.22-e2e-vsphere-ovn-serial

Failure Pattern (last 60 runs, newest to oldest):

```java
FFFFFFFFFFFFFFSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSFSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSFSSSSS
```

* 13 consecutive failures starting March 29, 2026
* Classification: Recent Permafail - was passing, now consistently failing
* Pattern indicates a clear regression starting at a specific point in time

```
The alertmanager is including a Source URL that points to /monitoring/graph for example

while correct would be

summary: CLONE - CVE-2026-33186 openshift4/ose-openstack-cluster-api-controllers-rhel9: gRPC-Go: Authorization bypass due to improper HTTP/2 path validation [openshift-4.16.z]
description:
Security Tracking Issue

Do not make this issue public.

Flaw:

---

gRPC-Go: Authorization bypass due to improper HTTP/2 path validation

gRPC-Go is the Go language implementation of gRPC. Versions prior to 1.79.3 have an authorization bypass resulting from improper input validation of the HTTP/2 \`:path\` pseudo-header. The gRPC-Go server was too lenient in its routing logic, accepting requests where the \`:path\` omitted the mandatory leading slash (e.g., \`Service/Method\` instead of \`/Service/Method\`). While the server successfully routed these requests to the correct handler, authorization interceptors (including the official \`grpc/authz\` package) evaluated the raw, non-canonical path string. Consequently, "deny" rules defined using canonical paths (starting with \`/\`) failed to match the incoming request, allowing it to bypass the policy if a fallback "allow" rule was present. This affects gRPC-Go servers that use path-based authorization interceptors, such as the official RBAC implementation in \`google.golang.org/grpc/authz\` or custom interceptors relying on \`info.FullMethod\` or \`grpc.Method(ctx)\`; AND that have a security policy contains specific "deny" rules for canonical paths but allows other requests by default (a fallback "allow" rule). The vulnerability is exploitable by an attacker who can send raw HTTP/2 frames with malformed \`:path\` headers directly to the gRPC server. The fix in version 1.79.3 ensures that any request with a \`:path\` that does not start with a leading slash is immediately rejected with a \`codes.Unimplemented\` error, preventing it from reaching authorization interceptors or handlers with a non-canonical path string. While upgrading is the most secure and recommended path, users can mitigate the vulnerability using one of the following methods: Use a validating interceptor (recommended mitigation); infrastructure-level normalization; and/or policy hardening.

\~\~\~

The following link provides references to all essential vulnerability management information. If something is wrong or missing, please contact a member of PSIRT.  
[https://prodsec.pages.redhat.com/prodsec-docs/incident-response/Vulnerability_Management_Essentials/](https://prodsec.pages.redhat.com/prodsec-docs/incident-response/Vulnerability_Management_Essentials/)

summary: CVE-2026-35092 rhcos: Corosync: Denial of Service via integer overflow in join message validation [openshift-4.19]
description:
Security Tracking Issue

Do not make this issue public.

Flaw:

---

Corosync: Denial of Service via integer overflow in join message validation

Integer overflow (wraparound) vulnerability in Corosync’s join message sanity validation in exec/totemsrp.c. The flaw is in check_memb_join_sanity(), where proc_list_entries and failed_list_entries are attacker-controlled 32-bit unsigned values received from the network and are added together before being promoted to size_t. This allows the addition (proc_list_entries + failed_list_entries) to wrap around in 32-bit arithmetic (e.g., 0x80000000 + 0x80000000 = 0), causing required_len to be calculated too small and allowing a short packet to pass validation. As a result, Corosync proceeds with processing malformed input that should have been rejected, which can be exploited remotely without authentication in totemudp/totemudpu mode via crafted UDP packets to crash the service, resulting in a denial of service.

\~\~\~

The following link provides references to all essential vulnerability management information. If something is wrong or missing, please contact a member of PSIRT.  
[https://prodsec.pages.redhat.com/prodsec-docs/incident-response/Vulnerability_Management_Essentials/](https://prodsec.pages.redhat.com/prodsec-docs/incident-response/Vulnerability_Management_Essentials/)

summary: CVE-2026-35091 rhcos: Corosync: Denial of Service and information disclosure via crafted UDP packet [openshift-4.19]
description:
Security Tracking Issue

Do not make this issue public.

Flaw:

---

Corosync: Denial of Service and information disclosure via crafted UDP packet

Wrong return value vulnerability in the Corosync membership commit token sanity check in exec/totemsrp.c. The flaw occurs in check_memb_commit_token_sanity() where truncated messages (msg_len < sizeof(struct memb_commit_token)) incorrectly return 0 (success) instead of -1 (failure). As a result, message_handler_memb_commit_token() continues processing attacker-controlled, undersized input, performs an allocation based on the short length, and then accesses struct memb_commit_token fields beyond the allocated region, triggering an out-of-bounds read (ASAN-confirmed). This can be exploited remotely without authentication in totemudp/totemudpu mode by sending a single crafted UDP packet to the Corosync port (default 5405), causing a denial of service and potentially leaking limited memory contents.

\~\~\~

The following link provides references to all essential vulnerability management information. If something is wrong or missing, please contact a member of PSIRT.  
[https://prodsec.pages.redhat.com/prodsec-docs/incident-response/Vulnerability_Management_Essentials/](https://prodsec.pages.redhat.com/prodsec-docs/incident-response/Vulnerability_Management_Essentials/)

summary: RHCOS fails to boot with Secure Boot enabled due to kernel-5.14.0-570.105.1.el9_6 signed with new CA8 certificate
description:
## Summary

RHCOS builds using kernel-5.14.0-570.105.1.el9_6 fail to boot when UEFI Secure Boot is enabled. This is blocking nightlies for OCP 4.19, 4.20, and 4.21.

The kernel is signed with a new certificate (Red Hat Secure Boot CA 8 / cert 801) instead of the previous certificate (Red Hat Secure Boot CA 5 / cert 501). This change was not coordinated with OpenShift/RHCOS.

## Impact

* **Nightlies blocked**: OCP 4.19/4.20/4.21 nightlies have been stalled for 2+ days
* **driver-toolkit mismatch**: driver-toolkit was built with kernel-570.105.1, but RHCOS cannot use this kernel, causing FAILED_CONSISTENCY_REQUIREMENT violations
* **Potential cluster bricking**: If this kernel were shipped, existing clusters with Secure Boot enabled could fail to boot after update

## Root Cause

The RHEL kernel team changed the Secure Boot signing certificate from CA5 (501) to CA8 (801) starting with kernel-5.14.0-570.105.1.el9_6 as part of migration from 2K to 4K certificates ([RHEL-129223](https://redhat.atlassian.net/browse/RHEL-129223)).

While the shim-15.8-4.el9_3 used in RHCOS technically trusts both CA5 and CA8, there appears to be an additional issue preventing kernel-105 from booting even when the shim should accept the signature.

### Console Error (basic.uefi-secure kola test)

```java
BdsDxe: loading Boot0002 "UEFI Misc Device" from PciRoot\(0x0\)/Pci\(0x2,0x0\)
BdsDxe: starting Boot0002 "UEFI Misc Device" from PciRoot\(0x0\)/Pci\(0x2,0x0\)

GRUB version 2.06

Booting `Red Hat Enterprise Linux CoreOS 9.6.20260331\-0 \(Plow\) \(ostree:0\)'

error: ../../grub\-core/kern/efi/sb.c:182:bad shim signature.
error: ../../grub\-core/loader/i386/efi/linux.c:258:you need to load the kernel first.

Press any key to continue...
```

### Kernel Signing Details

| **Kernel**  | **Signing Certificate**  | **Status**  |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 5.14.0-570.104.1.el9_6  | cert 501 (Red Hat Secure Boot CA 5)  | Working  |
| 5.14.0-570.105.1.el9_6  | cert 801 (Red Hat Secure Boot CA 8)  | _BROKEN_  |

## Short-term Mitigation

1. _Untag kernel-105_ from early-kernel-candidate and rhaos-4.1x-rhel-9-candidate tags
2. _Rebuild driver-toolkit_ against kernel-104 to resolve the consistency requirement violation
3. Consider using _kernel-stop-ship_ mechanism if available

## Long-term Resolution

Before CA8-signed kernels can be used in RHCOS/OCP:

1. _Enable bootupd automatic updates_ in RHCOS ([COS-4048](https://redhat.atlassian.net/browse/COS-4048), [BIFROST-1165](https://redhat.atlassian.net/browse/BIFROST-1165))

    * Nodes must update their shim/bootloader before receiving CA8-signed kernels
    * Without this, existing clusters with Secure Boot could be bricked on update
    
2. _Coordinate certificate changes_ with OpenShift/RHCOS team

    * Changes to Secure Boot signing certificates need advance notice
    * Ensure proper rollout sequence: bootloader update → new kernel signature
    
3. _Investigate why kernel-105 fails_ even though shim trusts CA8

    * Other CA8-s

summary: Update FIPS mode verification documentation for RHCOS 10 - fips-mode-setup no longer available
description:
## Summary

The `fips-mode-setup --check` command is no longer available in RHCOS 10 (based on RHEL 10). Documentation needs to be updated to reflect the new method for checking FIPS mode status.

## Background

The `fips-mode-setup` tool has been removed from RHEL 10 as per \[RHEL-65652|[https://redhat.atlassian.net/browse/RHEL-65652](https://redhat.atlassian.net/browse/RHEL-65652)\]. Users attempting to use the previous method will encounter an error:

```java
$ oc debug node/$node\_name \-\- chroot /host fips\-mode\-setup \-\-check
chroot: failed to run command fips\-mode\-setup : No such file or directory
```

## Proposed Documentation Update

Add the following information to the RHCOS 10 documentation:

_Checking FIPS Mode Status in RHCOS 10_

In RHCOS 10, the `fips-mode-setup` tool is no longer available. To check if FIPS mode is enabled, verify the contents of `/proc/sys/crypto/fips_enabled`:

```java
$ oc debug node/$node\_name \-\- chroot /host cat /proc/sys/crypto/fips\_enabled
```

* Output of `1` indicates FIPS mode is _enabled_
* Output of `0` indicates FIPS mode is _disabled_

## References

* \[RHEL-65652: Remove fips-mode-setup|[https://redhat.atlassian.net/browse/RHEL-65652](https://redhat.atlassian.net/browse/RHEL-65652)\]
* \[RHEL 10.0 Release Notes|[https://docs.redhat.com/en/documentation/red_hat_enterprise_linux/10/html-single/10.0_release_notes/index#Jira-RHEL-65652](https://docs.redhat.com/en/documentation/red_hat_enterprise_linux/10/html-single/10.0_release_notes/index#Jira-RHEL-65652)\]
* \[RHEL 10 Security Hardening: Switching RHEL to FIPS mode|[https://docs.redhat.com/en/documentation/red_hat_enterprise_linux/10/html/security_hardening/switching-rhel-to-fips-mode](https://docs.redhat.com/en/documentation/red_hat_enterprise_linux/10/html/security_hardening/switching-rhel-to-fips-mode)\]

## Additional Notes

* FIPS mode enablement is determined by the `fips=1` boot option on the kernel command line
* The system-wide cryptographic policy automatically follows this setting
* In RHEL 10/RHCOS 10, switching a system to FIPS mode after installation is not supported - FIPS must be enabled during installation

summary: [Assisted Installer] Installation of OCP cluster with tang encryption enabled not working with DASD disks
description:
**Description of problem:**

```
When trying OCP installation with DASD disks and tang encryption enabled, installation is failing
```

**Version-Release number of selected component (if applicable):**

```
4.22.0-ec.3
```

**How reproducible:**

```
Always
```

**Steps to Reproduce:**

```
1. From Assisted Installer GUI, create a cluster with tang encryption enabled(Select "Encryption of installation disks" -> Select "Tang" -> Provide tang server URL and thumbprint)
2. Generate the iPXE artifacts and boot the nodes with DASD as installation disk.
3. Once the host discovery is complete, continue with the installation. 
```

**Actual results:**

```
When control node is rebooting during installation, it is going into emergency shell and installation is failing.
```

**Expected results:**

```
Control nodes are rebooted successfully and installation completes.
```

**Additional info:**

summary: ironic-proxy DaemonSet pods enter CrashLoopBackOff due to Read-only filesystem; need to enforce privileged SCC via annotation.
description:
**Description of problem:**

```
After upgrading to OpenShift 4.20.8, ironic-proxy DaemonSet pods in the openshift-machine-api namespace enter CrashLoopBackOff with "read-only file system" errors.

The pods are assigned a non-default SCC (pure-csi-node-plugin-scc) created by the Portworx Enterprise Operator, which enforces a read-only root filesystem. This conflicts with ironic-proxy requirements and prevents normal operation.

Adding the openshift.io/required-scc: privileged annotation ensures the correct SCC is used and resolves the issue.
```

**Version-Release number of selected component (if applicable):**

```
‌
```

**How reproducible:**

```
Always
```

**Steps to Reproduce:**

```
1. Deploy an OpenShift cluster.
2. Install the Portworx Enterprise Operator which introduces the pure-csi-node-plugin-scc.
3. Observe the status of the ironic-proxy pods in the openshift-machine-api namespace.
```

**Actual results:**

```
1. ironic-proxy pods are in CrashLoopBackOff.
2. Pod metadata shows it is bound to openshift.io/scc: pure-csi-node-plugin-scc.
3. Container logs show failures related to writing to a read-only filesystem.
```

**Expected results:**

```
1. ironic-proxy pods should successfully bind to a privileged SCC.
2. Pods of ironic-proxy daemonset in the openshift-machine-api should be in running state.
```

**Additional info:**

```
‌
```

summary: Intermittent Pipeline Failure: Init Container "prepare" exits with Code 255 due to CRI-O/Process.
description:
**Description of problem:**

````
Tekton PipelineRuns are intermittently failing during the initialization phase. The taskrun status reports: init container failed, "prepare" exited with code 255.
```
pipelinerun:
status:
          artifacts: {}
          completionTime: '2026-03-13T14:18:53Z'
          conditions:
            - lastTransitionTime: '2026-03-13T14:18:53Z'
              message: 'init container failed, "prepare" exited with code 255'
              reason: Failed
              status: 'False'
              type: Succeeded
          podName: dpf-python-airflow-dee19500280201c97cb6996bebcf0750d5031b25-pod
```
```
taskrun:
status:
  artifacts: {}
  completionTime: '2026-03-13T14:18:53Z'
  conditions:
    - lastTransitionTime: '2026-03-13T14:18:53Z'
      message: 'init container failed, "prepare" exited with code 255'
      reason: Failed
      status: 'False'
      type: Succeeded
  podName: dpf-python-airflow-dee19500280201c97cb6996bebcf0750d5031b25-pod
```
Investigation indicates that that it's not a Tekton Pipelines issue and it's not resource pressure (the node shows no memory/disk pressure at all). 
It's a CRI-O bug.
```
Mar 23 10:15:19 crio[3016]: level=error msg="Failed to update container state for a0b136050c2701d377272f6f452834a64e67266da921800e3ca02a363d2a67ad: stdout: , stderr: "
```
What might be happening is that Tekton's init containers (prepare, place-scripts) finish their work in milliseconds so they just copy a binary and write a script. They're so fast that CRI-O can't capture the exit code before the process is already gone. When that happens, CRI-O reports exit code 255. The Tekton code actually completed successfully and the container log "Entrypoint initialization" is the success message.

This causes non-deterministic pipeline failures in high-performance environments, requiring manual retries despite the underlying task logic being sound.
````

```

```

summary: AWSEndpointService can have duplicate SubnetIDs causing AWS VPC Endpoint reconciliation failures
description:
## Problem

 The `AWSEndpointService` CRD's `Spec.SubnetIDs` field lacks a `+listType=set` marker, so the API server does not enforce uniqueness. If duplicate subnet IDs are present (from    
  manual edits, older resources, or server-side apply merges), the control-plane-operator passes them directly to AWS `CreateVpcEndpoint` and `ModifyVpcEndpoint` calls, which could fail because AWS rejects duplicate subnet IDs.                                                                                                                                  

## Root Cause

* `api/hypershift/v1beta1/endpointservice_types.go` — `SubnetIDs []string` has no `+listType=set`, so the CRD allows duplicates.                                                
* `control-plane-operator/controllers/awsprivatelink/awsprivatelink_controller.go` — `reconcileAWSEndpointService()` passes `Spec.SubnetIDs` directly to AWS APIs without  
  deduplication. The `diffIDs()` function also propagates duplicates from the desired list into `AddSubnetIds`.                                                                   

## Fix

1. Add `+listType=set` to the `SubnetIDs` field in the CRD so the API server rejects duplicates on new writes.                                                                  
2. Defensively deduplicate `SubnetIDs` in the CPO's `reconcileAWSEndpointService()` before calling AWS APIs, protecting against pre-existing resources that already have  
  duplicates stored.                                                                                                                                                              
3. Remove the corresponding linter exclusion in `api/.golangci.yml`.

## Notes

_Technically_ this can’t happen based on how the control-plane-operator is diffing security groups to add or remove. The `ModifyVPCEndpoint` API supports passing a list, even though we pass a list of length 1.

Coding defensively in case we ever change behavior to add or remove more than one subnets at a time to ensure those aren’t duplicate IDs is the intent of this bug.
