summary: CVE-2026-33997 openshift4/ose-agent-installer-api-server-rhel9: Moby: Privilege validation bypass during plugin installation [openshift-4.21]
description:
Security Tracking Issue

Do not make this issue public.

Flaw:

---

Moby: Privilege validation bypass during plugin installation

Moby is an open source container framework. Prior to version 29.3.1, a security vulnerability has been detected that allows plugins privilege validation to be bypassed during docker plugin install. Due to an error in the daemon's privilege comparison logic, the daemon may incorrectly accept a privilege set that differs from the one approved by the user. Plugins that request exactly one privilege are also affected, because no comparison is performed at all. This issue has been patched in version 29.3.1.

\~\~\~

summary: CVE-2026-33997 openshift4/ose-console: Moby: Privilege validation bypass during plugin installation [openshift-4.12.z]
description:
Security Tracking Issue

Do not make this issue public.

Flaw:

---

Moby: Privilege validation bypass during plugin installation

Moby is an open source container framework. Prior to version 29.3.1, a security vulnerability has been detected that allows plugins privilege validation to be bypassed during docker plugin install. Due to an error in the daemon's privilege comparison logic, the daemon may incorrectly accept a privilege set that differs from the one approved by the user. Plugins that request exactly one privilege are also affected, because no comparison is performed at all. This issue has been patched in version 29.3.1.

summary: CVE-2026-33186 openshift4/ose-vsphere-csi-driver-syncer-rhel9: gRPC-Go: Authorization bypass due to improper HTTP/2 path validation [openshift-4.22]
description:
Security Tracking Issue

Do not make this issue public.

Flaw:

---

gRPC-Go: Authorization bypass due to improper HTTP/2 path validation

gRPC-Go is the Go language implementation of gRPC. Versions prior to 1.79.3 have an authorization bypass resulting from improper input validation of the HTTP/2 \`:path\` pseudo-header. The gRPC-Go server was too lenient in its routing logic, accepting requests where the \`:path\` omitted the mandatory leading slash (e.g., \`Service/Method\` instead of \`/Service/Method\`). While the server successfully routed these requests to the correct handler, authorization interceptors (including the official \`grpc/authz\` package) evaluated the raw, non-canonical path string. Consequently, "deny" rules defined using canonical paths (starting with \`/\`) failed to match the incoming request, allowing it to bypass the policy if a fallback "allow" rule was present. This affects gRPC-Go servers that use path-based authorization interceptors, such as the official RBAC implementation in \`google.golang.org/grpc/authz\` or custom interceptors relying on \`info.FullMethod\` or \`grpc.Method(ctx)\`; AND that have a security policy contains specific "deny" rules for canonical paths but allows other requests by default (a fallback "allow" rule). The vulnerability is exploitable by an attacker who can send raw HTTP/2 frames with malformed \`:path\` headers directly to the gRPC server. The fix in version 1.79.3 ensures that any request with a \`:path\` that does not start with a leading slash is immediately rejected with a \`codes.Unimplemented\` error, preventing it from reaching authorization interceptors or handlers with a non-canonical path string. While upgrading is the most secure and recommended path, users can mitigate the vulnerability using one of the following methods: Use a validating interceptor (recommended mitigation); infrastructure-level normalization; and/or policy hardening.

\~\~\~

[https://prodsec.pages.redhat.com/prodsec-docs/incident-response/Vulnerability_Management_Essentials/](https://prodsec.pages.redhat.com/prodsec-docs/incident-response/Vulnerability_Management_Essentials/)

summary: Dashboard watch consolidation and missing memoization cause excessive re-renders
description:
## Description

The Cluster Dashboard opens **8-15+ independent K8s WebSocket watches** across its cards, each triggering Redux state updates that cascade re-renders through unmemoized card components. With 15 watches and the WSFactory flushing every 500ms, this produces \~30 Redux updates/second — each potentially re-rendering the entire dashboard.

## Root Cause

**1. Excessive independent watches:**

| Card | Watches |
| --- | --- |
| StatusCard  | 1 ClusterVersion + 3-8 health subsystem watches  |
| InventoryCard  | 5-10 watches (Pod, Node, PV, NetworkPolicy, VolumeSnapshot + extensions)  |
| ActivityCard  | 1 Events watch + N extension-based activity watches  |
| UtilizationCard  | 1 MachineConfigPool watch  |
| DetailsCard  | 1 ClusterVersion watch  |

The Project Dashboard is worse: 15-25 watches per namespace (adds Deployment, DeploymentConfig, StatefulSet, Service, Route, ConfigMap, Secret, ResourceQuota, etc.).

**2. Missing memoization on cards:**

`UtilizationCard` and `InventoryCard` are not wrapped in `React.memo`. When any watch updates Redux state, these cards re-render even if their specific data hasn't changed. Health item arrays in `StatusCard` are rebuilt every render.

Note: `ActivityCard` IS properly memoized. `StatusCard` uses Redux `connect()` which provides implicit memoization.

**3. No lazy-loading of below-fold cards:**

All cards start their watches immediately on mount, even if the card is below the visible fold. Cards like ActivityCard and UtilizationCard could defer watch creation until scrolled into view.

## Impact

* \~30 Redux updates/second with 15 active watches, each cascading through unmemoized components
* Visible UI jank on the dashboard, especially on large clusters
* Unnecessary resource consumption for cards not currently visible

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Deploy a cluster with many workloads and operators
2. Open browser DevTools > Performance tab
3. Navigate to **Home > Overview** (Cluster Dashboard)
4. Record a 10-second performance profile
5. Observe frequent re-renders of dashboard card components triggered by unrelated watch updates

## Proposed Fix

1. **Consolidate inventory watches** — use `useK8sWatchResources` (plural) to batch multiple watches into a single selector, reducing Redux update cascades
2. **Wrap cards in** `React.memo` — add `React.memo` to `UtilizationCard`, `InventoryCard`, and `DetailsCard` to prevent cascade re-renders when unrelated watches update
3. **Lazy-load below-fold cards** — use Intersection Observer to defer mounting cards (and starting watches) until they scroll into view

## Relevant Code

| File | Description |
| --- | --- |
| `frontend/public/components/dashboard/dashboards-page/cluster-dashboard/utilization-card.tsx:260`  | UtilizationCard — not memoized  |
| `frontend/public/components/dashboard/dashboards-page/cluster-dashboard/inventory-card.tsx:94`  | InventoryCard — not memoized  |
| `frontend/public/components/dashboard/dashboards-page/cluster-dashboard/

summary: Search page state mutation bug and unnecessary component remounts on filter change
description:
## Description

The `SearchPage_` component (`frontend/public/components/search.tsx`) has a direct state mutation bug that can cause stale UI state, and a component key strategy that forces all `ResourceList` components to unmount and remount on every filter change — restarting WebSocket watches and causing CPU spikes.

## Root Cause

**1. State mutation bug** (lines 134-142):

```typescript
const updateSelectedItems = (selection: string) => {
  const updateItems = selectedItems;  // reference, not a copy!
  updateItems.has(selection)
    ? updateItems.delete(selection)   // mutates state directly
    : updateItems.add(selection);
  setSelectedItems(updateItems);
};
```

`const updateItems = selectedItems` copies the Set reference, not the value. `.delete()` and `.add()` mutate the original state in place. React may not detect the change since the object reference is the same. The same bug exists at lines 144-149.

**2. Component key strategy forces remounts** (line 346):

```typescript
key={`${resource}-${labelFilter.join(',')}-${debouncedNameFilter}`}
```

Including filter values in the component key forces all `ResourceList` components to unmount and remount when any filter changes. This tears down and recreates WebSocket watches, causing:

* CPU spikes from watch churn
* Redundant LIST requests to re-fetch data that was just discarded
* Visible UI flicker

**3. No memoization** (lines 87-362):

`SearchPage_` has zero `React.memo`, `useMemo`, or `useCallback` usage. All event handlers are recreated on every render, causing cascading child re-renders.

## Impact

* Stale or inconsistent UI state when selecting/deselecting resources
* CPU spikes on every filter change due to watch teardown/recreation
* Unnecessary network requests from restarted watches

## Steps to Reproduce

**State mutation bug:**

1. Navigate to **Home > Search**
2. Select multiple resource types
3. Rapidly select and deselect resources
4. Observe that the selection state may become inconsistent (items appear selected but aren't, or vice versa)

**Remount churn:**

1. Navigate to **Home > Search**
2. Select several resource types so multiple ResourceList panels are visible
3. Open browser DevTools > Network tab
4. Type in the Name filter field
5. Observe new LIST requests for every keystroke as ResourceList components remount

## Proposed Fix

1. **Fix state mutation** — create a new Set before mutating: `const updateItems = new Set(selectedItems);`
2. **Use stable component keys** — use resource kind only (e.g., `key={resource}`) instead of embedding filter values. Pass filters as props so ResourceList can update without remounting.
3. **Add memoization** — wrap event handlers in `useCallback` and computed values in `useMemo`

## Relevant Code

| File | Description |
| --- | --- |
| `frontend/public/components/search.tsx:134-142`  | State mutation bug in `updateSelectedItems`  |
| `frontend/public/components/search.tsx:144-149`  | Same mutation bug in `updateNewItems`  |
|

summary: Search page resource dropdown is unusable on clusters with 1000+ API resources
description:
## Description

The **Search** page resource type dropdown becomes extremely slow on clusters with 1000+ API resources. Typing in the Resources field causes multi-second delays with 100% CPU utilization on the client. The dropdown takes several seconds to open and filter results.

## Root Cause

Multiple compounding issues in `ResourceListDropdown` (`frontend/public/components/resource-dropdown.tsx`):

1. **O(n²) model filtering** (lines 65-90): The component computes available resources using a nested `.filter()` + `.find()` on `allModels`, producing \~1,000,000 comparisons on a cluster with 1000+ API resources:

```typescript
const resources = allModels
  .filter(({ apiGroup, apiVersion, kind, verbs }) => {
    const sameGroupKind = (m) => m.kind === kind && m.apiGroup === apiGroup && m.apiVersion !== apiVersion;
    return !allModels.find((m) => sameGroupKind(m) && preferred(m)); // O(n) inside O(n)
  })
  .toOrderedMap()
  .sortBy(({ kind, apiGroup }) => `${kind} ${apiGroup}`);
```

2. **No debounce on filter** (lines 132-153): A `useEffect` fires on every keystroke performing a linear scan of all 1000+ resources with `String.toLowerCase().includes()`.
3. **Redux connector triggers excessive re-renders** (lines 484-491): The component is connected via `allModels: k8s.getIn(['RESOURCES', 'models'])`. Any K8s state change (pod update, namespace change, etc.) triggers a re-render. On a cluster with 3000+ pods under active watches, this means dozens of re-renders per second.
4. **No virtualization**: The dropdown renders all 1000+ resource items in the DOM without virtualization.

## Impact

* Multi-second delays when typing in the Resources field
* 100% CPU utilization on the client during dropdown interaction
* Search page is effectively unusable on large clusters

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Deploy a cluster with many operators installed (`oc api-resources | wc -l` > 1000)
2. Navigate to **Home > Search**
3. Click the Resources dropdown
4. Start typing a resource name
5. Observe multi-second delays between keystrokes

## Proposed Fix

1. **Eliminate O(n²) filtering** — precompute a `Map<groupKind, preferredModel>` once, then use O(1) lookups instead of nested `.filter()` + `.find()`
2. **Add 200-300ms debounce** on the filter `useEffect`
3. **Optimize Redux selector** — use `createSelector` (reselect) to prevent re-renders on unrelated Redux state changes
4. **Virtualize the dropdown list** — use `react-window` or PatternFly virtualized select for 1000+ items

## Relevant Code

| File | Description |
| --- | --- |
| `frontend/public/components/resource-dropdown.tsx:65-90`  | O(n²) model filtering logic  |
| `frontend/public/components/resource-dropdown.tsx:132-153`  | Undebounced filter `useEffect`  |
| `frontend/public/components/resource-dropdown.tsx:484-491`  | Redux connector causing excessive re-renders  |

summary: OpenAPI v2 spec (~50MB+) re-downloaded without conditional requests on large clusters
description:
## Description

The console fetches the full OpenAPI v2 schema from `/api/kubernetes/openapi/v2` at application startup, after every API discovery cycle, and on a **5-minute polling interval** -- without using HTTP conditional request headers (`ETag` / `If-None-Match` / `If-Modified-Since`). On clusters with many CRDs (1000+ API resources), the OpenAPI spec grows to **50MB+**, resulting in repeated full downloads of an unchanged payload.

## Root Cause

The `fetchSwagger()` function (`frontend/public/module/k8s/swagger.ts:28-47`) performs a plain `coFetchJSON('api/kubernetes/openapi/v2')` call:

```typescript
export const fetchSwagger = async (): Promise<SwaggerDefinitions> => {
  localStorage.removeItem(SWAGGER_LOCAL_STORAGE_KEY);
  try {
    const response: SwaggerAPISpec = await coFetchJSON('api/kubernetes/openapi/v2');
    // ...
  }
};
```

A comment on line 29 states _"we rely on the browser cache now"_, but `coFetchJSON` does not set conditional request headers, so the browser cache likely cannot prevent a full re-download.

This function is called in three places:

1. **App startup** -- `updateSwaggerDefinitionContinual()` in `frontend/public/components/app.tsx:441-452` calls `fetchSwagger()` immediately.
2. **5-minute polling** -- The same function sets a `setInterval` to re-fetch every 5 minutes.
3. **After API discovery** -- `frontend/public/actions/k8s.ts:44` calls `fetchSwagger()` after resource discovery completes, which also runs periodically.

On a cluster with 1000+ API resources (common when many operators are installed), the OpenAPI v2 spec is 50MB+. Each fetch involves:

* Full network transfer of the 50MB+ JSON payload
* Parsing the entire JSON document in the browser
* Storing the result in memory

## Impact

* \~50MB+ network transfer on every fetch (startup + every 5 minutes + after each API discovery cycle)
* Significant CPU cost from parsing a 50MB+ JSON document repeatedly
* Browser memory pressure from holding the full spec in memory
* On metered or slow connections, this creates noticeable latency

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Deploy a cluster with many CRDs installed (1000+ API resources -- `oc api-resources | wc -l` > 1000+)
2. Open browser DevTools > Network tab
3. Load the OpenShift Console
4. Observe the request to `api/kubernetes/openapi/v2` -- note the response size (\~50MB+)
5. Wait 5 minutes or trigger an API discovery cycle
6. Observe the same full-size request is issued again with no conditional headers

## Proposed Fix

Several options, in order of effort:

1. **Add conditional request headers** (lowest effort): Capture the `ETag` or `Last-Modified` header from the response and include `If-None-Match` / `If-Modified-Since` on subsequent requests. The server returns `304 Not Modified` when the spec hasn't changed, avoiding the full transfer.
2. **Switch to OpenAPI v3** (medium effort): The Kubernetes OpenAPI v3 endpoint (`/openapi/v3`) supports per-group fetching (e.g., `/openapi/v3/apis/apps/v1`), allowing th

summary: Cluster Settings page downloads ~600MB+ of ClusterServiceVersions on every visit
description:
## Description

Navigating to **Administration > Cluster Settings** triggers a LIST request to `/apis/operators.coreos.com/v1alpha1/clusterserviceversions` that downloads **all ClusterServiceVersion (CSV) objects across all namespaces**. On clusters with many installed operators, this payload reaches **600MB+** and is **re-downloaded every time** the user navigates to the page.

## Root Cause

The `ClusterNotUpgradeableAlert` component (`frontend/public/components/cluster-settings/cluster-settings.tsx:745-757`) opens an unscoped watch on all CSVs cluster-wide:

```typescript
const ClusterServiceVersionResource: WatchK8sResource = {
  isList: true,
  kind: referenceForModel(ClusterServiceVersionModel),
};
const [clusterServiceVersions] = useK8sWatchResource<ClusterServiceVersionKind[]>(
  ClusterServiceVersionResource,
);
```

This watch has **no namespace scoping or field/label selector**. The component's only purpose is to check whether any CSVs have an `Upgradeable=False` condition -- but it downloads the **full content** of every CSV to do so.

CSV objects are notoriously large (100KB+ each) because they embed ALM examples, full CRD descriptions, and base64-encoded icon data. OLM copies CSVs to every namespace where an operator is available, so a cluster with many operators across 350+ namespaces easily accumulates thousands of CSV objects.

Two factors make this worse:

1. **Watch teardown on navigation**: The watch is created inside a React component. Navigating away from the page unmounts the component and stops the watch. Navigating back remounts the component, issuing a **fresh LIST call** -- re-downloading the entire 600MB+ payload. There is no cross-page cache for the watch data.
2. **Always-on watch regardless of cluster state**: The `ClusterNotUpgradeableAlert` component always mounts and starts the CSV watch, even when the cluster has no `Upgradeable=False` condition on the ClusterVersion object. In the common case (cluster is upgradeable), the 600MB download produces zero visible UI -- the alert is simply not rendered.

## Impact

* \~600MB+ network transfer on every visit to Administration > Cluster Settings on large clusters
* Significant browser memory pressure from parsing and storing the full CSV list
* Multi-second page load delay while the LIST response is in flight
* WebSocket connection held open streaming CSV updates for the lifetime of the page visit

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Deploy a cluster with many operators installed (e.g., 20+ operators across 350+ namespaces)
2. Open browser DevTools > Network tab
3. Navigate to **Administration > Cluster Settings**
4. Observe the LIST request to `/apis/operators.coreos.com/v1alpha1/clusterserviceversions` -- note the response size
5. Navigate away from the page, then navigate back
6. Observe the same large LIST request is issued again

## Proposed Fix

Two complementary mitigations:

1. **Lazy-render the alert component** (quick win): Only mount `ClusterNotUpgradeableAlert` (and

summary: Pagination component missing i18n for "Items per page" and "per page" strings
description:
**Description of problem:**

The PatternFly `Pagination` component displays hardcoded English strings that are not internationalized in the OpenShift Console. Specifically, the pagination options menu shows:

* "Items per page" as the dropdown label
* "per page" as the suffix after each option (e.g., "10 per page", "20 per page")

These strings appear in English regardless of the user's selected language because the Console is only passing the `ofWord` translation to the `titles` prop, but not `itemsPerPage` and `perPageSuffix`.

**Affected files:**

* `public/components/api-explorer.tsx:433` (API Resources list pagination)
* `public/components/api-explorer.tsx:837` (Access Review tab pagination)
* `packages/console-app/src/components/data-view/ConsoleDataView.tsx:287` (DataView component)
* `packages/helm-plugin/src/components/details-page/history/HelmReleaseHistoryTable.tsx:150` (Helm release history)

**Version-Release number of selected component:** OpenShift 4.18+ (all versions with PatternFly v6)

**How reproducible:** Always

**Steps to Reproduce:**

1. Change the Console language to a non-English language (e.g., Japanese, Spanish)
2. Navigate to any page with pagination (e.g., API Explorer, Nodes list with DataView)
3. Click on the pagination dropdown that controls items per page
4. Observe the dropdown label and option suffixes

**Actual results:**

* The dropdown shows "Items per page" in English
* Each option shows the suffix "per page" in English (e.g., "10 per page")
* Only the word "of" in the pagination display is translated (e.g., "1 - 10 of 50")

**Expected results:**

* All pagination strings should be translated according to the user's selected language
* The "Items per page" label should be translated
* The "per page" suffix should be translated

**Additional info:**

The PatternFly `Pagination` component supports full i18n through its `titles` prop with the following properties:

* `titles.ofWord` - already being used
* `titles.itemsPerPage` - **missing** (defaults to "Items per page")
* `titles.perPageSuffix` - **missing** (defaults to "per page")

**Impact:**

* Non-English speaking users see mixed language pagination controls
* Affects all pages using the Pagination component
* Degrades the localized user experience

**Related:** This was partially fixed in OCPBUGS-74137 (PR #15982) which added the `ofWord` translation, but `itemsPerPage` and `perPageSuffix` were missed.

summary: [OVE] Appliance registry does not use IRI certs during the installation
description:
**Description of problem:**

```
The Appliance registry starts on bootstrap with its own self-signed certs. Later, 
when the installation is started, the IRI registry cert is effectively created (the same that will
be used also by the MCO IRI controller) by Assited Service.
A couple of bootstrap files, namely watch-iri-tls-certs.path and reconfigure-local-registry-iri-tls.sh,
should take care of restarting the Appliance registry, to let it consume the new
cert. 
This is required because the rendezvous node will not reboot until all the other
nodes installation is completed, and will meanwhile hold the api-int. Thus it 
must use the right IRI cert to let the other nodes contact it correctly.
Right now the watch-iri-tls-certs.path and reconfigure-local-registry-iri-tls.sh
are not added in the OVE ISO ignition, so the Appliance registry will not
be able to serve properly on api-int.
```

**Version-Release number of selected component (if applicable):**

```
4.22
```

**How reproducible:**

```
Using the latest nightly build

1. Run the make agent on dev-scripts
2. 
3.

summary: New BMH fields overwritten if assisted-service built with older BMH CRD definition
description:
**Description of problem:**

```
If a scenario is produced where the version of assisted-service that is running is built against a different version of the BMH CRD that is built into BMO or siteconfig, then there is a possibility that assisted-service may unintentionally overwrite newly added fields on the BMH CR instances.

We've only experienced this in a lab environment were there is such a mismatch between BMH CRD versions in use across the different components, but the possibility of this happening is real especially given that ACM supports multiple versions of OCP.
```

**Version-Release number of selected component (if applicable):**

```
$ oc version
Client Version: 4.17.5
Kustomize Version: v5.0.4-0.20230601165947-6ce0bf390ce3
Server Version: 4.21.2
Kubernetes Version: v1.34.2
$ oc get mce
NAME                 STATUS      AGE    CURRENTVERSION   DESIREDVERSION
multiclusterengine   Available   155d   2.9.2            2.9.2
$ oc get mch -A
NAMESPACE                 NAME              STATUS    AGE    CURRENTVERSION   DESIREDVERSION
open-cluster-management   multiclusterhub   Running   155d   2.14.2           2.14.2
```

**How reproducible:**

```
Easily reproducible, but requires a version of BMO and siteconfig built against a different BMH CRD version.
```

**Steps to Reproduce:**

```
(these steps assume the system is running with the stated BMH CRD definition mismatch)
1. Create a BMH with BMC credentials secret and PreProvisioning NetworkData secret
2. Wait for BMH to inspection and become "available"
3. Deploy a cluster using a site-config ClusterInstance
4. Observe the BMH spec attributes *immediately* after site-config sets them
5. Wait for assisted-service to update the BMH
6. Observe that any new fields set by siteconfig are now gone.
7. Evidence of the new fields having previously been set can be seen in the "managed-fields" of the BMH metadata since siteconfig uses the K8S SSA approach to modifying the BMH.
```

**Actual results:**

```
Newly added BMH attributes are gone after assisted-service modifies the BMH
```

**Expected results:**

```
Newly added BMH attributes are maintained regardless of the modifications by assisted-service
```

**Additional info:**

```
‌
```

summary: Authentication operator can't validate ROPC grant flow against ADFS OIDC IdP after cluster have been updated to 4.20.z
description:
This is a clone of issue <custom data-type="smartlink" data-id="id-0">https://redhat.atlassian.net/browse/OCPBUGS-78403#icft=OCPBUGS-78403</custom>. The following is the description of the original issue:   
—  
Description of problem:

    After upgrading the cluster to 4.20.z, the Authentication operator can no longer apply an ADFS identity provider due to the below error when attempting to validate the ROPC grant flow:

```java
2026-03-12T13:51:31.012200344Z I0312 13:51:31.010896       1 default_deployment.go:43] tracked resource versions: configmaps:v4-0-config-system-cliconfig:5308276649,configmaps:v4-0-config-system-metadata:17280,configmaps:v4-0-config-system-service-ca:4824070323,configmaps:v4-0-config-system-trusted-ca-bundle:5095151518,proxy:cluster:118468005,secrets:v4-0-config-system-ocp-branding-template:4936950650,secrets:v4-0-config-system-router-certs:5300084158,secrets:v4-0-config-system-serving-cert:4824073253,secrets:v4-0-config-system-session:5277285422,secrets:v4-0-config-user-idp-0-client-secret:5308276403,secrets:v4-0-config-user-template-provider-selection:3804618822
2026-03-12T13:51:33.063459958Z I0312 13:51:33.063408       1 idp_conversions.go:389] using cached result for OIDC password grant check
2026-03-12T13:51:33.065615410Z E0312 13:51:33.065594       1 base_controller.go:277] "Unhandled Error" err="\"ConfigObserver\" controller failed to sync \"key\", err: failed to apply IDP <IDP name> config: error attempting password grant flow: OIDC token endpoint returned server error 500 (https://<IDP URL>/adfs/oauth2/token/)"
```

    The _v4-0-config-system-cliconfig_ is missing for the failing identity provider. Here in the example just the data pertinent to another identity provider is present:

summary: CEO stays degraded after temporary redfish outage during fencing-job window
description:
## Description of problem

Temporary redfish outages during a fencing-job window cause the Cluster ETCD Operator (CEO) to remain degraded indefinitely. The cluster does not retry the fencing job after redfish connectivity is restored, leaving CEO in a degraded state.

## Version-Release number

4.22

## How reproducible

Sometimes (when redfish outage occurs during fencing-job window)

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Deploy TNF cluster with redfish fencing configured
2. Trigger node replacement operation that requires fencing
3. Cause temporary redfish outage during the fencing-job execution window
4. Restore redfish connectivity
5. Observe CEO status

## Actual results

CEO remains degraded after redfish connectivity is restored. The fencing job does not retry, and the cluster stays in a degraded state indefinitely.

## Expected results

The cluster should retry the fencing job after redfish connectivity is restored. CEO should eventually recover from degraded state once the fencing operation completes successfully on retry.

## Additional info

**Discovery context**: Found during node replacement test

**Impact**: Temporary network issues or BMC outages can leave the cluster permanently degraded, requiring manual intervention to recover.

**Component**: Two Node Fencing (cluster-etcd-operator fencing job retry logic)

description:
**Description of problem:**

```
When running cluster-compare against an OCP Hub 4.20 cluster, the tool reports deviations for registry ImageDigestMirrorSet (IDMS) and ImageTagMirrorSet (ITMS) operators if the isolated image registry was configured using the latest oc-mirror v2.
```

```c
Cluster CR: config.openshift.io/v1_ImageTagMirrorSet_itms-release-0
Reference File: required/registry/itms-release.yaml
Description:
  https://docs.redhat.com/en/documentation/openshift_container_platform/4.20/html/disconnected_environments/mirroring-in-disconnected-environments
Diff Output: diff -u -N /tmp/MERGED-3778227088/config-openshift-io-v1_imagetagmirrorset_itms-release-0 /tmp/LIVE-1322373897/config-openshift-io-v1_imagetagmirrorset_itms-release-0
--- /tmp/MERGED-3778227088/config-openshift-io-v1_imagetagmirrorset_itms-release-0      2026-03-31 23:00:50.285674626 +0900
+++ /tmp/LIVE-1322373897/config-openshift-io-v1_imagetagmirrorset_itms-release-0        2026-03-31 23:00:50.285674626 +0900
@@ -2,7 +2,9 @@
 kind: ImageTagMirrorSet
 metadata:
   annotations:
-    target.workload.openshift.io/management: '{"effect": "PreferredDuringScheduling"}'
+    createdAt: Tuesday, 27-Jan-26 19:24:58 UTC
+    createdBy: oc-mirror v2
+    oc-mirror_version: 4.20.0-202512070314.p2.gf4775a2.assembly.stream.el9-f4775a2
   name: itms-release-0

   imageTagMirrors:
```

```
This was observed during a partner Engagement while performing their Hub Cluster RDS analysis. Because Nokia is legitimately using oc-mirror v2 for their disconnected setup, they are not omitting the mirror configuration; it is just functionally different from the reference baseline. I have classified these locally as non-impacting, but they currently create validation noise during cluster-compare runs.

Request for Clarification/Resolution:

- Is it accurate to keep this reference CR strictly aligned with v1?

- Should we consider adding the oc-mirror v2 injected annotations as an accepted reference pattern?

- Should this deviation only trigger when there is absolutely no mirror configuration present whatsoever?

Affected CRs:

- required/registry/idms-operator.yaml

- required/registry/idms-release.yaml

- required/registry/itms-generic.yaml

- required/registry/itms-release.yaml

summary: Every day-2 operator now requires the user to provide GCP details (project, pool id, provider id, SA) when installed on a WIF-enabled OpenShift cluster

—  
Description of problem:

```java
I noticed that we now expect user to provide GCP details (project, pool id, provider id, service account) for every operator when installed on a WIF-enabled OpenShift cluster, regardless of whether the operator interacts with GCP APIs i.e. whether they need WIF-enablement or not.

1. It seems like an unnecessary friction to require these mandatory fields for operators that don't actually interact with the GCP APIs. I don't recall this flow in previous releases

2. If an operator requires these GCP fields, we should link the appropriate documentation section within the 'Cluster in GCP Workload Identity/Federated Identity Mode' alert (as seen in the screenshot). Without this guidance, users may be unclear on the specific actions required — for example, whether they need to create a unique GCP Service Account for each operator.
```

Version-Release number of selected component (if applicable):

```java
I see this on a 4.21.0 OSD cluster installed with GCP Workload Identity Federation (WIF). Not sure if this issue is seen on 4.20 and earlier versions.
```

How reproducible:

```java
Install an OpenShift Dedicated (OSD) cluster on Google Cloud using GCP Workload Identity Federation (WIF). Since the OperatorHub flow is independent of the cluster type, it should not matter whether the cluster is a managed OSD or self-managed OCP. My recommendation is to create a self-managed OpenShift cluster on Google Cloud with WIF; the same issue should be reproducible there
```

Steps to Reproduce:

```java
1.
    2.
    3.
```

Actual results:

```java
"Install an Operator" flow of the Software Catalog/OperatorHub now expect users to provide GCP details (project, pool id, provider id, service account) for every operator when installed on a WIF-enabled OpenShift cluster, regardless of whether the operator interacts with GCP APIs. 

Attached are a few screenshots. It shows up even for the AWS Load Balancer Operator.
```

Expected results:

```java
I assume it makes sense to show these fields only for operators that interact with GCP APIs and have completed the WIF-enablement work. 

If an operator interacts with GCP APIs but has not yet been updated for WIF (e.g. Quay), requiring users to provide a Workload Identity Pool ID or Provider ID is irrelevant.
```

summary: OLM prompts for GCP Workload Identity parameters during Kiali Operator installation despite no GCP token-auth support
description:
This is a clone of issue <custom data-type="smartlink" data-id="id-0">https://redhat.atlassian.net/browse/OCPBUGS-77000#icft=OCPBUGS-77000</custom>. The following is the description of the original issue:   
—

## **Description**

During installation of the **Kiali Operator** via **GUI** on an **OpenShift 4.20** cluster running on **GCP with Workload Identity Federation (WIF)** enabled, the OpenShift Console unexpectedly prompts for the following GCP-specific inputs:

* `GCP PROJECT_NUMBER`

* `GCP POOL_ID`

* `GCP PROVIDER_ID`

* `SERVICEACCOUNT_EMAIL`

**Environment**

* OpenShift: **v4.20**

* Kiali Operator: **v2.17.3**

* Platform: **GCP with Workload Identity / Federated Identity enabled**

### **Problem Statement**

The customer is questioning **why GCP WIF–related information is required** during the Kiali Operator installation, since:

* Kiali **does not have any GCP-specific requirements**

* The Kiali team has confirmed there is **no dependency on GCP Workload Identity**

* These input fields **were not shown previously** during Kiali installation

According to **CONSOLE-4176**, the OpenShift Console is expected to detect whether an operator supports cloud-specific Workload Identity features before prompting for credentials. However, this behavior does not align with what is observed for the Kiali Operator.

### **Operator Metadata Check**

The Kiali Operator CSV does **not advertise support** for GCP token-based authentication:

 

`$ oc get csv kiali-operator.v2.17.3 -o yaml | grep -E 'features.operators.openshift.io/token-auth|operators.openshift.io/infrastructure-features'`

Output:

 

{{features.operators.openshift.io/token-auth-aws: "false"  
features.operators.openshift.io/token-auth-azure: "false"  
features.operators.openshift.io/token-auth-gcp: "false"  
operators.openshift.io/infrastructure-features: '\["disconnected", "fips"\]'}}

There is **no** `token-auth-gcp: "true"` **annotation**, yet the console still evaluates the operator as requiring GCP Workload Identity inputs.

### **Related Issues**

* **CONSOLE-4176** – Console detection logic for operator cloud identity support

* **OCPBUGS-61183** – Potentially related issue affecting operator installation behavior in cloud environments

It is currently unclear whether **OCPBUGS-61183** influences this behavior or if the console is incorrectly inferring GCP WIF requirements based on cluster-level configuration rather than operator metadata.

---

### **Questions:**

1. Why does the OpenShift Console prompt for **GCP Workload Identity parameters** when installing the Kiali Operator, despite:

* 

    * No `token-auth-gcp: "true"` annotation
    

* 

    * No GCP-specific logic in the operator itself?
    

1. Is this behavior **expected** for clusters running in **GCP WIF mode**, regardless of operator metadata?

1. **Which condition, metadata, or detection logic** in the Console/OLM is triggering these input fields to appear?

1. Should the operator bundle be updated, or

summary: CVE-2026-33186 openshift4/ose-thanos-rhel9: gRPC-Go: Authorization bypass due to improper HTTP/2 path validation [openshift-4.22]
description:
Security Tracking Issue

Do not make this issue public.

Flaw:

---

gRPC-Go: Authorization bypass due to improper HTTP/2 path validation

gRPC-Go is the Go language implementation of gRPC. Versions prior to 1.79.3 have an authorization bypass resulting from improper input validation of the HTTP/2 \`:path\` pseudo-header. The gRPC-Go server was too lenient in its routing logic, accepting requests where the \`:path\` omitted the mandatory leading slash (e.g., \`Service/Method\` instead of \`/Service/Method\`). While the server successfully routed these requests to the correct handler, authorization interceptors (including the official \`grpc/authz\` package) evaluated the raw, non-canonical path string. Consequently, "deny" rules defined using canonical paths (starting with \`/\`) failed to match the incoming request, allowing it to bypass the policy if a fallback "allow" rule was present. This affects gRPC-Go servers that use path-based authorization interceptors, such as the official RBAC implementation in \`google.golang.org/grpc/authz\` or custom interceptors relying on \`info.FullMethod\` or \`grpc.Method(ctx)\`; AND that have a security policy contains specific "deny" rules for canonical paths but allows other requests by default (a fallback "allow" rule). The vulnerability is exploitable by an attacker who can send raw HTTP/2 frames with malformed \`:path\` headers directly to the gRPC server. The fix in version 1.79.3 ensures that any request with a \`:path\` that does not start with a leading slash is immediately rejected with a \`codes.Unimplemented\` error, preventing it from reaching authorization interceptors or handlers with a non-canonical path string. While upgrading is the most secure and recommended path, users can mitigate the vulnerability using one of the following methods: Use a validating interceptor (recommended mitigation); infrastructure-level normalization; and/or policy hardening.

\~\~\~

The following link provides references to all essential vulnerability management information. If something is wrong or missing, please contact a member of PSIRT.  
[https://prodsec.pages.redhat.com/prodsec-docs/incident-response/Vulnerability_Management_Essentials/](https://prodsec.pages.redhat.com/prodsec-docs/incident-response/Vulnerability_Management_Essentials/)

Tracker accuracy feedback form: [https://docs.google.com/forms/d/e/1FAIpQLSfa6zTaEGohRdiIqGVAvWTSAL0kpO_DkkEICuIHzQHFwmKswg/viewform](https://docs.google.com/forms/d/e/1FAIpQLSfa6zTaEGohRdiIqGVAvWTSAL0kpO_DkkEICuIHzQHFwmKswg/viewform)

summary: Monitor apiserver-incluster-availability setup fails intermittently on metal-ipi-ipv6 micro upgrade due to image pull timeout
description:
## Description of problem

The apiserver-incluster-availability monitor test fails intermittently during setup on the metal-ipi-ipv6 micro upgrade job. The failure occurs because the service-network-monitor deployment cannot roll out due to image pull failures. This is part of a broader upgrade failure pattern where the CVO fails to acknowledge the upgrade request, causing \~10 co-failing tests per run.

Regression IDs: 37405, 37406  
Regression opened: 2026-03-31

Test names:

```
[Monitor:apiserver-incluster-availability][Jira:"kube-apiserver"] monitor test apiserver-incluster-availability setup
```

```
[Monitor:apiserver-incluster-availability][Jira:"kube-apiserver"] monitor test apiserver-incluster-availability collection
```

Test IDs:  
\- openshift-tests-upgrade:2c64520629f771b89a5ef3a0a42ed12d (setup)  
\- openshift-tests-upgrade:1193835102fae3bc06e09a40f30de6b9 (collection)

## Version-Release number

4.22

## How reproducible

Sometimes (3 failures in \~19 runs, flaky pattern)

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Observe periodic job: periodic-ci-openshift-release-main-nightly-4.22-e2e-metal-ipi-upgrade-ovn-ipv6
2. Monitor test results for apiserver-incluster-availability setup/collection phases

## Actual results

Test fails with:

```
failed during setup
error creating service network deployment: deployment service-network-monitor didn't roll out: timed out waiting for the condition
```

The collection phase reports image pull failures for service-network-monitor pods. The upgrade itself fails - CVO does not acknowledge the upgrade request. \~10 tests co-fail in each failing run with consistent co-failure patterns (upgrade lifecycle tests, DNS, pod-network-availability).

## Expected results

The service-network-monitor deployment should roll out successfully and the micro upgrade should complete.

## Additional info

Affected variants: amd64, default, ipi, ovn, metal, ha, micro upgrade (IPv6)  
Pass sequence: FSSSSSSFSSSFSSSSSSS (flaky - 3 failures scattered in 19 runs)  
Failure consistency: 100% - all failures show the identical error message  
Global pass rate: 99.04% overall across all variants

Co-failing tests in each run (consistent across all failures):

* apiserver-incluster-availability collection (image pull failures)
* pod-network-availability collection (image pull failures)
* Cluster completes upgrade (operator failed to acknowledge)
* CVO acknowledges upgrade (timed out)
* Cluster upgradeable after finishing upgrade
* DNS availability during upgrade
* legacy-cvo-invariants

Sippy Test Details:

* Regression 37405 (setup): [Test Details](https://sippy-auth.dptools.openshift.org/sippy-ng/component_readiness/test_details?Architecture=amd64&FeatureSet=default&Installer=ipi&LayeredProduct=none&Network=ovn&Platform=metal&Suite=unknown&Topology=ha&Upgrade=micro&capability=Other&component=kube-apiserver&environment=Architecture%3Aamd64+FeatureSet%3Adefault+Installer%3Aipi+LayeredProduct%3Anone+Network%3Aovn+Platform%3Ametal+Suite%3Aunk

summary: openshift-tests disruption poller pods do not terminate on eviction, blocking MCO node drain during upgrades
description:
**Description of problem:**

During 4.22 to 5.0 upgrades, the MCO fails to drain control plane nodes because openshift-tests disruption poller pods do not terminate after eviction, blocking the drain for over 1-2 hours and causing the upgrade to time out. This has been observed in two consecutive runs of the same periodic job.

Detailed analysis of Job 2 artifacts reveals that the disruption poller container **never actually started** — the pod was stuck in `ContainerCreating` from the moment it was created, nearly an hour before the drain even began. The kubelet was then unable to delete the pod (no running container to kill), and the pod blocked the MCO drain indefinitely.

**Version-Release number of selected component (if applicable):**

openshift-tests 5.0.0-202603301628.p2.g31582ab.assembly.stream.el9    
Upgrade: 4.22 -> 5.0

**How reproducible:**

Reproduced in 2 out of 2 observed runs of the same periodic job (different nightlies).

**Steps to Reproduce:**

1. Run upgrade job periodic-ci-openshift-release-main-ci-5.0-upgrade-from-stable-4.22-e2e-aws-ovn-upgrade
2. MCO begins draining control plane nodes for master MachineConfigPool update
3. Disruption poller pod on the drained node does not terminate after eviction

**Actual results:**

The disruption poller pod (host-network-to-host-network-disruption-poller) does not terminate after eviction. The MCO drain controller retries repeatedly, always hitting:

```
node <control-plane-node>: Drain failed. Waiting 1 minute then retrying.  
Error message from drain: error when waiting for pod "host-network-to-host-network-disruption-poller-<id>"  
in namespace "e2e-pod-network-disruption-test-<id>" to terminate: context deadline exceeded  
```

Cascading failures:

* MCO declares FailedToDrain after 1 hour -> master MachineConfigPool degraded (still at 4.22)
* Evicted control plane pods (openshift-apiserver, oauth-apiserver, oauth-openshift) cannot reschedule due to anti-affinity on the cordoned node -> operators degraded
* Upgrade timed out with authentication, openshift-apiserver, and machine-config operators degraded

**Expected results:**

The disruption poller pod should terminate promptly when evicted during a node drain, allowing the MCO to complete the drain and the upgrade to proceed.

**Additional info:**

Observed in two consecutive job runs:

**Job 1:**

* URL: [https://prow.ci.openshift.org/view/gs/test-platform-results/logs/periodic-ci-openshift-release-main-ci-5.0-upgrade-from-stable-4.22-e2e-aws-ovn-upgrade/2038812866240319488](https://prow.ci.openshift.org/view/gs/test-platform-results/logs/periodic-ci-openshift-release-main-ci-5.0-upgrade-from-stable-4.22-e2e-aws-ovn-upgrade/2038812866240319488)
* Build ID: 2038812866240319488
* Upgrade: 4.22.0-0.nightly-2026-03-29-173136 -> 5.0.0-0.nightly-2026-03-31-025233
* Stuck node: ip-10-0-42-237 (control-plane)
* Blocking pod: host-network-to-host-network-disruption-poller-55647bc4bf-5f44j in namespace e2e-pod-network-disruption-test-b

summary: HCP:  Hardcoded MTU on hosted cluster nodes in AWS is incompatible with ISO (classified) regions
description:
This is a clone of issue <custom data-type="smartlink" data-id="id-0">https://redhat.atlassian.net/browse/OCPBUGS-77078#icft=OCPBUGS-77078</custom>. The following is the description of the original issue:   
—  
Description of problem:

```java
Some non-commercial AWS regions do not support full 9001 MTU, but hypershift and the network operator assume that 9001 should be used on hosted cluster nodes in AWS. There is currently no way to override this value for HostedCluster. It can be mitigated via activities on the worker nodes (Day 2) via migrations, but this is not desirable nor maintainable for automating large numbers of clusters.

Customers require a way to override the hardcoded MTU of 9001 during hosted cluster deployments in non-commercial AWS regions.  

References to the hardcoded MTU in the cluster network operator for hosted clusters in AWS can be found at:

https://github.com/openshift/cluster-network-operator/blob/9d540eb7929f83879d3dafbcd1ae6cb1feca36b3/pkg/controller/operconfig/mtu_probe.go#L27
https://github.com/openshift/cluster-network-operator/blob/9d540eb7929f83879d3dafbcd1ae6cb1feca36b3/pkg/controller/operconfig/mtu_probe.go#L38-L42
```

summary: Delete namespace after ClusterUserDefinedNetwork was configured doesn't work
description:
## Description of problem:

Namespace deletion is stuck in 'Terminating' if ClusterUserDefinedNetwork has been configured.

Maybe the correct step to delete a namespace is to remove it from the \`namespaceSelector\` field of ClusterUserDefinedNetwork before deleting it, but there is no such description in the OpenShift manuals.

[https://docs.redhat.com/en/documentation/openshift_container_platform/4.20/html/multiple_networks/primary-networks#nw-cudn-cr_user-defined-networks](https://docs.redhat.com/en/documentation/openshift_container_platform/4.20/html/multiple_networks/primary-networks#nw-cudn-cr_user-defined-networks) describes how to create namespace with ClusterUserDefinedNetwork, but there is no touch about how to delete.

If it's a designed behavior, it should be noted in the manuals.

## Version-Release number of selected component (if applicable):

OpenShift v4.20.6

## How reproducible:

Always

## Steps to Reproduce:

Step1. Create the following script.

```
#!/bin/bash

oc get clusterversion

for namespace in "test-cudn-l2-1" "test-cudn-l2-2" "test-cudn-l3-1" "test-cudn-l3-2" 
do
  cat << _EOF_ | oc apply -f -
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: $namespace
  labels:
    k8s.ovn.org/primary-user-defined-network: "" 
_EOF_
done

sleep 5

cat << _EOF_ | oc apply -f -
apiVersion: k8s.ovn.org/v1
kind: ClusterUserDefinedNetwork
metadata:
  name: test-cudn-l2
spec:
  namespaceSelector:
    matchExpressions:
    - key: kubernetes.io/metadata.name
      operator: In
      values: ["test-cudn-l2-1", "test-cudn-l2-2"]
  network:
    topology: Layer2
    layer2:
      role: Primary
      subnets:
        - "10.100.0.0/16" 
---
apiVersion: k8s.ovn.org/v1
kind: ClusterUserDefinedNetwork
metadata:
  name: test-cudn-l3
spec:
  namespaceSelector:
    matchExpressions:
    - key: kubernetes.io/metadata.name
      operator: In
      values: ["test-cudn-l3-1", "test-cudn-l3-2"]
  network:
    topology: Layer3
    layer3:
      role: Primary
      subnets:
      - cidr: 10.101.0.0/16
        hostSubnet: 24
_EOF_

sleep 5

for namespace in "test-cudn-l2-1" "test-cudn-l2-2" "test-cudn-l3-1" "test-cudn-l3-2" 
do
  cat << _EOF_ | oc apply -f -
---
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  labels:
    app: httpserver
  name: httpserver
  namespace: $namespace
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: httpserver
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: httpserver
    spec:
      affinity:
        podAntiAffinity:
          preferredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
          - podAffinityTerm:
              labelSelector:
                matchExpressions:
                - key: app
                  operator: In
                  values:
                  - "httpserver" 
              topologyKey: kubernetes.io/hostname
            weight: 100
      containers:
      - image: image-registry.openshift-image-regi

summary: [IBMCloud] MAPI Machine
description:
This is a clone of issue <custom data-type="smartlink" data-id="id-0">https://redhat.atlassian.net/browse/OCPBUGS-81317#icft=OCPBUGS-81317</custom>. The following is the description of the original issue:   
—  
This is a clone of issue <custom data-type="smartlink" data-id="id-1">https://redhat.atlassian.net/browse/OCPBUGS-77445#icft=OCPBUGS-77445</custom>. The following is the description of the original issue:   
—  
Description of problem:

```java
IBM Cloud Infrastructure Service added the ability to create VPC Security Group Rules using additional protocols, one included is 'any'. Older IBM Cloud vpc-go-sdk versions are unable to process SG's and SG Rules and cause an error. The IBM Cloud MAPI component is using an old SDK, and is unable to create new Machines as a result.
```

Version-Release number of selected component (if applicable):

```java
4.19.21
```

How reproducible:

```java
100%
```

Steps to Reproduce:

```java
1. Create a new IPI deployed OCP cluster on IBM Cloud
    2. Using the VPC API's, via curl, create a new SecurityGroup Rule using 'any' protocol in the cluster's VPC
    3. Attempt to create a new MachineSet
```

Actual results:

```java
Machine gets stuck Provisioning
machine.machine.openshift.io/ocp-jenkins-30-48nrh-worker-us-east-failset-ps8gt   Provisioning                                    61s


MAPI logs:

E0107 16:59:33.554905       1 actuator.go:66] ocp-jenkins-30-48nrh-worker-us-east-failset-ps8gt error: ocp-jenkins-30-48nrh-worker-us-east-failset-ps8gt: reconciler failed to Create machine: failed to create instance via ibm vpc client: could not retrieve security group ids of names: map[ocp-jenkins-30-48nrh-sg-cluster-wide: ocp-jenkins-30-48nrh-sg-openshift-net:]
```

Expected results:

```java
New Machine is Provisioned and joins the cluster
```

Additional info:

```java
Added an 'any' SG Rule to the existing cluster's default VPC SG
% ibmcloud is sg-rules r014-4da3252a-b066-45f1-a8d1-e424971fea1b ocp-jenkins-30-48nrh-vpc -q                  ID                                          Direction   IP version   Remote                        Protocol       Local       Name    r014-ec486e2b-ad12-483e-8ba4-0ec2e151992f   outbound    ipv4         0.0.0.0/0                     icmp_tcp_udp   0.0.0.0/0   outbound-icmp-tcp-udp    r014-6823adb1-a49e-4d7a-aaa4-2e1205a77e94   inbound     ipv4         polio-stoke-lurex-surcharge   icmp_tcp_udp   0.0.0.0/0   inbound-icmp-tcp-udp-from-this-security-group    r014-25f3f7cb-f117-4d3d-a006-85ebf1395493   outbound    ipv4         0.0.0.0/0                     any            0.0.0.0/0   shamrock-reformer-crawfish-trimester



The latest vpc-go-sdk available, should contain a fix to prevent this issue
https://github.com/IBM/vpc-go-sdk/releases/tag/v0.78.1

It is possible a long term fix is provided by IBM Cloud Infrastructure team soon, but updating to the latest vpc-go-sdk is still desirable.
```

summary: [4.19] linuxptp-daemon is not prepared to run kernel with mr 5470
description:
**Description of problem:**

```
Ice driver is introducing breaking API changes in MR 5470. 
To prepare for the change and keep the ptp-operator user intrface 
unaffected, a series of changes has been made. These changes must
be regressed on 4.22 with the old and the new kernel, and then
backported to 4.21, 4.20 and 4.19
The old kernel - any RHEL9.6 kernel
The new (test) kernel: https://brewweb.engineering.redhat.com/brew/taskinfo?taskID=70425027
The regression should be done on WPC and GNR-D
```

summary: Flaky single/own namespace tests after PR #524 – failure when namespace not deleted
description:
**Description:**  
After the changes introduced in [PR #524](https://github.com/openshift/operator-framework-operator-controller/pull/524/files), the single/own namespace tests may start to **flake** because we are failing when the namespace or ClusterExtension is not deleted in time.

We also removed the **SERIAL** marker, so these tests now run in parallel.  
This means that timing issues or delays in Kubernetes cleanup should not cause the test to fail.

**Example failure:**  
See: \[[https://prow.ci.openshift.org/view/gs/test-platform-results/logs/periodic-ci-openshift-](https://prow.ci.openshift.org/view/gs/test-platform-results/logs/periodic-ci-openshift-)\[…\]ly-4.21-e2e-azure-ovn-runc-techpreview/1980106371709800448|[https://prow.ci.openshift.org/view/gs/test-platform-results/logs/periodic-ci-openshift-release-master-nightly-4.21-e2e-azure-ovn-runc-techpreview/1980106371709800448](https://prow.ci.openshift.org/view/gs/test-platform-results/logs/periodic-ci-openshift-release-master-nightly-4.21-e2e-azure-ovn-runc-techpreview/1980106371709800448)\]  
 

```java
fail [/build/openshift/tests-extension/pkg/helpers/cluster_extension.go:185]: Timed out after 60.039s. Cleanup ClusterExtension install-webhook-bothns-ownns-ce-tz9c failed to delete Expected <bool>: false to be true
```

{{}}

**Expected behaviour:**  
We should not fail the test if cleanup resources (like namespaces or ClusterExtensions) take longer to be deleted.  
Kubernetes can take extra time to remove resources due to dependency cleanup, and that’s normal.

**Proposed fix:**

* Ensure test resources (namespaces, CEs, etc.) use **unique names** per scenario.

* Ensure we **call Kubernetes delete** for all resources properly.

* If deletion fails or takes too long, **log a warning** instead of failing the test — to avoid sending a false failure signal to **Sippy or block other teams**

**Impact:**  
Unnecessary flakes and false failure signals on CI due to normal Kubernetes cleanup behaviour, which may block other teams and send bad signal to Sippy

summary: [plugin-backend] Honor Centralized TLS Configuration for PQC-Safety
description:
This is a clone of issue <custom data-type="smartlink" data-id="id-0">https://redhat.atlassian.net/browse/OCPBUGS-62276#icft=OCPBUGS-62276</custom>. The following is the description of the original issue:   
—  
Description of problem:

```java
The [plugin-backend] in the [monitoring-plugin-] pod in the [openshift-monitoring] component is using a local, hardcoded TLS configuration. This component's TLS Security Profile is expected to be managed by the API Server config custom resource. If you feel you should be using the Kubelet or Ingress configuration as the source-of-truth for TLS configuration, please leave a comment justifying your decision and rationale.

Hardcoding TLS configuration creates a security vulnerability because it does not adhere to our evolving, centrally-managed security policy for Post-Quantum Cryptography (PQC) readiness.

This ticket requires refactoring the component to dynamically inherit its TLS settings from the designated global configuration source, rather than managing them locally.

Business & Security Impact
This is a critical, long-term security initiative. Hardcoded configurations prevent agile responses to new threats and lead to policy drift. By not using the central policy, we are exposed to "store-now-decrypt-later" attacks, where adversaries capture today's data to decrypt with future quantum computers.
Centralizing this configuration is the only scalable way to ensure every service in our ecosystem remains secure against emerging cryptographic threats.An audit has confirmed this component is not using the central TLS policy.
```

Version-Release number of selected component (if applicable):

```java
4.20
```

How reproducible:

```java
100%
A scan of its endpoint shows a configuration that is out-of-sync with the current global standard.
Evidence Link:
```

[https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1nb7o0g3Ahn9GMTV-XvqraZY4Q7-1ZSYYlXH2EUpU4Dk/edit?gid=410280687#gid=410280687](https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1nb7o0g3Ahn9GMTV-XvqraZY4Q7-1ZSYYlXH2EUpU4Dk/edit?gid=410280687#gid=410280687) 

 

Steps to Reproduce:

```java
1. Deploy OCP
    2. Configure to "Modern Profile" the Control Plane, kubelet and Ingress
    3. Scan for non-TLS 1.3 reply from component (there should be none)
```

Actual results:

```java
TLS 1.2 responded
```

Expected results:

```java
TLS 1.3 only. If using a custom TLS 1.3 profile, the custom profile should be honored.
```

Additional info:

```java
The service/component must be refactored to dynamically pull and apply TLS policy from the official, centrally-managed sources, like the APIServer configuration, Kubelet configuration, or Ingress controller configuration (e.g., hardcoded protocols or cipher lists).

Hardcoding values like "TLS 1.3" is not an acceptable fix. The component must remain compliant as the central policy is updated over time without requiring a code change.

Central Configuration Source:
Documentation: https://docs.redhat.com/en/documentatio

summary: Catalog Tests has not been so longer executed when the indexes files are changed
description: After we changed OLMv1 to use a Helm chart, we also moved the location of the manifests that define the catalog indexes. However, we forgot to update the pre-merge CI, which is only triggered when those manifests are modified.

Central Configuration Source:
Documentation: https://docs.redhat.com/en/documentation/openshift_container_platform/4.19/html/security_and_compliance/tls-security-profiles#tls-profiles-kubernetes-configuring_tls-security-profiles 

Acceptance Criteria

summary: [kube-rbac-proxy - node-ca] Honor Centralized TLS Configuration for PQC-Safety
description:
Description of problem:

```java
The [kube-rbac-proxy] in the [node-ca] pod in the [openshift-image-registry] component is using a local, hardcoded TLS configuration. This component's TLS Security Profile is expected to be managed by the API Server config custom resource. If you feel you should be using the Kubelet or Ingress configuration as the source-of-truth for TLS configuration, please leave a comment justifying your decision and rationale.

Hardcoding TLS configuration creates a security vulnerability because it does not adhere to our evolving, centrally-managed security policy for Post-Quantum Cryptography (PQC) readiness.

This ticket requires refactoring the component to dynamically inherit its TLS settings from the designated global configuration source, rather than managing them locally.

Business & Security Impact
This is a critical, long-term security initiative. Hardcoded configurations prevent agile responses to new threats and lead to policy drift. By not using the central policy, we are exposed to "store-now-decrypt-later" attacks, where adversaries capture today's data to decrypt with future quantum computers.
Centralizing this configuration is the only scalable way to ensure every service in our ecosystem remains secure against emerging cryptographic threats.An audit has confirmed this component is not using the central TLS policy.
```

Version-Release number of selected component (if applicable):

```java
4.20
```

How reproducible:

```java
100%
A scan of its endpoint shows a configuration that is out-of-sync with the current global standard.
Evidence Link:
```

[https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1nb7o0g3Ahn9GMTV-XvqraZY4Q7-1ZSYYlXH2EUpU4Dk/edit?gid=410280687#gid=410280687](https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1nb7o0g3Ahn9GMTV-XvqraZY4Q7-1ZSYYlXH2EUpU4Dk/edit?gid=410280687#gid=410280687) 

 

Steps to Reproduce:

```java
1. Deploy OCP
    2. Configure to "Modern Profile" the Control Plane, kubelet and Ingress
    3. Scan for non-TLS 1.3 reply from component (there should be none)
```

Actual results:

```java
TLS 1.2 responded
```

Expected results:

```java
TLS 1.3 only. If using a custom TLS 1.3 profile, the custom profile should be honored.
```

Additional info:

```java
The service/component must be refactored to dynamically pull and apply TLS policy from the official, centrally-managed sources, like the APIServer configuration, Kubelet configuration, or Ingress controller configuration (e.g., hardcoded protocols or cipher lists).

Hardcoding values like "TLS 1.3" is not an acceptable fix. The component must remain compliant as the central policy is updated over time without requiring a code change.

Central Configuration Source:
Documentation: https://docs.redhat.com/en/documentation/openshift_container_platform/4.19/html/security_and_compliance/tls-security-profiles#tls-profiles-kubernetes-configuring_tls-security-profiles

summary: Apply and revert alert Allowance for InstallPlanStepAppliedWithWarnings
description:
Description of problem:

```java
During the k8s 1.34 rebase work, we noticed the InstallPlanStepAppliedWithWarnings alert started firing.

A warning is being generated by the API server for the VirtualServices CRD. This is due to a recent change in Kubernetes 1.34 that now flags unknown string formats.

This warning is currently blocking rebase efforts. To temporarily resolve this and unblock the rebase, we'll add an allowed alert for InstallPlanStepAppliedWithWarnings. This allowance is a temporary measure and should be removed once the root cause has been addressed.

summary: Unable to Move to 4.20 Index Due to Missing Installable Content with Max OCP Version in 4.20 Catalog
description:
We need to **bump the 4.20 catalogue images** to support **OLMv1**.  
To do that, the pull request must **pass CI**: [https://github.com/openshift/operator-framework-operator-controller/pull/393](https://github.com/openshift/operator-framework-operator-controller/pull/393)

For us to make the test pass, we need to have:

* An **operator version** that exists in **both 4.19 and 4.20 catalogs**
* It must be installed successfully with **OLMv1**
* It must have **olm.maxOpenShiftVersion < 4.20**

This test checks that **cluster upgrades are blocked** when such an operator is installed.

❗ **Problem:** We **don’t have any operator** in the 4.20 catalog that meets **all** these conditions.  
That’s why can not change the test to use ANY, and we can’t move forward with the 4.20 bump. [https://redhat.atlassian.net/browse/OCPBUGS-60154?focusedId=27733043&page=com.atlassian.jira.plugin.system.issuetabpanels:comment-tabpanel#comment-27733043](https://redhat.atlassian.net/browse/OCPBUGS-60154?focusedId=27733043&page=com.atlassian.jira.plugin.system.issuetabpanels:comment-tabpanel#comment-27733043)

**Impact:** Blocks CI and prevents OLMv1 upgrade Catalog Sources indexes images from uses 4.20 tags. Consequently, it blocks the release

**Test Details:**

* **File:** [https://github.com/openshift/origin/blob/main/test/extended/olm/olmv1.go#L107-L147](https://github.com/openshift/origin/blob/main/test/extended/olm/olmv1.go#L107-L147)

summary: prometheus-operator needs to sync ServiceMonitor secrets+configmaps
description:
Description of problem:

```java
During the run of the etcd certificate rotation tests that is rotating metrics client/signer certs and bundle we see the metrics taking a long time (15-20m) to recover etcd (or sometimes never).

This is similar to https://github.com/prometheus-operator/prometheus-operator/issues/6018

I'm setting this to critical, since this auto rotation is a feature we're delivering in 4.17 and this is causing etcd metrics to go dark for sometimes 15-20 minutes.

source slack convo:
https://redhat-internal.slack.com/archives/C0VMT03S5/p1719834061510629

reference run:
https://prow.ci.openshift.org/view/gs/test-platform-results/pr-logs/pull/openshift_clus[…]rator-master-e2e-aws-etcd-certrotation/1808773983189864448

search:
https://search.dptools.openshift.org/?search=.*&maxAge=48h&context=1&type=junit&name=.*aws-etcd-certrotation.*&excludeName=&maxMatches=5&maxBytes=20971520&groupBy=job
```

4.17, but surely happened earlier
```

```java
always
```

Steps to Reproduce:

```java
1. you can run the origin test suite with "openshift-test run "openshift/etcd/certrotation" 
    2. ???
    3. see that the metric signer rotation test either fails or one of the invariants that test the alerts/metrics
```

Actual results:

```java
metric signer test takes a long time to execute, or the test suite fails entirely because the metrics don't recover during the runtime
```

Expected results:

```java
I would expect both ca bundle and new client cert to be immediately picked up as they change
```

work-around would be to bump the respective etcd service monitors, so the secret and bundles are reloaded
```

summary: `KubeCPUOvercommit` Alert Not Triggered Despite Node CPU is Overcommitment
description:
One of our customers observed this issue. In order to reproduce, In my test cluster, I intentionally increased the overall CPU limits to **over 200%** and monitored the cluster for more than 2 days. However, I did not see the `KubeCPUOvercommit` alert, which ideally should trigger after 10 minutes of overcommitment. 

> Allocated resources:  
>   (Total limits may be over 100 percent, i.e., overcommitted.)  
>   Resource                       Requests      Limits  
>   --------                          --------            ------  
>   cpu                                2654m (75%)        8450m (241%)  
>   memory                         5995Mi (87%)  12264Mi (179%)  
>   ephemeral-storage         0 (0%)        0 (0%)  
>   hugepages-1Gi             0 (0%)        0 (0%)  
>   hugepages-2Mi             0 (0%)        0 (0%)

 

**OCP console --> Observe --> alerting --> alert rule and select for the \`KubeCPUOvercommit\` alert.**

**Expression:**

sum by (cluster) (namespace_cpu:kube_pod_container_resource_requests:sum{job="kube-state-metrics"}) - (sum by (cluster) (kube_node_status_allocatable{job="kube-state-metrics",resource="cpu"}) - max by (cluster) (kube_node_status_allocatable{job="kube-state-metrics",resource="cpu"})) > 0 and (sum by (cluster) (kube_node_status_allocatable{job="kube-state-metrics",resource="cpu"}) - max by (cluster) (kube_node_status_allocatable{job="kube-state-metrics",resource="cpu"})) > 0
